It is likely Robert Raikes, the founder of the modern Sunday-school movement, never thought of telling stories to "the terrible bad boys," the waifs from the alleys of Gloucester, whom in 1780 he gathered into his first Sunday-school in that city. Nor did the four teachers whom he hired at one shilling each week seem to dream of the children's thirst for stories. They were perfectly content to teach these "young savages" to repeat simple prayers, the Church of England catechism, Bible questions and answers, and to sing Doctor Watts' hymns; and occasionally Robert Raikes gave them a crack on the head with his walking-stick in order to impress some knotty point of instruction. But the recent study of child-nature, and the influence of modern psychology and pedagogy on the church, have clearly marked out a better way. In the religious training of children, no less than in their general education, story-telling is seen to be the easiest, simplest, and most effective means of impressing upon a new generation the lessons that have been learned by those who have gone before.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 直到最近，主日学校才开始重视故事教学的价值。现代主日学运动的创始人罗伯特·雷克斯很可能从未想过给“那些可怕的坏孩子”——1780年他在格洛斯特巷子里收进第一所主日学校的流浪儿——讲故事。他每周花一先令雇来的四位老师，似乎也从未想到孩子们对故事的渴望。他们满足于教这些“小野人”背诵简单的祈祷文、英国国教教义问答、圣经问答，并唱瓦茨博士的赞美诗；雷克斯偶尔还会用手杖敲他们的头，以强化某个难懂的知识点。但近年来对儿童天性的研究，以及现代心理学和教育学对教会的影响，已明确指明了一条更好的道路。在儿童的宗教教育中，讲故事与普通教育一样，被视为向新一代传递前人经验的最简单、最有效的方式。

paragraphs.text: Dr. H. E. Tralle, in "Teacher-Training Essentials," says: "All in all, the story method is probably the most valuable of all methods of teaching in the Sunday-school."
paragraphs.translation_zh: H.E.特拉勒博士在《教师培训要点》中说：“总而言之，故事法可能是主日学校所有教学方法中最有价值的一种。”

paragraphs.text: "Of all the things that a teacher should know how to do," says President G. Stanley Hall, "the most important, without exception, is to be able to tell a good story."
paragraphs.translation_zh: G.斯坦利·霍尔校长说：“在教师应掌握的所有技能中，最重要的无疑是能讲一个好故事。”

paragraphs.text: Every Sunday-school teacher who would be successful in teaching modern boys and girls must give attention to this golden method of instruction, and should, as early as possible, learn this "the easiest of all the creative arts," the delightful art of story-telling.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每一位希望成功教育现代儿童的主日学教师，都必须重视这种黄金教学法，并尽早学会这门“所有创造性艺术中最容易的”——令人愉悦的讲故事艺术。

paragraphs.text: But oral story-telling has value in the Sunday-school outside the class instruction. The story form is the best expression of children's worship, and should be employed in what is called "the opening and closing exercises." A short story is soon told, but its influence abides long after "the address" is forgotten. Let the story-tellers and their stories be selected with care, and many a dull opening or closing exercise will be enlivened and enriched. Bible stories, Christmas and Thanksgiving stories, missionary stories, altruistic stories, stories of hymns, stories of noble acts of children recorded in our daily papers, all are serviceable. Many of the stories in this volume have been told again and again in the opening and closing exercises of Sunday-schools with good results.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然而，口头讲故事在主日学校的价值不仅限于课堂教学。故事形式是儿童崇拜的最佳表达方式，应运用于所谓的“开场和结束仪式”中。一个简短的故事很快就能讲完，但其影响远在“训话”被遗忘后仍会持续。精心挑选讲故事的人和故事，许多枯燥的开场或结束仪式将变得生动而丰富。圣经故事、圣诞节和感恩节故事、传教故事、利他故事、赞美诗故事、日报上记载的儿童高尚行为故事——所有这些都很有用。本书中的许多故事已在主日学校的开场和结束仪式中反复讲述，效果良好。

paragraphs.text: Dr. Richard Morse Hodge well says: "If you do not tell stories at the services of a Sunday-school, please reflect that some one else may be telling stories to the same children at some other time and place; may be doing more to promote their worship of God than what you may be doing for them by a less intelligent method of conducting the Sunday-school services."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 理查德·莫尔斯·霍奇博士说得好：“如果你在主日学校的仪式上不讲故事，请反思一下：也许别人正在其他时间、其他地点给同样的孩子讲故事；他们可能比你用不那么明智的方式主持主日学校仪式，更能促进孩子们对上帝的崇拜。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE YOUNG CUPBEARER

paragraphs.text: He was not petted and spoiled like many other princes. Although his father was a king, Cyrus was brought up like the son of a common man.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他并未像其他王子那样被娇惯纵容。尽管父亲是国王，居鲁士却像平民之子般被抚养长大。

paragraphs.text: He knew how to work with his hands. He ate only the plainest food. He slept on a hard bed. He learned to endure hunger and cold.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他懂得亲手劳作，只吃最简朴的食物，睡硬板床，学会了忍受饥寒。

paragraphs.text: When Cyrus was twelve years old he went with his mother to Media to visit his grandfather. His grandfather, whose name was Astyages, was king of Media, and very rich and powerful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 居鲁士十二岁时随母亲前往米底探望外祖父。外祖父阿斯提阿格斯是米底国王，极其富有且权势滔天。

paragraphs.text: Cyrus was so tall and strong and handsome that his grandfather was very proud of him. He wished the lad to stay with him in Media. He therefore gave him many beautiful gifts and everything that could please a prince. One day King Astyages planned to make a great feast for the lad. The tables were to be laden with all kinds of food. There was to be music and dancing; and Cyrus was to invite as many guests as he chose. The hour for the feast came. Everything was ready. The servants were there, dressed in fine uniforms. The musicians and dancers were in their places. But no guests came.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 居鲁士高大强壮、相貌英俊，外祖父对他引以为傲，希望他留在米底。于是赠予他许多精美礼物和一切能取悦王子的东西。一日，阿斯提阿格斯国王计划为外孙举办盛宴，桌上摆满珍馐，安排乐舞，让居鲁士随意邀请宾客。宴席时辰已到，万事俱备，侍从身着华服，乐师舞者各就各位，却无一位宾客到来。

paragraphs.text: "How is this, my dear boy?" asked the king. "The feast is ready, but no one has come to partake of it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “这是怎么回事，亲爱的孩子？”国王问道，“宴席已备好，却无人赴宴。”

paragraphs.text: "That is because I have not invited any one," said Cyrus." In Persia we do not have such feasts. If any one is hungry, he eats some bread and meat, with perhaps a few cresses, and that is the end of it. We never go to all this trouble and expense of making a fine dinner in order that our friends may eat what is not good for them."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “因为我谁也没邀请，”居鲁士说，“在波斯，我们从不这样设宴。若有人饥饿，吃些面包、肉食，或许再加点水芹，便足矣。我们从不费这般周折与开销大摆筵席，让朋友吃些对他们无益的东西。”

paragraphs.text: "Well," said he, "all these rich foods that were prepared for the feast are yours. What will you do with them?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好吧，”国王说，“这些为宴席准备的所有珍馐都归你了。你打算怎么处理？”

paragraphs.text: So he gave one portion to the king's officer who had taught him to ride. Another portion he gave to an old servant who waited upon his grandfather. And the rest he divided among the young women who took care of his mother.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他将一份赐予教他骑马的国王近臣，另一份赠予侍奉外祖父的老仆，余下的分给了照料母亲的那些年轻女子。

paragraphs.text: The king's cupbearer, Sarcas, was very much offended because he was not given a share of the feast. The king also wondered why this man, who was his favorite, should be so slighted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王的斟酒人萨卡斯因未分到宴席而大为恼怒。国王也纳闷，为何自己最宠爱的人会受此冷落。

paragraphs.text: "Well, truly," said Cyrus, "I do not like him. He is proud and overbearing. He thinks that he makes a fine figure when he waits on you." "And so he does," said the king. "He is very skillful as a cupbearer." "That may be so," answered Cyrus, "but if you will let me be your cupbearer tomorrow, I think I can serve you quite as well."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "说实话，"居鲁士说，"我不喜欢他。他傲慢自大，以为侍奉您时姿态优雅。" "确实如此，"国王说，"他斟酒技艺娴熟。" "也许吧，"居鲁士答道，"但若您明日让我担任斟酒人，我定能同样胜任。"

paragraphs.text: King Astyages smiled. He saw that Cyrus had a will of his own, and this pleased him very much.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿斯提阿格斯国王笑了。他看出居鲁士有自己的主见，这令他十分欣慰。

paragraphs.text: "I shall be glad to see what you can do," he said. "Tomorrow, you shall be the king's cupbearer."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "我很想见识你的本事，"他说，"明日就由你来做国王的斟酒人。"

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 12. ALTRUISTIC STORIES

paragraphs.text: Stories of unselfish heroism appeal to every age, but they find their strongest interest for the spirit of youth during the years of middle adolescence. Such stories of self-sacrifice may be selected from the Bible, history, fiction, or modern life. They not only show what is noble action, but touch the soul with the contagion of self-sacrificing deeds. From the Ethical Index, on page 291, under Altruism, Loyalty, Self-sacrifice, and such synonyms, a list of altruistic stories may be made.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 无私英雄主义的故事对每个年龄段都有吸引力，但他们对青春期中期的青春精神最感兴趣。这些自我牺牲的故事可以选自圣经、历史、小说或现代生活。他们不仅展示了什么是高尚的行为，而且以自我牺牲的行为感染着灵魂。从《道德索引》第 291 页，在利他主义、忠诚、自我牺牲和此类同义词下，可以列出一份利他故事列表。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 8. THE CROW AND THE PITCHER

paragraphs.text: Once a Crow who was very thirsty found a pitcher with a little water at the bottom which he was unable to reach. He tried to overturn the pitcher but it was too heavy. "Ah! Ah! I know what I'll do," he said. So he gathered up pebbles from the ground, and one after another dropped them into the pitcher until the water gradually reached the top. Then the wise Crow was able to drink all the water he wanted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只非常口渴的乌鸦发现了一个水罐，底部有一点水，但他够不到。他试图打翻水罐，但它太重了。 “啊！啊！我知道我要做什么，”他说。于是，他从地上捡起小石子，一个又一个地扔进水罐里，直到水逐渐到达顶部。然后聪明的乌鸦就能够喝到他想要喝的水了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A very young Fox, who had never before seen a Lion, happened to meet one in the forest. A single look was enough to send the Fox off at top speed for the nearest hiding place.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只非常年轻的狐狸以前从未见过狮子，但碰巧在森林里遇到了一只狮子。只要看一眼就足以让狐狸以最快的速度逃往最近的藏身之处。

paragraphs.text: The second time the Fox saw the Lion he stopped behind a tree to look at him a moment before slinking away. But the third time, the Fox went boldly up to the Lion and, without turning a hair, said, "Hello, there, old top."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸第二次看到狮子时，他在一棵树后面停下来看了他一会儿，然后就溜走了。但第三次，狐狸勇敢地走到狮子面前，头也不回地说道：“你好，老头儿。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: When you have finished reading, write the answers to these questions. If you can not answer immediately, find the answer somewhere in the story, but do not read it all again; read only enough to get the correct answer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 读完后，写下这些问题的答案。如果你不能立即回答，请在故事中的某个地方找到答案，但不要再读一遍；只阅读足以获得正确答案的内容。

paragraphs.text: 1. What two reasons can you find to account for the greater loss by fire in the United States than in Europe?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 你能找到哪两个原因来解释美国的火灾损失比欧洲更大？

paragraphs.text: 2. Which of these two reasons can be the more quickly removed? Which one can you help to remove?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 这两个原因哪一个可以更快地排除？你可以帮忙删除哪一个？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 14. THE CONCEITED GRASSHOPPER

paragraphs.text: One day a very young Grasshopper and an old Rooster met out in a field. "I can jump higher than anybody," chirped the Grasshopper. "All right; let me see you do it," said the Rooster, at the same time opening his mouth wide as if he meant to yawn. "Here I go, then," cried the Grasshopper. He jumped so high he landed right in the mouth of the Rooster, who gulped him down. That was the end of the boasting Grasshopper.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只年幼的蚱蜢和一只老公鸡在田野里相遇。 “我比任何人都跳得更高。”蚱蜢叽叽喳喳地说。 “好吧，让我看看你怎么做，”公鸡说着，同时张大了嘴，好像要打哈欠。 “那我走了，”蚱蜢喊道。他跳得太高了，正好落在公鸡的嘴里，公鸡把他吞了下去。自吹自擂的蚱蜢就这样结束了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Sixth Eclectic Reader: EXAMPLES. (23)

paragraphs.text: 3. As he spoke without fear of consequences', so his actions were marked with the most unbending resolution,
paragraphs.translation_zh: 3. 由于他说话时不担心后果，因此他的行动也表现出最不屈不挠的决心，

paragraphs.text: 5. If then, his Providence' out of our evil, seek to bring forth good', our labor must be to prevent that end.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 5. 如果他的旨意“从我们的邪恶中寻求良善”，我们的努力就必须阻止这一结果的发生。

paragraphs.text: 6. He', born for the universe', narrowed his mind, And to party gave up what was meant for mankind.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 6.他“为宇宙而生”，狭隘了自己的心胸，为党放弃了对人类的意义。

paragraphs.text: Exception 1.--Relative emphasis often reverses this and the first rule, because emphasis is here expressed in part by changing the usual inflections.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 例外 1——相对强调常常颠倒这一规则和第一条规则，因为这里的强调部分是通过改变通常的词形变化来表达的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE HEDGEHOG

paragraphs.text: A Fox, swimming across a river, was barely able to reach the bank, where he lay bruised and exhausted from his struggle with the swift current. Soon a swarm of blood-sucking flies settled on him; but he lay quietly, still too weak to run away from them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狐狸游过一条河，好不容易才到达岸边，他躺在那里，因与湍急的水流搏斗而伤痕累累，精疲力尽。很快，一群吸血苍蝇落在了他身上。但他静静地躺着，仍然太虚弱，无法逃离他们。

paragraphs.text: "No, no!" exclaimed the Fox, "do not disturb them! They have taken all they can hold. If you drive them away, another greedy swarm will come and take the little blood I have left."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，不！”狐狸喊道：“别打扰他们！他们已经夺走了他们所能拥有的一切。如果你把他们赶走，另一群贪婪的蜂群就会过来，夺走我仅剩的一点血。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE PEACOCK AND THE CRANE

paragraphs.text: A Peacock, puffed up with vanity, met a Crane one day, and to impress him spread his gorgeous tail in the Sun.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只自负的孔雀遇到了一只鹤，为了给鹤留下深刻的印象，孔雀在阳光下展开了它华丽的尾巴。

paragraphs.text: "Look," he said. "What have you to compare with this? I am dressed in all the glory of the rainbow, while your feathers are gray as dust!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看，”他说。 “你有什么可与此相比的？我身着彩虹般的光彩，而你的羽毛却灰如尘土！”

paragraphs.text: "Follow me if you can," he said. But the Peacock stood where he was among the birds of the barnyard, while the Crane soared in freedom far up into the blue sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果可以的话，跟我来，”他说。但孔雀站在谷仓里的鸟儿中间，而鹤则自由地飞向蓝天。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: How do you like to go up in a swing, Up in the air so blue? Oh, I do think it the pleasantest thing Ever a child can do!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你喜欢荡秋千吗，在如此蔚蓝的天空中？哦，我确实认为这是孩子能做的最愉快的事情！

paragraphs.text: Up in the air and over the wall, Till I can see so wide, Rivers and trees and cattle and all Over the countryside--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在空中，越过墙，直到我能看到如此广阔，河流、树木、牛群以及整个乡村——

paragraphs.text: Till I look down on the garden green, Down on the roof so brown-- Up in the air I go flying again, Up in the air and down!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 直到我俯视绿色的花园， 俯视棕色的屋顶—— 在空中我再次飞翔， 在空中又向下！

paragraphs.text: LIBRARIES, The Free Travelling, in Wisconsin. The Story of Their Growth, etc. _Wisconsin Free Library Commission_, Madison, 1897.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 威斯康星州的图书馆，自由旅行。他们的成长故事等。_威斯康星州免费图书馆委员会_，麦迪逊，1897 年。

paragraphs.text: LIBRARY Literature in England and in the United States During the Nineteenth Century (F. J. Teggart)--_Library Journal_, 26:257.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 十九世纪英国和美国的图书馆文学（F. J. Teggart）--《图书馆杂志》，26:257。

paragraphs.text: LIBRARY Work, Rational, With Children, and the Preparation For It (Frances J. Olcott)--Carnegie Library, Pittsburgh. Boston: A. L. A. Pub. Board. Reprint Series, No. 9, $0.05.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 图书馆工作，理性的，与孩子一起，及其准备（弗朗西斯·J·奥尔科特）——匹兹堡卡内基图书馆。波士顿：A. L. A. 酒吧。木板。重印系列，第 9 期，0.05 美元。

paragraphs.text: MUSIC, The Introduction of the Study of, into the Public Schools of Boston and of America (J. C. Johnson)--_Boston_, 1:622.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 音乐，波士顿和美国公立学校的研究简介（J. C. Johnson）--_波士顿_，1:622。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 7. THE FOX AND THE GRAPES

paragraphs.text: A Fox who was hungry saw some large, juicy grapes on a vine high up in a tree. "How good they will taste," said he; "I am going to have some of them." Then he gave a run and leaped as high as he could, but the grapes were still far above his head. He could not reach them no matter how high he jumped. At last he trotted off in a rage, muttering, "I know those are sour grapes and not worth eating."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只饥饿的狐狸看到高高的藤蔓上有一些又大又多汁的葡萄。 “它们尝起来多美味啊，”他说。 “我要一些。”然后他跑了起来，尽可能地跳高，但葡萄仍然离他头顶很远。无论他跳得多高，他都够不到他们。最后他怒气冲冲地跑开了，嘟囔道：“我知道那是酸葡萄心理，不值得吃。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Fifth Eclectic Reader: Passion and Grief

paragraphs.text: "Come back! come back!" he cried, in grief. "Across this stormy water, And I'll forgive your Highland chief, My daughter! O, my daughter!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “回来！回来！”他悲伤地哭了起来。 “跨越这暴风雨的水域，我会原谅你的高地酋长，我的女儿！哦，我的女儿！”

paragraphs.text: Plaintive I have lived long enough: my way of life Is fallen into the sear, the yellow leaf: And that which should accompany old age, As honor, love, obedience, troops of friends, I must not look to have.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哀怨的我已经活得够久了：我的生活方式已落入焦灼，黄叶之中：而那些应该伴随老年的东西，如荣誉、爱、服从、朋友的军队，我一定不希望拥有。

paragraphs.text: Calm A very great portion of this globe is covered with water, which is called sea, and is very distinct from rivers and lakes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 平静 地球的很大一部分被水覆盖，称为海洋，与河流和湖泊截然不同。

paragraphs.text: The only elaborate and scholarly study in English. Vol. I of a reprint of _Caxton's Aesop_. Published in 1889 in a limited edition and not easily accessible.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 唯一详尽且学术性的英语研究。卷。我是_卡克斯顿的《伊索寓言》的重印本。该书于 1889 年出版，限量版，不易获取。

paragraphs.text: Eighty-two selected fables. The Introduction is a summary of all the essential conclusions reached in the study above.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 精选寓言八十二篇。引言是上述研究中得出的所有基本结论的总结。

paragraphs.text: These three books are excellent for simplified versions of the eastern group. Those desiring to get closer to the sources may refer to Cowell , _The Jataka, or Stories of the Buddha's Former Births_; Rhys-Davids, _Buddhist Birth Stories_; Keith-Falconer, _Bidpai's Fables_.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这三本书非常适合东方组的简化版本。那些想要更接近来源的人可以参考考威尔的《本生经》或《佛陀前世故事》；里斯-戴维斯，《佛教诞生故事》；基思-福尔科纳，_Bidpai 的寓言_。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: DICK WHITTINGTON--II

paragraphs.text: The things were sold, and when the ship came back, each person had the money for what he had sent.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 东西都卖了，船回来的时候，每个人都拿到了自己所寄东西的钱。

paragraphs.text: He had gone but a short way when he heard the sound of the six great bells of Bow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他刚走了没多久，就听到了弓六大钟的声音。

paragraphs.text: "It is strange that the bells should speak to me," said Dick, "but if I am to be Lord Mayor of London, I will gladly turn back."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “钟声竟然对我说话，这很奇怪，”迪克说，“但如果我成为伦敦市长，我会很乐意回去。”

paragraphs.text: "I hope they have not missed me," said Dick, as he gently opened the door and stole softly in.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我希望他们没有错过我，”迪克一边说，一边轻轻地打开门，轻轻地溜了进去。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: ALL THINGS BEAUTIFUL

paragraphs.text: All things bright and beautiful, All creatures great and small, All things wise and wonderful, The Lord God made them all.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一切明亮而美丽的事物，一切伟大和渺小的生物，一切智慧而美妙的事物，主上帝创造了它们。

paragraphs.text: Each little flower that opens, Each little bird that sings, He made their glowing colors, He made their tiny wings.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每朵开放的小花，每只歌唱的小鸟，他创造了它们绚丽的色彩，他创造了它们微小的翅膀。

paragraphs.text: The cold wind in the winter, The pleasant summer sun, The ripe fruits in the garden, He made them every one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天的寒风，夏天的宜人阳光，花园里成熟的果实，每一个都是他创造的。

paragraphs.text: He gave us eyes to see them, And lips that we might tell How great is God Almighty, Who hath made all things well.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他给了我们眼睛来观察它们，给了我们嘴唇让我们可以讲述全能的上帝是多么伟大，他使万物变得美好。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: BED IN SUMMER

paragraphs.text: In winter I get up at night And dress by yellow candle-light. In summer, quite the other way, I have to go to bed by day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天我晚上起床，在黄色烛光下穿衣。在夏天，情况恰恰相反，我白天必须上床睡觉。

paragraphs.text: I have to go to bed and see The birds still hopping on the tree, Or hear the grown-up people's feet Still going past me in the street.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我必须上床睡觉，看看鸟儿仍在树上跳跃，或者听到大人的脚步声仍在街上从我身边经过。

paragraphs.text: And does it not seem hard to you, When all the sky is clear and blue, And I should like so much to play, To have to go to bed by day?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当所有的天空都晴朗而蔚蓝时，我会非常喜欢玩耍，而白天却必须上床睡觉，这对你来说不似乎很难吗？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had stolen a Lamb and was carrying it off to his lair to eat it. But his plans were very much changed when he met a Lion, who, without making any excuses, took the Lamb away from him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼偷了一只羔羊，并把它带到自己的巢穴里吃掉。但当他遇到一只狮子时，他的计划发生了很大的变化，狮子没有任何借口，就把羔羊从他身边带走了。

paragraphs.text: The Lion looked back, but as the Wolf was too far away to be taught a lesson without too much inconvenience, he said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狮子回头看了一眼，但由于狼距离太远，给教训带来了很大不便，他说道：

paragraphs.text: "Your property? Did you buy it, or did the Shepherd make you a gift of it? Pray tell me, how did you get it?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你的财产？是你买的，还是牧羊人送给你的礼物？请告诉我，你是怎么得到它的？”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Discussion: 1. What became of the arrow? Of the song? 2. Where was the arrow found? When? 3. Where was the Song found? 4. Point out lines that rime. 5. What is Longfellow's purpose in this poem? 6. Why is the poet's song compared to the flight of an arrow? 7. A poet once said, "Let me make the Songs of a nation, and I care not who makes the laws." What did he mean? 8. What was the Song doing "in the heart of a friend"?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论： 1. 箭头变成了什么？歌曲的？ 2. 箭头在哪里找到的？什么时候？ 3. 这首歌曲是在哪里发现的？ 4. 指出有韵律的线条。 5.朗费罗这首诗的目的是什么？ 6.为什么诗人把歌曲比作箭的飞行？ 7. 一位诗人曾经说过：“让我创作一个国家的歌曲，我不在乎谁制定法律。”他是什么意思？ 8. 宋词“在朋友心里”在做什么？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: How to Tell Stories to Children: "DEAR ME, WHERE IS MY TOADSTOOL?"

paragraphs.text: From these four instances we may, perhaps, deduce certain general principles of adaptation which have at least proved valuable to those using them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从这四个例子中，我们或许可以推断出某些适应的一般原则，这些原则至少被证明对那些使用它们的人来说是有价值的。

paragraphs.text: These are suggestions which the practised story-teller will find trite. But to others they may prove a fair foundation on which to build a personal method to be developed by experience. I have given them a tabular arrangement below.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 对于经验丰富的讲故事者来说，这些建议会显得陈词滥调。但对其他人来说，它们可能是建立通过经验发展的个人方法的公平基础。我在下面给了他们一个表格安排。

paragraphs.text: The preliminary step in all cases is _Analysis of the Story._ The aim, then, is to _reduce_ a long story or to _amplify_ a short one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 所有情况下的第一步都是_故事分析。_那么，目的就是_减少_长篇故事或_放大_短篇故事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE KID AND THE WOLF

paragraphs.text: A frisky young Kid had been left by the herdsman on the thatched roof of a sheep shelter to keep him out of harm's way. The Kid was browsing near the edge of the roof, when he spied a Wolf and began to jeer at him, making faces and abusing him to his heart's content.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 牧民将一只活泼的小孩子留在了羊棚的茅草屋顶上，以免他受到伤害。孩子正在屋顶边缘附近闲逛，这时他发现了一只狼，开始嘲笑他，做鬼脸，尽情地辱骂他。

paragraphs.text: "I hear you," said the Wolf, "and I haven't the least grudge against you for what you say or do. When you are up there it is the roof that's talking, not you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我听到你的声音了，”狼说，“我对你的言行没有丝毫怨恨。当你在上面时，是屋顶在说话，而不是你。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE PLANE TREE

paragraphs.text: Two Travellers, walking in the noonday sun, sought the shade of a widespreading tree to rest. As they lay looking up among the pleasant leaves, they saw that it was a Plane Tree.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两个旅人在正午的阳光下行走，寻找一棵大树的阴凉处休息。当他们躺在宜人的树叶中抬头一看时，他们发现那是一棵梧桐树。

paragraphs.text: "How useless is the Plane!" said one of them. "It bears no fruit whatever, and only serves to litter the ground with leaves."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “飞机多么无用啊！”其中一位说道。 “它不会结出任何果实，只会在地上散落叶子。”

paragraphs.text: "Ungrateful creatures!" said a voice from the Plane Tree. "You lie here in my cooling shade, and yet you say I am useless! Thus ungratefully, O Jupiter, do men receive their blessings!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “忘恩负义的生物！”梧桐树上传来一个声音。 “你躺在我凉爽的树荫下，却说我毫无用处！朱庇特啊，人们就是这样忘恩负义地接受他们的祝福！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: THE ARROW AND THE SONG

paragraphs.text: I shot an arrow into the air; It Fell to earth, I knew not where; For, so swiftly it flew, the sight Could not follow it in its flight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我向空中射了一箭；它掉到地上，我不知道在哪里；因为它飞得如此之快，视线无法追随它的飞行。

paragraphs.text: I breathed a Song into the air; It fell to earth, I knew not where; For who has sight so keen and strong That it can follow the flight of Song?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我向空气中吹出一首歌曲；它掉到了地上，我不知道在哪里；谁的视力如此敏锐和强大，能够追随宋的飞行？

paragraphs.text: Long, long afterwards, in an oak I found the arrow, still unbroke; And the song, from beginning to end, I found again in the heart of a friend.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 很久很久以后，我在一棵橡树里发现了那支箭，它仍然完好无损。而这首歌，从头到尾，我又在朋友的心里找到了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 2. MISSIONARY STORIES

paragraphs.text: Numerous short and simple stories of heroic lives have recently been written in a very attractive way for boys and girls. These hero stories are for telling, not reading, in home, Sunday-school classes and opening exercises, junior mission circles, or young people's missionary meetings. A few of the best are: "Fifty Missionary Heroes Every Boy and Girl Should Know," by Julia H. Johnson; "Love Stories of Great Missionaries," by Belle M. Brain; "The White Man at Work," and "The Splendid Quest," by Matthews (suitable for children eight to fifteen).
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最近，许多关于英雄生活的短小而简单的故事以非常吸引男孩和女孩的方式写成。这些英雄故事适合在家庭、主日学课程和开场练习、初级传教圈或年轻人的传教会议上讲述，而不是阅读。其中最好的一些是：朱莉娅·H·约翰逊（Julia H. Johnson）的《每个男孩和女孩都应该知道的五十个传教士英雄》； 《伟大传教士的爱情故事》，作者：Belle M. Brain；马修斯的《工作中的白人》和《辉煌的探索》（适合八到十五岁的孩子）。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ALFRED TENNYSON

paragraphs.text: What does little birdie say, In her nest at peep of day? "Let me fly," says little birdie, "Mother, let me fly away." "Birdie, rest a little longer, Till the little wings are stronger." So she rests a little longer, Then she flies away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小鸟在黎明时分在她的巢里说什么？ “让我飞吧，”小鸟说，“妈妈，让我飞走吧。” “小鸟，再休息一会儿，直到小翅膀更加坚强。”于是她休息了一会儿，然后就飞走了。

paragraphs.text: What does little baby say, In her bed at peep of day? Baby says, like little birdie, "Let me rise and fly away." "Baby, sleep a little longer, Till the little limbs are stronger." If she sleeps a little longer, Baby too shall fly away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小宝贝在凌晨时分在床上说什么？宝宝像小鸟一样说：“让我起来飞走吧。” “宝贝，再睡一会儿吧，等小四肢更加有力了。”如果她再睡一会儿，宝宝也会飞走的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: IF EVER I SEE

paragraphs.text: If ever I see, On bush or tree, Young birds in their pretty nest; I must not, in play, Steal the birds away, To grieve their mother's breast.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果我看见，在灌木丛或树上，幼鸟在它们美丽的巢中；我决不能在玩耍时，偷走鸟儿，让它们的母亲的胸膛悲伤。

paragraphs.text: My mother, I know, Would sorrow so, Should I be stolen away; So I'll speak to the birds In my softest words, Nor hurt them in my play.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我知道，如果我被偷走，我的母亲会如此悲伤；所以我要用我最温柔的话语对鸟儿说话，也不用我的游戏伤害它们。

paragraphs.text: And when they can fly In the bright blue sky, They'll warble a song to me; And then if I'm sad It will make me glad To think they are happy and free.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当它们能飞翔在明亮的蓝天时，它们将为我吟唱一首歌；然后，如果我感到悲伤，想到他们是快乐和自由的，我就会感到高兴。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: LITTLE BIRDIE

paragraphs.text: What does little birdie say, In her nest at peep of day? Let me fly, says little birdie, Mother, let me fly away. Birdie, rest a little longer, Till the little wings are stronger. So she rests a little longer, Then she flies away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小鸟在黎明时分在她的巢里说什么？让我飞吧，小鸟说，妈妈，让我飞走吧。小鸟，再休息一会儿，直到小翅膀更加坚强。于是她休息了一会儿，然后就飞走了。

paragraphs.text: What does little baby say, In her bed at peep of day? Baby says, like little birdie, Let me rise and fly away. Baby, sleep a little longer, Till the little limbs are stronger. If she sleeps a little longer, Baby too shall fly away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小宝贝在凌晨时分在床上说什么？宝贝像小鸟一样说，让我起来飞走吧。宝贝，再睡一会儿，直到小四肢更加有力。如果她再睡一会儿，宝宝也会飞走的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 3. SELECT THE STORY

paragraphs.text: Select your story with some definite purpose in mind--pure enjoyment or some definite ethical principle, and let the aim be clearly in mind in the preparation for telling it. Select your story also with the child's story-interests in mind, as presented in Chapter II. Make sure also that it is suitable in length and in style. Children who are accustomed to hearing stories can listen a longer time than those whose ears and brains are quite untrained. With very young children five minutes gives room for a really stirring tale.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 选择你的故事时要有明确的目的——纯粹的享受或某种明确的道德原则，并在准备讲述它时清楚地记住目标。选择故事时还要考虑到孩子的故事兴趣，如第二章所述。还要确保它的长度和样式合适。习惯听故事的孩子比那些耳朵和大脑未经训练的孩子听故事的时间更长。对于年幼的孩子来说，五分钟的时间可以为一个真正激动人心的故事提供空间。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FISHERMAN AND THE LITTLE FISH

paragraphs.text: A poor Fisherman, who lived on the fish he caught, had bad luck one day and caught nothing but a very small fry. The Fisherman was about to put it in his basket when the little Fish said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位贫穷的渔夫靠捕到的鱼为生，有一天运气不好，只捕到了一条很小的鱼苗。渔夫正要把它放进篮子里，小鱼说：

paragraphs.text: "Please spare me, Mr. Fisherman! I am so small it is not worth while to carry me home. When I am bigger, I shall make you a much better meal."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “渔夫先生，请放过我吧！我这么小，不值得背我回家。等我长大了，我会给你做一顿更好的饭菜。”

paragraphs.text: "How foolish I should be," he said, "to throw you back. However small you may be, you are better than nothing at all."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我真是太愚蠢了，”他说，“把你扔了回去。不管你有多小，你总比什么都没有好。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE QUACK TOAD

paragraphs.text: An old Toad once informed all his neighbors that he was a learned doctor. In fact he could cure anything. The Fox heard the news and hurried to see the Toad. He looked the Toad over very carefully.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只老蟾蜍曾经告诉他所有的邻居，他是一位博学的医生。事实上他可以治愈任何事情。狐狸听到这个消息，赶紧去看蟾蜍。他非常仔细地打量着蟾蜍。

paragraphs.text: "Mr. Toad," he said, "I've been told that you cure anything! But just take a look at yourself, and then try some of your own medicine. If you can cure yourself of that blotchy skin and that rheumatic gait, someone might believe you. Otherwise, I should advise you to try some other profession."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “蟾蜍先生，”他说，“有人告诉我，你可以治愈任何疾病！但是你看看你自己，然后尝试一下你自己的药物。如果你能治愈自己的斑点皮肤和风湿步态，有人可能会相信你。否则，我应该建议你尝试其他职业。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE BOY AND THE FILBERTS

paragraphs.text: A Boy was given permission to put his hand into a pitcher to get some filberts. But he took such a great fistful that he could not draw his hand out again. There he stood, unwilling to give up a single filbert and yet unable to get them all out at once. Vexed and disappointed he began to cry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个男孩被允许把手伸进水罐里去拿一些榛子。但他握得太紧了，以至于他无法再抽出手来。他站在那里，不愿意放弃一根榛子，但又无法一下子把它们全部拿出来。他既烦恼又失望，开始哭泣。

paragraphs.text: "My boy," said his mother, "be satisfied with half the nuts you have taken and you will easily get your hand out. Then perhaps you may have some more filberts some other time."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我的孩子，”他的母亲说，“只要拿了一半的坚果就够了，这样你就可以很容易地把手伸出来了。那么也许下次你可能会得到更多的榛子。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: ENGLISH VARIANTS OF CINDERELLA

paragraphs.text: _Remarks._--In going over these various versions, the first and perhaps most striking thing that comes out is the substantial agreement of the variants in each _language_. The English--_i.e._, Scotch, variants go together; the Gaelic ones agree to differ from the English. I can best display this important agreement and difference by the accompanying two tables, which give, in parallel columns, Miss Cox's abstracts of her tabulations, in which each incident is shortly given in technical phraseology. It is practically impossible to use the long tabulations for comparative purposes without some such shorthand.
paragraphs.translation_zh: _备注._--在检查这些不同的版本时，首先出现的，也许是最引人注目的事情是每种_语言_中的变体的基本一致。英语（即苏格兰语）的变体是在一起的；盖尔人同意与英国人不同。我可以通过随附的两个表格最好地展示这一重要的一致和差异，这两个表格在平行的列中给出了考克斯小姐的表格摘要，其中每个事件都简短地用技术术语给出了。如果没有这样的速记，实际上不可能将长列表用于比较目的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: TOM THUMB--III

paragraphs.text: Then Tom's mother took him in her apron and ran with him to the house, but he was not hurt in the least.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后汤姆的妈妈把他围在围裙里，带着他跑进了房子，但他没有受到丝毫伤害。

paragraphs.text: As they were passing over the sea, Tom got away and fell into the water, where a great fish swallowed him at one mouthful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他们经过大海时，汤姆逃脱并掉进了水里，一条大鱼把他吞了下去。

paragraphs.text: Soon after this the fish was caught, and it was such a big one that it was sent at once to King Arthur.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久之后，这条鱼就被捕获了，而且这条鱼很大，所以它立即被送到了亚瑟王那里。

paragraphs.text: When the cook cut open the fish, out jumped Tom Thumb. Tom was brought before the king, and his story was told.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当厨师切开鱼时，大拇指汤姆跳了出来。汤姆被带到国王面前，并讲述了他的故事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: TRY THIS

paragraphs.text: This nonsense test must be worked out carefully or it may fool you. You will need only a small piece of paper for your answers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个无意义的测试必须仔细进行，否则它可能会欺骗你。您只需要一张小纸即可获得答案。

paragraphs.text: 1. If your name is Geraldine, or if you are not yet past 37 years of age, or
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 如果您的名字是 Geraldine，或者您尚未超过 37 岁，或者

paragraphs.text: sign your name anywhere on your paper. Then, if you have signed your name, never mind the second paragraph, but skip to the third.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在纸上的任意位置签上您的名字。然后，如果您签了名字，请不要介意第二段，而是跳到第三段。

paragraphs.text: 2. Rub out your name and write the name of the first president of the United States in its stead. Then take the remaining paragraph.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 擦掉你的名字并写上美国第一任总统的名字。然后取剩下的段落。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ISAAC WATTS

paragraphs.text: How doth the little busy bee Improve each shining hour, And gather honey all the day From every opening flower!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 忙碌的小蜜蜂如何改善每一个闪亮的时刻，并整天从每一朵开放的花朵中采集蜂蜜！

paragraphs.text: How skilfully she builds her cell, How neat she spreads the wax! And labors hard to store it well With the sweet food she makes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她建造她的牢房多么熟练，她涂蜡多么整齐！并努力地把它和她做的甜食一起储存起来。

paragraphs.text: In works of labor or of skill, I would be busy too; For Satan finds some mischief still For idle hands to do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 无论是体力工作还是技术工作，我也会很忙。因为撒旦仍然发现一些恶作剧可以让闲着的人去做。

paragraphs.text: In books, or work, or healthful play, Let my first years be past, That I may give for every day Some good account at last.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在书籍、工作或健康的游戏中， 让我的最初几年成为过去， 以便我最终可以为每一天提供一些好的记录。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Children's Hour, Volume 7: (The NISTER PICTURE-BOOKS are sold in this country by DUTTON.)

paragraphs.text: CALDECOTT, RANDOLPH--Toy Books. Warne, (4 vols.) $1.25 each. The separate stories are sold at $0.25 each, and comprise, among a large number, the following: The Farmer's Boy; A Frog He Would A-Wooing Go; Hey Diddle Diddle and Baby Bunting; The House That Jack Built; The Milk Maid; The Queen of Hearts; Ride a Cock Horse; Sing a Song of Sixpence; John Gilpin.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 兰道夫·卡尔德科特——玩具书籍。 Warne，（4 卷）每本 1.25 美元。这些独立的故事每篇售价为 0.25 美元，其中包括以下内容：《农夫的男孩》；《农夫的男孩》；一只青蛙，他会向他求爱；嘿迪德尔迪德尔和婴儿彩旗；杰克建造的房子；挤奶女工；红心皇后；骑公鸡马；唱一首六便士之歌；约翰·吉尔平.

paragraphs.text: Mother Hubbard; This Little Pig; Aladdin; Beauty and the Beast; Bluebeard; The Forty Thieves; The Frog Prince; Goody Two Shoes; Sleeping Beauty; The Fairy Ship; Baby's A B C. Lane, $0.25 each.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哈伯德妈妈；这只小猪；阿拉丁；美女和野兽;蓝胡子；四十大盗；青蛙王子；古迪两双鞋；睡美人;仙女船； Baby's A B C. Lane，每个 0.25 美元。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: English Fairy Tales: MOUSE AND MOUSER

paragraphs.text: CAT (_gruffly_). I'll wear' em and tear 'em, good body, good body. I'll wear 'em and tear 'em, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫（_粗暴_）。我会穿上它们，撕裂它们，好身体，好身体。我会穿上它们并撕裂它们，好身体。

paragraphs.text: CAT (_snarling_). The more meat you had, good body, good body, The more meat you had, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫（_咆哮_）。肉越多，身体越好，身体越好， 肉越多，身体越好。

paragraphs.text: MOUSE. I put it in the window to cool, my lady, I put it in the window to cool.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老鼠。我把它放在窗户上降温，女士，我把它放在窗户上降温。

paragraphs.text: CAT. (_sharply_). The faster you'd eat it, good body, good body, The faster you'd eat it, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫。 （_尖锐_）。吃得越快，身体好，身体好， 吃得越快，身体好。

paragraphs.text: MOUSE (_timidly_). The cat came and ate it, my lady, my lady, The cat came and ate it, my lady.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 鼠标（_胆怯_）。猫来了，吃了它，我的女士，我的女士， 猫来了，吃了它，我的女士。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE SHEEP

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had been hurt in a fight with a Bear. He was unable to move and could not satisfy his hunger and thirst. A Sheep passed by near his hiding place, and the Wolf called to him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼在与一只熊的战斗中受伤了。他无法动弹，也无法满足自己的饥渴。一只羊从他藏身的地方附近经过，狼呼唤着他。

paragraphs.text: "Please fetch me a drink of water," he begged, "that might give me strength enough so I can get me some solid food."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “请给我喝点水，”他恳求道，“这可能会给我足够的力量，这样我就可以吃点固体食物了。”

paragraphs.text: "Solid food!" said the Sheep. "That means me, I suppose. If I should bring you a drink, it would only serve to wash me down your throat. Don't talk to me about a drink!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “固体食物！”羊说。 “我想，那指的是我。如果我给你拿一杯饮料，那只会让我咽下去。别跟我谈论饮料！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: 2. GREAT SMALL

paragraphs.text: Complete the exercise by selecting the opposites from each remaining group, and writing them after the proper figure on your paper. When you have finished put down your pencil and wait quietly for the others.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从剩下的每一组中选择相反的图形，并将它们写在纸上正确的图形后面，以完成练习。当你完成后，放下铅笔，静静地等待其他人。

paragraphs.text: 1. HIGH (sky, low, above, deep). 2. GREAT (less, large, small, beautiful). 3. HILL (mountain, valley, high, river). 4. MANY (few, more, plenty, less). 5. GRADUAL (quick, slowly, sudden, degree). 6. WOUND (sword, nurse, heal, bind). 7. LIGHT (bright, sun, shadow, darkness). 8. STRAIGHT (long, uneven, twist, crooked). 9. LAND (plain, water, farm, river). 10. SPRING (fall, cool, October, green).
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 高（天空、低、上方、深）。 2. 伟大（少、大、小、漂亮）。 3. HILL（山、谷、高、河）。 4.很多（少、多、多、少）。 5. 渐进（快、慢、突然、程度）。 6.伤口（剑、护士、治愈、捆绑）。 7. 光（明亮、阳光、阴影、黑暗）。 8. 直（长、参差不齐、扭曲、弯曲）。 9. 土地（平原、水域、农场、河流）。 10. 春天（秋天，凉爽，十月，绿色）。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 6. THE DOVE AND THE ANT

paragraphs.text: Once a little Ant went down to the river to drink. He fell into the water and began to drown. Just then a Dove, perched on a tree, saw him and quickly dropped down a leaf, which served as a little boat on which the Ant sailed safely to the shore. "Thank you," said the Ant as he shook his wet feet, "I shall not forget this." Next day a Hunter was aiming his bow and arrow straight at the Dove when the Ant bit his foot, making the man jump, and the Dove flew away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只小蚂蚁到河边去喝水。他掉进水里，开始淹死。就在这时，一只栖息在树上的鸽子看到了他，很快就从一片叶子上掉了下来，这片叶子成了一只小船，蚂蚁乘着它安全地驶到了岸边。 “谢谢你，”蚂蚁摇着湿漉漉的脚说道，“我不会忘记这一点。”第二天，一位猎人正用弓箭瞄准鸽子，蚂蚁咬了他的脚，使猎人跳了起来，鸽子飞走了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Sixth Eclectic Reader: III. ACCENT AND EMPHASIS. (33)

paragraphs.text: That syllable in a word which is uttered more forcibly than the others, is said to be accented, and is marked thus, ('); as the italicized syllables in the following words:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 单词中比其他音节发音更有力的音节被认为是重音音节，因此标记为 (')；如以下单词中的斜体音节：

paragraphs.text: Common usage alone determines upon what syllable the accent should be placed, and to the lexicographer it belongs, to ascertain and record its decision on this point.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 仅常见用法就决定了重音应该放在哪个音节，以及它所属的词典编纂者，以确定并记录其在这一点上的决定。

paragraphs.text: In some few cases, we can trace the reasons for common usage in this respect. In words which are used as different parts of speech, or which have different meanings, the distinction is sometimes denoted by changing the accent.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在某些情况下，我们可以追溯这方面常见用法的原因。在用作不同词类或具有不同含义的单词中，有时通过改变重音来表示区别。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND HIS SHADOW

paragraphs.text: A Wolf left his lair one evening in fine spirits and an excellent appetite. As he ran, the setting sun cast his shadow far out on the ground, and it looked as if the wolf were a hundred times bigger than he really was.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，一只狼离开了他的巢穴，心情很好，胃口也很好。当他奔跑的时候，夕阳将他的影子远远地投射在地上，看起来狼比他实际的体型大了一百倍。

paragraphs.text: "Why," exclaimed the Wolf proudly, "see how big I am! Fancy _me_ running away from a puny Lion! I'll show him who is fit to be king, he or I."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哎呀，”狼骄傲地喊道，“看看我有多大！想象一下我从一只弱小的狮子身边逃跑吧！我会让他知道谁适合当国王，是他还是我。”

paragraphs.text: Just then an immense shadow blotted him out entirely, and the next instant a Lion struck him down with a single blow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，一道巨大的阴影将他完全遮蔽，下一瞬间，一只狮子一击将他击倒。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: We built a ship upon the stairs All made of the back-bedroom chairs, And filled it full of sofa pillows To go a-sailing on the billows.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们在楼梯上造了一艘船，全是后卧室的椅子，里面装满了沙发枕头，要在波涛上航行。

paragraphs.text: We took a saw and several nails, And water in the nursery pails; And Tom said, "Let us also take An apple and a slice of cake;"-- Which was enough for Tom and me To go a-sailing on, till tea.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们拿了一把锯子和几颗钉子，还有苗圃里的水；汤姆说：“我们也带一个苹果和一块蛋糕吧；”——这足以让汤姆和我继续航行，直到喝茶。

paragraphs.text: We sailed along for days and days, And had the very best of plays; But Tom fell out and hurt his knee, So there was no one left but me.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们航行了几天又几天， 并享受了最好的表演；但是汤姆摔倒了，伤到了膝盖，所以除了我以外就没有人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: A GOOD PLAY

paragraphs.text: We built a ship upon the stairs, All made of the back-bedroom chairs, And filled it full of sofa pillows, To go a-sailing on the billows.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们在楼梯上造了一艘船，全部用后卧室的椅子组成，里面装满了沙发枕头，要在波涛上航行。

paragraphs.text: We took a saw and several nails, And water in the nursery pails; And Tom said, "Let us also take An apple and a slice of cake,"-- Which was enough for Tom and me To go a-sailing on, till tea.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们拿了一把锯子和几颗钉子，还有苗圃里的水；汤姆说：“我们也带一个苹果和一块蛋糕吧。”——这足以让汤姆和我继续航行，直到喝茶。

paragraphs.text: We sailed along for days and days, And had the very best of plays; But Tom fell out and hurt his knee, So there was no one left but me.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们航行了几天又几天， 并享受了最好的表演；但是汤姆摔倒了，伤到了膝盖，所以除了我以外就没有人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: Ella Higginson

paragraphs.text: I know a place where the sun is like gold, And the cherry blooms burst with snow; And down underneath is the loveliest nook, Where the four-leaf clovers grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我知道一个地方，阳光如金，樱花盛开，雪花缭绕；下面是最可爱的角落，四叶草生长的地方。

paragraphs.text: One leaf is for hope, and one is for faith, And one is for love, you know; But God put another in for luck-- If you search, you will find where they grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一片叶子代表希望，一片叶子代表信仰，一片叶子代表爱，你知道；但上帝为了好运而安排了另一个——如果你寻找，你就会找到它们生长的地方。

paragraphs.text: But you must have hope, and you must have faith; You must love and be strong; and so, If you work, if you wait, you will find the place Where the four-leaf clovers grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但你必须有希望，你必须有信心；你必须爱并坚强；所以，只要你努力，如果你等待，你就会找到四叶草生长的地方。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: JUPITER AND THE MONKEY

paragraphs.text: There was once a baby show among the Animals in the forest. Jupiter provided the prize. Of course all the proud mammas from far and near brought their babies. But none got there earlier than Mother Monkey. Proudly she presented her baby among the other contestants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 森林里的动物们曾经举办过一场婴儿表演。木星提供了奖品。当然，来自四面八方的骄傲妈妈们都带着她们的孩子来了。但没有人比猴妈妈更早到达那里。她自豪地将自己的孩子展示在其他参赛者中。

paragraphs.text: As you can imagine, there was quite a laugh when the Animals saw the ugly flat-nosed, hairless, pop-eyed little creature.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你可以想象，当动物们看到这个丑陋的扁鼻子、无毛、睁大眼睛的小生物时，大家都大笑起来。

paragraphs.text: "Laugh if you will," said the Mother Monkey. "Though Jupiter may not give him the prize, I know that he is the prettiest, the sweetest, the dearest darling in the world."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果你愿意的话，就笑吧。”猴妈妈说。 “虽然朱庇特可能不会给他奖品，但我知道他是世界上最漂亮、最可爱、最可爱的宝贝。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: A FAIRY

paragraphs.text: Ralph Waldo Emerson (1803-1882) is America's greatest spiritual teacher. His essays, such as "Self-Reliance" and "The American Scholar," are his chief claim to fame. The two brief poems given here are well known. "Fable" should be studied along with No. 236, since they emphasize the same lesson that size is after all a purely relative matter. "Concord Hymn" is a splendidly dignified expression of the debt of gratitude we owe to the memory of those who made our country possible. Of course no reader will fail to notice the famous last two lines of the first stanza.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 拉尔夫·沃尔多·爱默生（1803-1882）是美国最伟大的精神导师。他的文章，如《自力更生》和《美国学者》，是他成名的主要原因。这里给出的两首短诗是众所周知的。 《寓言》应该与第236号一起研究，因为它们强调同样的教训，即大小毕竟是纯粹相对的问题。 《协和赞美诗》以庄严而庄严的方式表达了我们对那些使我们的国家成为可能的人们的感激之情。当然，没有读者不会注意到第一节著名的最后两行。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: THE MAN AND THE SATYR

paragraphs.text: A Man and a Satyr having struck up an acquaintance, sat down together to eat. The day being wintry and cold, the Man put his fingers to his mouth and blew upon them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个人和一个色狼认识了，坐在一起吃饭。那天天气寒冷，男人把手指放在嘴边，对着手指吹气。

paragraphs.text: In a little while some hot food was placed before them, and the Man, raising the dish to his mouth, again blew upon it. "And what's the meaning of that, now?" said the Satyr.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不一会儿，一些热腾腾的食物就摆在了他们面前，男人把盘子举到嘴边，又对着它吹了一口气。 “那现在这有什么意义呢？”萨特说。

paragraphs.text: "Nay, then," said the Satyr, "from this moment I renounce your friendship, for I will have nothing to do with one who blows hot and cold with the same mouth."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，那么，”萨特说，“从这一刻起，我断绝了你的友谊，因为我不会和一个用同一张嘴忽冷忽热的人有任何关系。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: TOM THUMB--IV

paragraphs.text: The king grew very fond of Tom and his wise sayings. He took Tom with him wherever he went.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王非常喜欢汤姆和他的智慧名言。无论他走到哪里，他都带着汤姆。

paragraphs.text: If it began to rain, Tom would creep into the king's pocket and sleep until the rain was over.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果开始下雨，汤姆就会爬进国王的口袋里睡觉，直到雨停。

paragraphs.text: The king and queen never tired of seeing him ride his queer little horse and bravely wave his sword.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王和王后乐此不疲地看着他骑着那匹奇怪的小马，勇敢地挥舞着剑。

paragraphs.text: The king ran to help poor Tom, but the little mouse was dead, and Tom was scratched and bitten.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王跑去帮助可怜的汤姆，但小老鼠死了，汤姆也被抓伤和咬伤。

paragraphs.text: No indeed! he was soon well again, and fought many brave battles and did many brave deeds to please the king.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 确实不！他很快就康复了，并进行了许多英勇的战斗，做了许多英勇的事迹来取悦国王。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ELIZA LEE FOLLEN

paragraphs.text: O look at the moon! She is shining up there; O mother, she looks Like a lamp in the air.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哦，看月亮！她在那儿闪闪发光；哦，母亲，她看起来就像空中的一盏灯。

paragraphs.text: Last week she was smaller, And shaped like a bow; But now she's grown bigger, And round as an O.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 上周她变小了，形状像弓；但现在她长大了，圆得像个O形。

paragraphs.text: Pretty moon, pretty moon, How you shine on the door, And make it all bright On my nursery floor!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 美丽的月亮，美丽的月亮，你如何照在门上，让我的婴儿房地板上都明亮起来！

paragraphs.text: You shine on my playthings, And show me their place, And I love to look up At your pretty bright face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你照耀着我的玩具，让我看到它们的位置，我喜欢抬头看着你漂亮明亮的脸。

paragraphs.text: And there is a star Close by you, and maybe That small twinkling star Is your little baby.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你附近有一颗星星，也许那颗闪烁的小星星就是你的小宝贝。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: CHRISTINA G. ROSSETTI

paragraphs.text: The city mouse lives in a house;-- The garden mouse lives in a bower; He's friendly with the frogs and toads, And sees the pretty plants in flower.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 城市老鼠住在房子里；——花园老鼠住在凉亭里；——他对青蛙和蟾蜍很友好，并且看到美丽的植物开花。

paragraphs.text: The city mouse eats bread and cheese;-- The garden mouse eats what he can; We will not grudge him seeds and stocks, Poor little timid furry man.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 城市老鼠吃面包和奶酪；——花园老鼠吃他能吃的；——我们不会吝惜他的种子和股票，可怜的胆怯的毛茸茸的小男人。

paragraphs.text: The most famous use of this fable in literature is found in the _Satires_ of the great Roman poet, Horace (B.C. 65-8). He is regarded as one of the most polished of writers, and the ancient world's most truthful painter of social life and manners. Horace had a country seat among the Sabine hills to which he could retire from the worries and distractions of the world. His delight in his Sabine farm is shown clearly in his handling of the story. The passage is a part of Book II, Satire 6, and is in Conington's translation. Some well-known appearances of this same fable in English poetry may be found in Prior and Montagu's _City Mouse and Country Mouse_ and in Pope's _Imitations of Horace_.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个寓言在文学中最著名的运用出现在伟大的罗马诗人贺拉斯（Horace，公元前 65-8 年）的《讽刺诗》中。他被认为是最优秀的作家之一，也是古代世界最真实地描绘社会生活和礼仪的画家。贺拉斯在萨宾山间有一座乡间别墅，在那里他可以远离世俗的忧虑和干扰。他对萨宾农场的喜悦在他对故事的处理中清晰可见。这段文字是第二册讽刺书 6 的一部分，由科宁顿翻译。这个寓言在英语诗歌中的一些著名出现可以在普赖尔和蒙塔古的《城市老鼠和乡村老鼠》以及蒲柏的《模仿贺拉斯》中找到。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: CHARLES KINGSLEY

paragraphs.text: Three fishers went sailing out into the west,-- Out into the west as the sun went down; Each thought of the woman who loved him the best, And the children stood watching them out of the town; For men must work, and women must weep; And there's little to earn, and many to keep, Though the harbor bar be moaning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 三个渔夫向西航行，——太阳落山时向西航行；每个人都想起最爱他的女人， 孩子们站着看着他们出城；因为男人必须工作，女人必须哭泣；虽然港口酒吧在呻吟，但赚的钱很少，留的却很多。

paragraphs.text: Three wives sat up in the light-house tower, And trimmed the lamps as the sun went down; And they looked at the squall, and they looked at the shower, And the rack it came rolling up, ragged and brown; But men must work, and women must weep, Though storms be sudden, and waters deep, And the harbor bar be moaning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 三个妻子坐在灯塔塔上，在太阳落山时修剪灯光；他们看着暴风雨，看着淋浴， 淋浴架卷了起来，破烂不堪，呈棕色；但男人必须工作，女人必须哭泣， 尽管暴风雨突然，水深， 港口酒吧正在呻吟。

paragraphs.text: Three corpses lay out on the shining sands In the morning gleam as the tide went down, And the women are watching and wringing their hands, For those who will never come back to the town; For men must work, and women must weep,-- And the sooner it's over, the sooner to sleep,-- And good-by to the bar and its moaning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 三具尸体躺在闪亮的沙滩上，在潮水退去的晨光中，女人们正在观看并绞着双手，为那些永远不会回到小镇的人；因为男人必须工作，女人必须哭泣，——越早结束，越早睡觉，——告别酒吧和它的呻吟。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Federalist Papers: PUBLIUS

paragraphs.text: 1 This statement of the matter is taken from the printed collection of State constitutions. Pennsylvania and North Carolina are the two which contain the interdiction in these words: "As standing armies in time of peace are dangerous to liberty, THEY OUGHT NOT to be kept up." This is, in truth, rather a CAUTION than a PROHIBITION. New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Delaware, and Maryland have, in each of their bills of rights, a clause to this effect: "Standing armies are dangerous to liberty, and ought not to be raised or kept up WITHOUT THE CONSENT OF THE LEGISLATURE"; which is a formal admission of the authority of the Legislature. New York has no bills of rights, and her constitution says not a word about the matter. No bills of rights appear annexed to the constitutions of the other States, except the foregoing, and their constitutions are equally silent. I am told, however that one or two States have bills of rights which do not appear in this collection; but that those also recognize the right of the legislative authority in this respect.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1 该事项的陈述摘自各州宪法印刷集。宾夕法尼亚州和北卡罗来纳州是两个包含以下禁令的州：“由于和平时期的常备军对自由构成危险，因此不应保留它们。”事实上，这与其说是禁止，不如说是警告。新罕布什尔州、马萨诸塞州、特拉华州和马里兰州在其各自的权利法案中都有这样的条款：“常备军对自由构成危险，未经立法机关同意不得征集或维持”；这是对立法机关权威的正式承认。纽约没有权利法案，她的宪法也没有提及此事。除上述内容外，其他州的宪法中似乎没有附加任何权利法案，而且它们的宪法也同样保持沉默。然而，有人告诉我，有一两个州制定了权利法案，但没有出现在这本汇编中；但这些也承认立法机关在这方面的权利。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE TWO POTS

paragraphs.text: Two Pots, one of brass and the other of clay, stood together on the hearthstone. One day the Brass Pot proposed to the Earthen Pot that they go out into the world together. But the Earthen Pot excused himself, saying that it would be wiser for him to stay in the corner by the fire.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两个锅子，一个是黄铜的，另一个是粘土的，并排放在炉石上。有一天，铜锅向土锅提议，他们一起出去闯荡世界。但土锅却原谅了自己，说他最好呆在火边的角落里。

paragraphs.text: "It would take so little to break me," he said. "You know how fragile I am. The least shock is sure to shatter me!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “只要一点点就能让我崩溃，”他说。 “你知道我有多脆弱，一点点的打击都会让我崩溃！”

paragraphs.text: "Don't let that keep you at home," urged the Brass Pot. "I shall take very good care of you. If we should happen to meet anything hard I will step between and save you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “别让它让你呆在家里，”黄铜锅敦促道。 “我会好好照顾你的。如果我们遇到什么困难，我会挺身而出，救你。”

paragraphs.text: So the Earthen Pot at last consented, and the two set out side by side, jolting along on three stubby legs first to this side, then to that, and bumping into each other at every step.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 土锅终于同意了，两人并肩出发，三只粗短的腿摇摇晃晃地先到这边，再到那边，每一步都撞在一起。

paragraphs.text: The Earthen Pot could not survive that sort of companionship very long. They had not gone ten paces before the Earthen Pot cracked, and at the next jolt he flew into a thousand pieces.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 土锅无法在这种陪伴下存活太久。还没走出十步，土锅就裂开了，下一次摇晃，土锅就被摔成了一千块。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: LVI. TATTERCOATS

paragraphs.text: _Source._--Told to Mrs. Balfour by a little girl named Sally Brown, when she lived in the Cars in Lincolnshire. Sally had got it from her mother, who worked for Mrs. Balfour. It was originally told in dialect, which Mrs. Balfour has omitted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: _来源._-贝尔福夫人住在林肯郡的汽车里时，一位名叫莎莉·布朗的小女孩告诉她。莎莉是从她为贝尔福夫人工作的母亲那里得到的。它最初是用方言讲述的，但贝尔福夫人省略了。

paragraphs.text: _Parallels._--Miss Cox has included "Tattercoats" in her exhaustive collection of parallels of _Cinderella_ (Folk-Lore Society Publications, 1892), No. 274 from the MS. which I had lent her. Miss Cox rightly classes it as "Indeterminate," and it has only the _Menial Heroine_ and _Happy Marriage_ episodes in common with stories of the Cinderella type.
paragraphs.translation_zh: _Parallels._--考克斯小姐将“Tattercoats”收录在她详尽的《灰姑娘》平行集（民间传说协会出版物，1892 年）中，来自 MS 第 274 期。这是我借给她的。考克斯小姐正确地将其归类为“不确定”，并且它只有_卑微女主角_和_幸福婚姻_情节与灰姑娘类型的故事有共同之处。

paragraphs.text: _Remarks._--_Tattercoats_ is of interest chiefly as being without any "fairy" or supernatural elements, unless the magic pipe can be so considered; it certainly gives the tale a fairy-like element. It is practically a prose variant of _King Cophetua and the Beggar Maid_, and is thus an instance of the folk-novel pure and simple, without any admixture of those unnatural incidents which transform the folk-novel into the serious folk-tale as we are accustomed to have it. Which is the prior, folk-novel or tale, it would be hard to say.
paragraphs.translation_zh: _备注._--_Tattercoats_ 之所以有趣，主要是因为它没有任何“仙女”或超自然元素，除非可以考虑魔笛；它确实给这个故事带来了童话般的元素。它实际上是《科菲图亚国王和乞丐女仆》的散文变体，因此是纯粹而简单的民间小说的一个例子，没有掺杂任何将民间小说转变为我们所习惯的严肃民间故事的非自然事件。哪一个是先有的，是民间小说还是故事，很难说。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Biography. Alice Cary (1820-1871), an American poet, was born in Cincinnati. She and her sister, Phoebe, wrote many beautiful poems and sketches. They removed to New York City and lived together there. "November" is one of Alice Cary's most widely known poems.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 传。爱丽丝·卡里（Alice Cary，1820-1871），美国诗人，出生于辛辛那提。她和她的妹妹菲比写了许多美丽的诗歌和素描。他们搬到纽约市并在那里住在一起。 《十一月》是爱丽丝·卡里最广为人知的诗歌之一。

paragraphs.text: Discussion. 1. What signs of autumn are mentioned in the first stanza? 2. What signs of the coming winter are mentioned in the second stanza? 3. Where have the birds gone? 4. What is meant by the word "here" in line 4, above? 5. Why are the brooks "dry and dumb" in November? 6. Is this true in all parts of the country? 7. What are we told about the spring in "October's Bright Blue Weather"? 8. What will happen when the winter is over? 9. Where does the swallow build his nest? 10. What wonder of Nature, about which you read in A Forward Look, above, does the second stanza tell you? 11. How can the snow help keep the roots alive? 12. In what stanza is this thought repeated? 13. Find in the Glossary the meaning of: fading; quail; eaves.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论。 1.第一节提到了秋天的哪些迹象？ 2.第二节提到了冬天即将来临的什么征兆？ 3. 鸟儿去哪儿了？ 4. 上面第 4 行中的“这里”一词是什么意思？ 5. 为什么11月的小溪“干涸、无水”？ 6、全国各地都是这样吗？ 7. 《十月碧蓝的天气》讲述了哪些关于春天的故事？ 8. 冬天结束后会发生什么？ 9. 燕子在哪里筑巢？ 10. 您在上文《展望》中读到的有关自然奇迹的第二节告诉您什么？ 11. 雪如何帮助根部保持活力？ 12. 这个想法在哪一节中重复出现？ 13. 在术语表中找到以下含义： 褪色； 褪色；鹌鹑;檐。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Discussion. 1. Read lines that tell Iagoo's story of adventures. 2. Where do you think he had seen these things? 3. What was the "bitter" water Iagoo told about? 4. What were the "lightning" and the "thunder" that came from the "canoe with pinions"? 5. Why was his story laughed at as false by the Indians? 6. How did Hiawatha know it was all true? 7. How did Hiawatha say they should receive the White Man when he came? 8. What secrets came to Hiawatha in the vision? 9. What "darker vision" did he see? 10. Has Hiawatha's vision come true? 11. What do you think of Hiawatha's character? 12. Which of all the stories in this poem do you like best? 13. Give the reason for your answer. 14. You no doubt enjoyed reading this poem; can you tell why? 15. Read "A Forward Look," and tell why you think Longfellow was a real author. 16. You will enjoy reading Eastman's Indian Legends Retold. 17. Find in the Glossary the meaning of: tittered; hither; counsels. 18. Pronounce: pinions; derision; vision; regions; vague; warring.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论。 1. 阅读讲述 Iagoo 冒险故事的台词。 2.你认为他在哪里见过这些东西？ 3. Iagoo 所说的“苦”水是什么？ 4.“带小齿轮的独木舟”发出的“闪电”和“雷声”是什么？ 5. 为什么他的故事被印第安人嘲笑为虚假？ 6. 海华沙怎么知道这一切都是真的？ 7. 当白人到来时，海华沙说他们应该如何接待他？ 8. 海华沙在异象中发现了什么秘密？ 9. 他看到了什么“更黑暗的景象”？ 10. 海华沙的愿景实现了吗？ 11.你觉得海华沙这个角色怎么样？ 12.这首诗中的所有故事中，你最喜欢哪个？ 13. 给出答案的理由。 14.你无疑喜欢读这首诗；你能告诉我为什么吗？ 15. 阅读《展望》，并说出为什么你认为朗费罗是一位真正的作家。 16. 你会喜欢阅读伊士曼的《印第安传奇重述》。 17. 在词汇表中找到以下含义： 窃笑；窃笑；到这里；忠告。 18. 发音：小齿轮；嘲笑；想象;地区；模糊的;交战。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Science of Human Nature: TO THE STUDENT

paragraphs.text: You are beginning the study of human nature. You can not study human nature from a book, you must study yourself and your neighbors. This book may help you to know what to look for and to understand what you find, but it can do little more than this. It is true, this text gives you many facts learned by psychologists, but you must verify the statements, or at least see their significance to _you_, or they will be of no worth to you. However, the facts considered here, properly understood and assimilated, ought to prove of great value to you. But perhaps of greater value will be the psychological frame of mind or attitude which you should acquire. The psychological attitude is that of seeking to find and understand the _causes of human action, and the causes, consequences, and significance of the processes of the human mind_. If your first course in psychology teaches you to look for these things, gives you some skill in finding them and in using the knowledge after you have it, your study should be quite worth while.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你正在开始研究人性。你不能从书本上研究人性，你必须研究你自己和你的邻居。这本书可以帮助你知道要寻找什么并理解你发现了什么，但它能做的也仅此而已。确实，本文为您提供了心理学家学到的许多事实，但您必须验证这些陈述，或者至少看到它们对您的意义，否则它们对您来说毫无价值。然而，这里考虑的事实，如果正确理解和吸收，应该对你有很大的价值。但也许更有价值的是你应该获得的心理心态或态度。心理学态度是寻求并理解“人类行为的原因，以及人类思维过程的原因、后果和意义”。如果你的第一门心理学课程教你寻找这些东西，给你一些寻找它们的技巧，并在你掌握这些知识后使用这些知识，那么你的学习应该是非常值得的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fourth Reader: The Alexandra Readers: SAINT CHRISTOPHER

paragraphs.text: For many a year Saint Christopher Served God in many a land; And master painters drew his face, With loving heart and hand, On altar fronts and churches' walls; And peasants used to say,-- To look on good Saint Christopher Brought luck for all the day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 多年来，圣克里斯托弗在许多地方侍奉上帝；画家们用爱心和双手，在祭坛前和教堂的墙壁上画下了他的脸；农民们常说：—— 看着善良的圣克里斯托弗会带来一整天的好运。

paragraphs.text: For many a year, in lowly hut, The giant dwelt content Upon the bank, and back and forth Across the stream he went; And on his giant shoulders bore All travellers who came, By night, by day, or rich or poor, All in King Jesus' name.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多年来，巨人在简陋的小屋里，心满意足地住在岸边，在小溪上来来回回；他巨大的肩膀上承载着所有前来的旅行者，无论白天黑夜，无论贫富，都是奉耶稣王的名。

paragraphs.text: But much he doubted if the King His work would note or know, And often with a weary heart He waded to and fro. One night, as wrapped in sleep he lay, He sudden heard a call,-- "O Christopher, come, carry me!" He sprang, looked out, but all
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但他很怀疑国王是否会注意到或知道他的工作，他常常怀着疲倦的心来回走动。一天晚上，当他熟睡时，他突然听到一声呼唤，——“噢，克里斯托弗，来抱我吧！”他跳了起来，向外张望，但一切

paragraphs.text: Was dark and silent on the shore. "It must be that I dreamed," He said, and laid him down again; But instantly there seemed Again the feeble, distant cry,-- "Oh, come and carry me!" Again he sprang and looked; again No living thing could see.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 岸边一片漆黑，寂静无声。 “一定是我做梦了，”他说着，又把他放了下来。但立刻又传来远处微弱的呼喊声：“噢，来抱我吧！”他再次跳了起来，四处张望。又没有生物能看见。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Stories to Tell to Children: THE BOY WHO CRIED "WOLF!"

paragraphs.text: There was once a shepherd-boy who kept his flock at a little distance from the village. Once he thought he would play a trick on the villagers and have some fun at their expense. So he ran toward the village crying out, with all his might,--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一个牧童，他的羊群离村子有一段距离。有一次，他想捉弄一下村民，以牺牲他们的利益为乐。于是他拼命向村子跑去，大声喊道——

paragraphs.text: The kind villagers left their work and ran to the field to help him. But when they got there the boy laughed at them for their pains; there was no wolf there.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 好心的村民放下手中的活，跑到地里去帮助他。但当他们到达那里时，男孩嘲笑他们的痛苦。那里没有狼。

paragraphs.text: Still another day the boy tried the same trick, and the villagers came running to help and got laughed at again. Then one day a wolf did break into the fold and began killing the lambs. In great fright, the boy ran for help. "Wolf! Wolf!" he screamed. "There is a wolf in the flock! Help!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 又有一天，男孩又玩了同样的把戏，村民们跑来帮忙，又被嘲笑了。有一天，一只狼闯进了羊圈，开始杀死羔羊。男孩吓坏了，跑去寻求帮助。 “狼！狼！”他尖叫道。 “羊群里有狼！救命！”

paragraphs.text: The villagers heard him, but they thought it was another mean trick; no one paid the least attention, or went near him. And the shepherd-boy lost all his sheep.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 村民们听到了，但认为这又是卑鄙的把戏。没有人注意到他，也没有人靠近他。牧童失去了所有的羊。

paragraphs.text: That is the kind of thing that happens to people who lie: even when they tell the truth no one believes them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这就是发生在撒谎的人身上的事情：即使他们说真话，也没有人相信他们。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Science of Human Nature: AUTHOR'S PREFACE

paragraphs.text: This book is written for young students in high schools and normal schools. No knowledge can be of more use to a young person than a knowledge of himself; no study can be more valuable to him than a study of himself. A study of the laws of human behavior,--that is the purpose of this book.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 本书是为高中和师范学校的年轻学生而写的。对于年轻人来说，没有什么知识比了解自己更有用了。对他来说，没有什么研究比研究他自己更有价值了。研究人类行为规律——这就是本书的目的。

paragraphs.text: What is human nature like? Why do we act as we do? How can we make ourselves different? How can we make others different? How can we make ourselves more efficient? How can we make our lives more worth while? This book is a manual intended to help young people to obtain such knowledge of human nature as will enable them to answer these questions.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 人性是什么样的？我们为什么要这样做？我们怎样才能让自己与众不同？我们怎样才能让别人与众不同？我们怎样才能让自己更有效率呢？我们怎样才能让自己的人生更有价值？本书是一本手册，旨在帮助年轻人获得有关人性的知识，从而使他们能够回答这些问题。

paragraphs.text: I have not attempted to write a complete text on psychology. There are already many such books, and good ones too. I have selected for treatment only such topics as young students can study with interest and profit. I have tried to keep in mind all the time the practical worth of the matters discussed, and the ability and experience of the intended readers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我并没有试图写一篇完整的心理学文章。这类书已经有很多了，而且也不错。我只选择了年轻学生能够有兴趣和受益的学习主题。我一直努力牢记所讨论问题的实际价值以及目标读者的能力和经验。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Science of Human Nature: FREE ASSOCIATION

paragraphs.text: =Object.= This test determines the speed of the free flow of ideas. The result of the test is a criterion of the quickness of the flow of ideas when no restriction or limitation is put on this flow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 目的： 该测试决定了思想自由流动的速度。当没有对思想流动施加任何限制或限制时，测试的结果是思想流动速度的标准。

paragraphs.text: =Method.= The procedure in this test is to give the pupils a word, and tell them to write this word down and all the other words that come into their minds. Make it clear to them that they are to write whatever word comes to mind, whether it has any relation to the word that is given them or not. Start them with the word "cloud." Give the following instructions:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 方法： 该测试的程序是给学生一个单词，并告诉他们写下这个单词以及他们想到的所有其他单词。让他们清楚地知道，他们要写下想到的任何单词，无论它与给他们的单词是否有任何关系。以“云”一词开头。给出以下指示：

paragraphs.text: I wish to see how many words you can think of and write down in three minutes. I shall name a word, you may write it down and then all the other words that come into your minds. Do not write sentences, merely the words that come into your minds. Work as fast as you can.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我想看看你在三分钟内能想到并写下多少单词。我将命名一个词，你可以把它写下来，然后写下你想到的所有其他词。不要写出句子，只写出你脑海中出现的单词。尽可能快地工作。

paragraphs.text: =Results.= Score the work by counting the number of words that have been written. The norms are shown in Figure XIII.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 结果： 通过计算已写的字数来对作业进行评分。标准如图十三所示。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 2. THE PERIOD OF EARLY CHILDHOOD

paragraphs.text: This period is from three to six years. It begins in an interest in live things, in domestic animals, and later in flowers, wind, rain, stars, and other expressions of nature. The child now finds delight in picture-books, short stories of animals, birds, and flowers. When a little older he enjoys fables, short fairy stories, and folk and wonder-tales, short moral stories and imaginative stories of home, play, and humor. Historic tales of the nation and Bible stories, well adapted and simplified in language, will prove of the greatest interest to children of this early period. No hard and fast lines can be drawn in ages. Allowance must always be made for temperament, disposition, heredity, and family environment. I have found little children, under three years of age, reproducing to me, without having previously seen me, or hearing them from me, several of the fairy stories and fables in this volume; and I have found boys and girls nine and ten years old still enjoying them. But with the average child such short fairy and folk-tales are keenly enjoyed between the ages of three and six years.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个时期是三到六年。它始于对生物、家畜的兴趣，后来对花朵、风、雨、星星和其他自然表现形式的兴趣。孩子现在喜欢看图画书、动物、鸟类和花卉的短篇故事。当年龄稍大一些时，他喜欢寓言、短篇童话故事、民间故事和奇幻故事、短篇道德故事以及关于家庭、游戏和幽默的富有想象力的故事。国家的历史故事和圣经故事，经过精心改编和语言简化，将成为这个早期阶段的孩子们最感兴趣的。多年来无法划定硬性界限。必须始终考虑到气质、性格、遗传和家庭环境。我发现三岁以下的小孩子正在向我复述本书中的一些童话故事和寓言，而他们之前并没有见过我，也没有听过我的说法。我发现九岁和十岁的男孩和女孩仍然喜欢它们。但对于一般的孩子来说，这种短篇童话和民间故事在三岁到六岁之间是非常受欢迎的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE RAT AND THE ELEPHANT

paragraphs.text: A Rat was traveling along the King's highway. He was a very proud Rat, considering his small size and the bad reputation all Rats have. As Mr. Rat walked along--he kept mostly to the ditch--he noticed a great commotion up the road, and soon a grand procession came in view. It was the King and his retinue.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只老鼠沿着国王的高速公路行驶。考虑到他的个头小以及所有属鼠人都享有的坏名声，他是一只非常骄傲的属鼠人。当老鼠先生沿着沟渠走时，他注意到路上有一阵骚动，很快，一支盛大的游行队伍就出现在了视野中。那是国王和他的随从。

paragraphs.text: The King rode on a huge Elephant adorned with the most gorgeous trappings. With the King in his luxurious howdah were the royal Dog and Cat. A great crowd of people followed the procession. They were so taken up with admiration of the Elephant, that the Rat was not noticed. His pride was hurt.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王骑着一头巨大的大象，大象身上装饰着最华丽的服饰。与国王一起乘坐豪华豪车的是皇家狗和猫。一大群人跟随在游行队伍后面。他们全神贯注于对大象的钦佩，以至于没有注意到老鼠。他的自尊心受到了伤害。

paragraphs.text: "What fools!" he cried. "Look at me, and you will soon forget that clumsy Elephant! Is it his great size that makes your eyes pop out? Or is it his wrinkled hide? Why, I have eyes and ears and as many legs as he! I am of just as much importance, and"--
paragraphs.translation_zh: “真是一群蠢货！”他哭了。 “看着我，你很快就会忘记那只笨拙的大象！是他巨大的体型让你的眼睛凸出来吗？还是他皱巴巴的皮？为什么，我有眼睛、耳朵和和他一样多的腿！我同样重要，而且”——

paragraphs.text: But just then the royal Cat spied him, and the next instant, the Rat knew he was _not_ quite so important as an Elephant.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但就在那时，皇家猫发现了他，下一瞬间，老鼠就知道他并不像大象那么重要。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: A MAN OF WORDS

paragraphs.text: A man of words and not of deeds Is like a garden full of weeds; And when the weeds begin to grow, It's like a garden full of snow; And when the snow begins to fall, It's like a bird upon the wall; And when the bird away does fly, It's like an eagle in the sky; And when the sky begins to roar, It's like a lion at the door; And when the door begins to crack, It's like a stick across your back; And when your back begins to smart, It's like a penknife in your heart; And when your heart begins to bleed, You're dead, and dead, and dead, indeed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 光说而不做的人，就像长满杂草的花园；当杂草开始生长时，它就像一个充满雪的花园；当雪开始飘落时，它就像墙上的一只鸟；当鸟儿飞翔时，它就像天空中的雄鹰；当天空开始咆哮时，它就像门口的狮子；当门开始破裂时，它就像一根棍子横在你的背上；当你的背开始发痛时，它就像一把小刀插在你的心里；当你的心开始流血时，你就死了，死了，真的死了。

paragraphs.text: The first stanza of this jingle was long attributed to Longfellow as an impromptu made on one of his children. He took occasion to deny this, as well as the authorship of the almost equally famous "Mr. Finney had a turnip." The last two stanzas bear evidence of a more sophisticated origin than that of real nursery rhymes. Mr. Lucas, in his _Book of Verses for Children_, gives two different versions of these stanzas.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这首顺口溜的第一节长期以来被认为是朗费罗为他的一个孩子即兴创作的。他借机否认了这一点，并否认了几乎同样著名的“芬尼先生有一颗萝卜”的作者身份。最后两节的证据表明其起源比真正的童谣更为复杂。卢卡斯先生在他的《儿童诗集》中给出了这些诗节的两个不同版本。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: I have a little shadow that goes in and out with me, And what can be the use of him is more than I can see. He is very, very like me from the heels up to the head; And I see him jump before me, when I jump into my bed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我有一个小影子，随我进出，他的用处比我想象的还要多。他从头到脚跟我都非常非常像。当我跳上床时，我看到他在我面前跳。

paragraphs.text: The funniest thing about him is the way he likes to grow-- Not at all like proper children, which is always very slow; For he sometimes shoots up taller like an india-rubber ball, And he sometimes gets so little that there's none of him at all.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他最有趣的是他喜欢成长的方式——一点也不像正常的孩子，总是很慢；因为他有时会像印度橡皮球一样弹得更高，有时他得到的却很少，以至于根本没有他。

paragraphs.text: He hasn't got a notion of how children ought to play, And can only make a fool of me in every sort of way. He stays so close beside me, he's a coward you can see; I'd think shame to stick to nursie as that shadow sticks to me!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他不知道孩子们应该如何玩耍，只能用各种方式来愚弄我。他离我那么近，你看得出他是个胆小鬼；当那个影子粘在我身上时，我会觉得粘着护士是一种耻辱！

paragraphs.text: One morning, very early, before the sun was up, I rose and found the shining dew on every buttercup; But my lazy little shadow, like an arrant sleepy-head, Had stayed at home behind me and was fast asleep in bed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天早上，很早，太阳还没升起，我就起身，发现每一朵毛茛上都挂着闪亮的露珠；但我那懒惰的小影子，像个彻头彻尾的瞌睡虫，一直待在我身后的家里，在床上睡着了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Yellow Fairy Book: By Sir George Webbe Dasent

paragraphs.text: One day the Bear met the Fox, who came slinking along with a string of fish he had stolen.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，熊遇到了狐狸，狐狸带着他偷来的一串鱼偷偷溜了过来。

paragraphs.text: So the Bear had a mind to learn to fish too, and bade the Fox tell him how he was to set about it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是，熊也想学钓鱼，并吩咐狐狸告诉他该怎么做。

paragraphs.text: "Oh! It's an easy craft for you", answered the Fox, "and soon learned. You've only got to go upon the ice, and cut a hole and stick your tail down into it; and so you must go on holding it there as long as you can. You're not to mind if your tail smarts a little; that's when the fish bite. The longer you hold it there the more fish you'll get; and then all at once out with it, with a cross pull sideways, and with a strong pull too."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦！这对你来说很容易，”狐狸回答道，“很快就学会了。你只需要在冰上走下去，挖一个洞，把你的尾巴伸进去；所以你必须尽可能长时间地把它固定在那里。如果你的尾巴有点刺痛，你不用介意；那是鱼咬钩的时候。你把它固定在那里的时间越长，你就会得到越多的鱼；然后立刻用它，用横向拉力，用力拉。也是。”

paragraphs.text: Yes; the Bear did as the Fox had said, and held his tail a long, long time down in the hole, till it was fast frozen in. Then he pulled it out with a cross pull, and it snapped short off. That's why Bruin goes about with a stumpy tail this very day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 是的;熊按照狐狸的话做了，把尾巴在洞里扎了很长很长一段时间，直到它很快被冻住。然后他用力一拉，把尾巴拉了出来，尾巴很快就折断了。这就是为什么棕熊今天会带着粗短的尾巴到处走。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FARMER AND HIS SONS

paragraphs.text: A rich old farmer, who felt that he had not many more days to live, called his sons to his bedside.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位富有的老农觉得自己的日子不多了，就把儿子们叫到床边。

paragraphs.text: "My sons," he said, "heed what I have to say to you. Do not on any account part with the estate that has belonged to our family for so many generations. Somewhere on it is hidden a rich treasure. I do not know the exact spot, but it is there, and you will surely find it. Spare no energy and leave no spot unturned in your search."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我的儿子们，”他说，“请听我对你们说的话。千万不要放弃我们家族世代相传的庄园。在它的某个地方藏着丰富的宝藏。我不知道确切的地点，但它就在那里，你们一定会找到它。不遗余力，不遗余力地寻找。”

paragraphs.text: The father died, and no sooner was he in his grave than the sons set to work digging with all their might, turning up every foot of ground with their spades, and going over the whole farm two or three times.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 父亲去世了，他一入坟墓，儿子们就开始使出浑身解数挖地，用铁锹把每一英尺的地都翻出来，把整个农场都翻了两三遍。

paragraphs.text: No hidden gold did they find; but at harvest time when they had settled their accounts and had pocketed a rich profit far greater than that of any of their neighbors, they understood that the treasure their father had told them about was the wealth of a bountiful crop, and that in their industry had they found the treasure.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们没有找到隐藏的金子；但到了收割的季节，当他们结清账目，获得了远远超过邻居的丰厚利润时，他们才明白，父亲告诉他们的宝藏是五谷丰登的财富，他们是在自己的勤劳中找到了宝藏。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE LION AND THE MOUSE

paragraphs.text: A Lion lay asleep in the forest, his great head resting on his paws. A timid little Mouse came upon him unexpectedly, and in her fright and haste to get away, ran across the Lion's nose. Roused from his nap, the Lion laid his huge paw angrily on the tiny creature to kill her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狮子在森林里睡着了，他的大头枕在爪子上。一只胆怯的小老鼠出乎意料地向他袭来，她惊慌失措，急忙逃跑，跑到了狮子的鼻子上。狮子从午睡中醒来，愤怒地将巨大的爪子放在这个小生物身上，想要杀死她。

paragraphs.text: "Spare me!" begged the poor Mouse. "Please let me go and some day I will surely repay you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “饶了我吧！”可怜的老鼠哀求道。 “请放过我吧，总有一天我一定会报答你的。”

paragraphs.text: The Lion was much amused to think that a Mouse could ever help him. But he was generous and finally let the Mouse go.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狮子想到老鼠能帮助他，感到非常好笑。但他很慷慨，最终放走了老鼠。

paragraphs.text: Some days later, while stalking his prey in the forest, the Lion was caught in the toils of a hunter's net. Unable to free himself, he filled the forest with his angry roaring. The Mouse knew the voice and quickly found the Lion struggling in the net. Running to one of the great ropes that bound him, she gnawed it until it parted, and soon the Lion was free.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几天后，当狮子在森林里追踪猎物时，被猎人的网缠住了。他无法挣脱，愤怒的咆哮充满了森林。老鼠认出了这个声音，很快就发现了在网中挣扎的狮子。她跑到一根绑住他的大绳子旁，咬住它直到它分开，很快狮子就自由了。

paragraphs.text: "You laughed when I said I would repay you," said the Mouse. "Now you see that even a Mouse can help a Lion."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当我说我会报答你时，你笑了。”老鼠说。 “现在你知道了，即使是老鼠也能帮助狮子。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: 1. Make a list of the things in everyday life which we take for granted as necessities which the earliest men had to learn how to make.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 列出日常生活中我们认为理所当然的必需品，而最早的人类必须学习如何制作这些东西。

paragraphs.text: 2. What was the earliest important discovery made by man? Do you think this was as important as the discovery of electricity? Why? Name any inventions that have come into common use within your own or your parents' lifetime.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 人类最早的重要发现是什么？您认为这与电的发现一样重要吗？为什么？列出您或您父母一生中普遍使用的任何发明。

paragraphs.text: 3. Before man discovered fire, what did he eat? Mention two steps by which he came to have better food to eat.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 3. 人类发现火之前吃什么？说出他为了吃到更好的食物所采取的两个步骤。

paragraphs.text: 4. Mention in order five kinds of dwellings which the early men lived in, and three kinds of clothing which they wore.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 4. 按顺序列出早期人类居住的五种住宅以及他们所穿的三种服装。

paragraphs.text: 7. From what source did each article of food on your dinner table to-day come? How many people had something to do with this food before it reached you?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 7. 你今天餐桌上的每样食物都来自哪里？在这食物到达你手中之前，有多少人与它有过关系？

paragraphs.text: 8. Compare the clothing of people to-day with that of primitive man. Are we more or less dependent on others for food and clothing than primitive man?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 8. 将现代人的服装与原始人的服装进行比较。与原始人相比，我们在衣食上是更多还是更少地依赖他人？

paragraphs.text: 9. We are still making new words. Make a list of words that have come into use since the World War began.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 9. 我们还在创造新词。列出自第一次世界大战开始以来使用过的单词。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 20. THE MONKEY AND THE CATS

paragraphs.text: Two Cats who had stolen a large piece of cheese were quarreling over dividing it. At last they decided to refer the matter to a Monkey, who took a pair of scales and, breaking the cheese into two pieces, placed a piece in each scale. "Let me see," he said, taking out the heavier piece, "this piece weighs more than the other." Then he bit off quite a piece and put it back on the scale, and, of course, it was lighter than the other piece. So he took a mouthful from that side, and continued taking from first one side and then the other, until the Cats cried, "Hold! Hold! Give us the two pieces and we will be satisfied."
