paragraphs.text: At Dalton, near Thirsk, in Yorkshire, there is a mill. It has quite recently been rebuilt; but when I was at Dalton, six years ago, the old building stood. In front of the house was a long mound which went by the name of "the giant's grave," and in the mill you can see a long blade of iron something like a scythe-blade, but not curved, which was called "the giant's knife," because of a very curious story which is told of this knife. Would you like to hear it? Well, it isn't very long.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在约克郡瑟斯克附近的道尔顿，有一家磨坊。它最近才被重建；但六年前，当我在道尔顿时，那座老建筑依然矗立着。房子前面有一个长长的土堆，名字叫“巨人坟墓”，在磨坊里你可以看到一把长长的铁刀，类似镰刀刀刃，但不是弯曲的，被称为“巨人刀”，因为这把刀有一个非常奇怪的故事。你想听听吗？嗯，时间不是很长。

paragraphs.text: There once lived a giant at this mill who had only one eye in the middle of his forehead, and he ground men's bones to make his bread. One day he captured on Pilmoor a lad named Jack, and instead of grinding him in the mill he kept him grinding as his servant, and never let him get away. Jack served the giant seven years, and never was allowed a holiday the whole time. At last he could bear it no longer. Topcliffe fair was coming on, and Jack begged that he might be allowed to go there.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个磨坊里曾经住着一个巨人，他额头中间只有一只眼睛，他把人的骨头磨碎来制作面包。有一天，他在皮尔莫尔捕获了一个名叫杰克的小伙子，并没有把他放在磨坊里磨碎，而是让他作为仆人磨碎，并且从不让他逃脱。杰克为巨人服务了七年，自始至终都没有被允许休假。终于，他再也无法忍受了。托普克利夫集市即将举行，杰克请求允许他去那里。

paragraphs.text: "I've been grinding and grinding these seven years," said Jack, "and not a holiday have I had. I'll have one now, whatever you say."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “这七年来我一直在磨磨蹭蹭，”杰克说，“我没有休过假。不管你说什么，我现在就休个假。”

paragraphs.text: Well, the day was hot, and after dinner the giant lay down in the mill with his head on a sack and dozed. He had been eating in the mill, and had laid down a great loaf of bone bread by his side, and the knife I told you about was in his hand, but his fingers relaxed their hold of it in sleep. Jack seized the knife, and holding it with both his hands drove the blade into the single eye of the giant, who woke with a howl of agony, and starting up, barred the door. Jack was again in difficulties, for he couldn't get out, but he soon found a way out of them. The giant had a favourite dog, which had also been sleeping when his master was blinded. So Jack killed the dog, skinned it, and threw the hide over his back.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 天很热，晚饭后，巨人躺在磨坊里，头枕在麻袋上打瞌睡。他一直在磨坊里吃饭，并在身旁放了一大条骨面包，我告诉你的那把刀就在他手里，但他的手指在睡梦中放松了对它的握持。杰克抓住了那把刀，用双手握住它，把刀刃刺进了巨人的单眼，巨人在一声痛苦的嚎叫中醒来，猛地站起来，把门锁上了。杰克再次陷入困境，因为他无法摆脱困境，但他很快就找到了摆脱困境的方法。巨人有一只最喜欢的狗，当他的主人失明时，它也在睡觉。于是杰克杀了狗，剥了皮，然后把皮扔到了背上。

paragraphs.text: "At him, Truncheon," said the giant; "at the little wretch that I've fed these seven years, and now has blinded me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “对他来说，警棍，”巨人说道。 “我养了这个小可怜七年，现在却让我失明了。”

paragraphs.text: "Bow, wow," says Jack, and ran between the giant's legs on all-fours, barking till he got to the door. He unlatched it and was off, and never more was seen at Dalton Mill.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “鞠躬，哇，”杰克说，然后四肢着地跑到巨人的两腿之间，一直叫到门口。他打开门闩就走了，道尔顿米尔再也没有看到更多的人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 16. THE CRIPPLE AT THE BEAUTIFUL GATE

paragraphs.text: One afternoon two friends were walking along a street in Jerusalem on their way to the evening sacrifice in the temple. At one of the entrances--the Beautiful Gate (so named because of its snow-white marble steps leading up to its great door of costly brass)--sat a poor lame man, begging. His feet and ankles were so crippled that he had never been able to walk or even to stand. His friends carried this helpless cripple and laid him every morning at this temple entrance to beg charity from those who went to pray. As soon as the man saw these two friends, Peter and John, he cried piteously, "Give charity!" Standing still and looking him quietly in the eye, the two friends said, "Look on us!" He looked up at once most expectantly. Peter said, "Silver and gold have I none, but what I have, I give thee. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk!" Taking him by the hand Peter lifted him up, and immediately his feet and ankle-bones became strong. The man leaped up and went into the temple with Peter and John, walking and leaping and praising God. All the people were amazed, seeing him leaping and hearing his shouts of joy as he held fast to his two friends. A great throng gathered about them in the large open court, called Solomon's porch. Peter, seeing the throng, began to tell them about the wonderful Prince of Life, Jesus, whom they had put to death. Such preaching within the temple courts aroused the people and offended the priests, and the chief officer seized Peter and John and cast them into prison. This caused hundreds of the people to declare themselves Christians. The next morning when Peter and John were brought before the council and questioned, the officers said, "We will let you go if you will promise not to speak or teach in this name again." They answered: "Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you, rather than unto God, judge ye; for we cannot but speak the things we have seen and heard." So, failing to frighten them, the officers were compelled to let them go. And being let go, they returned at once to the Christian company, and told what the Jewish officers had said to them. And the cripple who had been healed at the Beautiful Gate was a happy Christian in that company, and was one of the loudest in giving true praise to God that day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天下午，两个朋友沿着耶路撒冷的一条街道行走，前往圣殿参加晚祭。在其中一个入口——美丽之门（因其雪白的大理石台阶通向昂贵的黄铜大门而得名）——坐着一个可怜的跛子，正在乞讨。他的脚和脚踝严重残疾，无法行走，甚至无法站立。他的朋友们每天早上都会抬着这个无助的瘸子，把他放在这座寺庙的入口处，向那些去祈祷的人乞求施舍。那人一看到彼得和约翰这两个朋友，就可怜兮兮地喊道：“施舍吧！”两个朋友静静地站着，静静地看着他的眼睛，说道：“看着我们！”他立刻满怀期待地抬起头来。彼得说：“金银我都没有，但我把我所有的都给你。奉拿撒勒人耶稣基督的名，你行走吧！”彼得拉着他的手把他举了起来，他的脚和踝骨立刻变得坚强起来。那人就跳起来，和彼得、约翰一起走进圣殿，边走边跳，赞美神。所有人都惊讶不已，看到他跳跃着，听到他紧紧抓住两个朋友的欢呼声。在称为所罗门门廊的大露天庭院里，一大群人聚集在他们周围。彼得看到这群人，就开始向他们讲述奇妙的生命之君耶稣，他被他们处死了。圣殿院内的这种讲道激怒了民众，并冒犯了祭司，大官抓住了彼得和约翰，将他们投入监狱。这导致数百人宣布自己是基督徒。第二天早上，当彼得和约翰被带到公会面前接受审问时，军官们说：“如果你们保证不再以这个名字说话或教导，我们就释放你们。”他们回答说：“在上帝看来，听从你们而不是听从上帝是否正确，你们自己判断吧；因为我们不能不说出我们所看到和听到的事情。”于是，警察们没能吓住他们，只得将他们放了。被释放后，他们立即回到基督徒队伍中，并告诉了犹太军官对他们所说的话。在美丽之门被治愈的瘸子是那群人中一位快乐的基督徒，并且是那天最大声地真正赞美上帝的人之一。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: FRANKLIN WRITES FOR THE NEWSPAPER

paragraphs.text: Here is Benjamin Franklin's own account of his first attempt at writing. You see, he did not have a chance to learn to write, as you do, by practice in composition in school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 以下是本杰明·富兰克林对他第一次写作尝试的描述。你看，他没有机会像你一样，在学校里通过作文练习来学习写作。

paragraphs.text: My brother had, in 1720 or 1721, begun to print a newspaper. It was the second that appeared in America, and was called the "New England Courant". The only one before it was the "Boston News-Letter". I remember his being _dissuaded_ by some of his friends from the undertaking, as not likely to succeed, one newspaper being, in their judgment, enough for America. At this time (1771) there are not less than five and twenty. He went on, however, with the undertaking; and after having worked in composing the types and printing off the sheets, I was employed to carry the papers through the streets to the customers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我的兄弟在 1720 年或 1721 年开始印刷报纸。这是美国第二份报纸，被称为“新英格兰新闻报”。之前唯一的一份是《波士顿新闻通讯》。我记得他的一些朋友劝阻他放弃这项事业，因为不太可能成功，在他们看来，一份报纸对美国来说就足够了。此时（1771年）不少于五、二十人。然而，他还是继续了他的事业。在完成排版和打印纸张后，我受雇将纸张穿过街道运送给顾客。

paragraphs.text: He had some ingenious men among his friends, who amused themselves by writing little pieces for this paper, which gained it credit and made it more in demand; and these gentlemen often visited us. Hearing their conversations, and their accounts of the _approbation_ their papers were received with, I was excited to try my hand among them; but being still a boy, and suspecting that my brother would object to printing anything of mine in his paper if he knew it to be mine, I contrived to _disguise_ my hand; and, writing an _anonymous_ paper, I put it in at night under the door of the printing-house. It was found in the morning and communicated to his writing friends when they called in as usual. They read it, and commented on it in my hearing; and I had the _exquisite_ pleasure of finding it met with their approbation, and that in their different guesses at the author, none were named but men of some _character_ among us for learning and _ingenuity_. I suppose now that I was rather lucky in my judges and that perhaps they were not really so very good ones as I then _esteemed_ them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的朋友中有一些聪明人，他们通过为这份报纸写一些小文章来取乐，这为这份报纸赢得了声誉，并使其更受欢迎。这些先生们经常来拜访我们。听到他们的谈话，以及他们对论文获得认可的描述，我很高兴能在他们中间尝试一下。但当时我还是个孩子，我怀疑如果我哥哥知道我的任何东西是我的，他会反对在他的报纸上印上我的任何东西，所以我设法掩饰我的手。晚上我写了一篇匿名论文，把它放在印刷厂的门下。这是早上发现的，当他的写作朋友像往常一样打电话来时，他们就将其传达给了他们。他们读了它，并在我的听证会上发表了评论；我非常高兴地发现它得到了他们的认可，而且在他们对作者的不同猜测中，除了我们当中一些具有学识和创造力的人之外，没有人被点名。我现在想，我对我的评委们相当幸运，也许他们并不像我当时尊敬的那样好。

paragraphs.text: Encouraged, however, by this, I wrote and conveyed in the same way to the press several more papers which were equally approved; and I kept my secret till my small fund of sense for such performances was pretty well exhausted, and then I _discovered_ it, when I began to be considered a little more by my brother's acquaintance, and in a manner that did not quite please him, as he thought, probably with reason, that it tended to make me too vain.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然而，受此鼓舞，我又以同样的方式撰写并转交了几篇同样获得认可的论文；我一直保守着我的秘密，直到我对这种表演的一点理智都耗尽了，然后当我哥哥的熟人开始更多地考虑我时，我发现了它，而且以一种不太让他高兴的方式，因为他认为，可能是有道理的，这往往会让我变得太虚荣。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE HORSESHOE NAILS

paragraphs.text: "Shoe him quickly, for the king wishes to ride him to battle," said the groom who had brought him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “快给他穿鞋，国王要骑着他去打仗。”带他来的马夫说道。

paragraphs.text: "Most certainly, and very soon, too," answered the man. "The king's enemies are even now advancing, and all are ready for the fight. To- day will decide whether Richard or Henry shall be king of England."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当然，而且很快，”那人回答道。 “国王的敌人现在还在逼近，所有人都做好了战斗的准备。今天将决定理查还是亨利将成为英格兰国王。”

paragraphs.text: The smith went on with his work. From a bar of iron he made four horseshoes. These he hammered and shaped and fitted to the horse's feet. Then he began to nail them on.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 铁匠继续他的工作。他用一根铁棒制作了四个马蹄铁。他将它们锤击成型并安装到马脚上。然后他开始把它们钉上。

paragraphs.text: But after he had nailed on two shoes, he found that he had not nails enough for the other two. "I have only six nails," he said, "and it will take a little time to hammer out ten more."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但当他钉完两只鞋后，他发现自己的钉子不够钉另外两只了。 “我只有六颗钉子，”他说，“还需要一点时间才能再钉出十颗。”

paragraphs.text: "Oh, well," said the groom, "won't six nails do? Put three in each shoe. I hear the trumpets now. King Richard will be impatient."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “噢，好吧，”新郎说，“六颗钉子不行吗？在每只鞋子里放三颗。我现在听到喇叭声了。理查德国王会不耐烦的。”

paragraphs.text: "Three nails in each shoe will hold them on," said the smith. "Yes, I think we may risk it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “每只鞋上有三颗钉子就能把它们固定住，”铁匠说。 “是的，我想我们可以冒险。”

paragraphs.text: The battle had been raging for some time. King Richard rode hither and thither, cheering his men and fighting his foes. His enemy, Henry, who wished to be king, was pressing him hard.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 战斗已经激烈了一段时间。理查国王策马驰骋，为他的部下欢呼，并与敌人作战。他的敌人亨利一心想当国王，对他施加了很大的压力。

paragraphs.text: Far away, at the other side of the field, King Richard saw his men falling back. Without his help they would soon be beaten. So he spurred his horse to ride to their aid.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 远处，在战场的另一边，理查国王看到他的部下正在向后退去。如果没有他的帮助，他们很快就会被打败。于是他策马前去救援。

paragraphs.text: He was hardly halfway across the stony field when one of the horse's shoes flew off. The horse was lamed on a rock. Then another shoe came off. The horse stumbled, and his rider was thrown heavily to the ground.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他刚穿过石地的一半时，一只马蹄飞落了。那匹马被跛在一块岩石上。然后另一只鞋掉了。马匹绊倒了，骑手重重地摔在地上。

paragraphs.text: Before the king could rise, his frightened horse, although lame, had galloped away. The king looked, and saw that his soldiers were beaten, and that the battle was everywhere going against him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王还没站起来，他那匹受惊的马虽然跛了，但已经飞奔而去了。国王一看，发现他的士兵都被打败了，而且到处都是对他不利的战斗。

paragraphs.text: He waved his sword in the air. He shouted, "A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse." But there was no horse for him. His soldiers were intent on saving themselves. They could not give him any help.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他在空中挥舞着剑。他喊道：“一匹马！一匹马！我的王国是一匹马。”但他没有马。他的士兵一心想自救。他们无法给他任何帮助。

paragraphs.text: "For the want of a nail the shoe was lost; For the want of a shoe the horse was lost; For the want of a horse the battle was lost; For the failure of battle the kingdom was lost;-- And all for the want of a horseshoe nail."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “因为缺少一颗钉子，所以失去了鞋子；因为缺少了鞋子，所以失去了马；因为缺少一匹马，所以战斗失败了；因为战斗的失败，失去了王国；——而这一切都是因为缺少一颗马蹄钉。”

paragraphs.text: Richard the Third was one of England's worst kings. Henry, the Duke of Richmond, made war upon him and defeated him in a great battle.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 理查三世是英格兰最糟糕的国王之一。里士满公爵亨利向他宣战，并在一场大战中击败了他。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: THE HOME OF ROBIN IN SHERWOOD FOREST

paragraphs.text: Many hundreds of years ago, when the Plantagenets were kings, England was so covered with woods that a squirrel was said to be able to hop from tree to tree from the Severn to the Humber.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 数百年前，当金雀花王朝还是国王时，英格兰被树林覆盖，据说松鼠能够从塞文河到亨伯河从一棵树跳到另一棵树。

paragraphs.text: It must have been very different-looking from the country we travel through now; but still there were roads that ran from north to south and from east to west, for the use of those who wished to leave their homes, and at certain times of the year these roads were thronged with people. Pilgrims going to some holy shrine passed along, merchants taking their wares to Court, Abbots and Bishops ambling by on palfreys to bear their part in the King's Council, and, more frequently still, a solitary Knight, seeking adventures.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它一定与我们现在所经过的国家有很大不同。但仍然有从北到南、从东到西的道路，供那些想要离开家园的人使用，并且在一年中的某些时候，这些道路上挤满了人。前往圣地的朝圣者经过，商人将货物带到宫廷，修道院院长和主教骑着小马漫步经过，在国王的议会中发挥作用，更常见的是，孤独的骑士寻求冒险。

paragraphs.text: Besides the broad roads there were small tracks and little green paths, and these led to clumps of low huts, where dwelt the peasants, charcoal-burners, and plowmen, while here and there some larger clearing than usual told that the house of a yeoman was near.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 除了宽阔的道路之外，还有小路和小绿道，通向一丛丛低矮的小屋，那里住着农民、烧炭的人和犁地的人，而到处都有比平时更大的空地，表明附近有一个自耕农的房子。

paragraphs.text: Now and then as you passed through the forest you might ride by a splendid abbey, and catch a glimpse of monks in long black or white gowns, fishing in the streams and rivers that abound in this part of England, or casting nets in the fish ponds which were in the midst of the abbey gardens. Or you might chance to see a castle with round turrets and high battlements, circled by strong walls, and protected by a moat full of water.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当你穿过森林时，你可能会时不时地骑马经过一座辉煌的修道院，瞥见穿着黑色或白色长袍的僧侣，他们在英格兰这一地区盛产的溪流和河流中钓鱼，或者在修道院花园中央的鱼塘里撒网。或者您可能有机会看到一座拥有圆形塔楼和高城垛的城堡，周围有坚固的城墙，并有一条充满水的护城河保护。

paragraphs.text: This was the sort of England into which the famous Robin Hood was born. We know very little about him, who he was, or where he lived, except that for some reason he had offended the King, who had declared him an outlaw, so that any man might kill him and never pay a penalty for it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这就是著名的罗宾汉出生的英格兰。我们对他、他是谁、他住在哪里知之甚少，只知道出于某种原因，他冒犯了国王，国王宣布他为亡命之徒，以便任何人都可以杀死他，并且永远不会为此付出代价。

paragraphs.text: But, outlaw or not, the poor people loved him and looked on him as their friend, and many a stout fellow came to join him, and led a merry life in the greenwood, with moss and fern for bed, and for meat the King's deer, which it was death to slay.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但是，不管是不是亡命之徒，穷人都爱他，视他为朋友，许多粗壮的家伙也来加入他的行列，在绿林里过着快乐的生活，以苔藓和蕨类植物为床，以国王的鹿为食，杀死它就是死亡。

paragraphs.text: Peasants of all sorts, tillers of the land, yeomen, and, as some say, Knights, went on their ways freely, for of them Robin took no toll; but rich men with moneybags well filled trembled as they drew near to Sherwood Forest--who was to know whether behind every tree there did not lurk Robin Hood or some of his men?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 各种各样的农民、耕种者、自耕农，还有一些人所说的骑士，都自由地前行，因为罗宾没有对他们造成任何伤害。但是，当他们接近舍伍德森林时，钱袋子装得满满的富人都在颤抖——谁知道每棵树后面是否潜伏着罗宾汉或他的一些手下呢？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 9. AUDUBON, NATURALIST

paragraphs.text: Every boy who loves out-of-door life should know the story of John James Audubon. He was born on a farm in Louisiana, May 4, 1780. His parents were French and when very young he was taken to France where he attended school. His favorite study was of animals and birds. He often roamed the woods, bringing home birds' nests and eggs, curious rocks, and bits of moss. His father bought him a picture-book of birds. The delighted boy painted these copies, but saw they were not like real birds. Later he took lessons of the great French painter, David, who taught him to draw and paint things as they are. Returning to America, his father gave him a large farm in Pennsylvania where his studies of birds led him to decide to write a book on bird life, and illustrate it by his own drawings. This was a great task, but when this young man decided to do anything he never allowed difficulties to stand in his way. So he began his work and studied and painted year after year. He had to live much of the time in the woods, studying how the birds lived and built their nests. Sometimes he went by boat down the river; sometimes he went on horseback. Often he tramped alone through the trackless woods. Many nights he slept out-of-doors. He lost all his money and was obliged to stop his work and paint portraits and sell his choice drawings for a living. His heroic wife took up school-teaching to help him out with his work. One day while traveling he left his paintings of nearly a thousand birds in a wooden box in the home of a friend. Two months later, when he returned and opened the box, he found two large rats had got into the box and cut up all the paintings with their sharp teeth, making a nest for their young among the gnawed pieces. He said, "I will make better paintings!" It took him four long years to complete his pictures, but at last the great book was completed and published and praised throughout France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个热爱户外生活的男孩都应该知道约翰·詹姆斯·奥杜邦的故事。 1780 年 5 月 4 日，他出生在路易斯安那州的一个农场。他的父母是法国人，他很小的时候就被带到法国并在那里上学。他最喜欢的研究是动物和鸟类。他经常在树林里漫步，带回家鸟巢和鸟蛋、奇怪的岩石和一些苔藓。他父亲给他买了一本鸟类图画书。男孩很高兴地画了这些复制品，但发现它们并不像真正的鸟。后来他师从法国伟大画家大卫，大卫教他画画并描绘事物的本来面目。回到美国后，他的父亲在宾夕法尼亚州给了他一个大农场，在那里他对鸟类的研究使他决定写一本关于鸟类生活的书，并用自己的图画来说明。这是一项艰巨的任务，但是当这个年轻人决定做任何事情时，他从不让困难阻碍他。于是他开始了他的工作，年复一年地学习、绘画。他大部分时间都住在树林里，研究鸟儿如何生活和筑巢。有时他乘船顺河而下；有时他骑马。他常常独自一人在无路可走的树林里行走。许多个夜晚他都睡在户外。他失去了所有的钱，被迫停止工作，画肖像并出售他选择的画作谋生。他英勇的妻子开始在学校教书，以帮助他完成工作。有一天，他在旅行时将近千只鸟的画落在一位朋友家中的一个木箱里。两个月后，当他回来打开盒子时，发现两只大老鼠钻进了盒子，用锋利的牙齿把所有的画都咬碎了，并在啃碎的碎片中为幼崽筑巢。他说：“我会画出更好的画！”他花了四年的时间才完成了他的画作，但这本伟大的书最终完成并出版，并在法国、英国和美国受到赞扬。

paragraphs.text: The Society for the Protection of Our Feathered Friends was organized by this great naturalist, who spent the rest of his life for this great object. At present there are few places where boys and girls have not heard of Audubon. He died at his beautiful home on the Hudson River, greatly honored and beloved in France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 保护我们的羽毛朋友协会是由这位伟大的博物学家组织的，他为这个伟大的目标度过了余生。目前，几乎每个地方的男孩和女孩都没有听说过奥杜邦。他在哈德逊河畔美丽的家中去世，在法国、英国和美国受到极大的尊敬和爱戴。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: English Fairy Tales: HOW JACK WENT TO SEEK HIS FORTUNE

paragraphs.text: Once on a time there was a boy named Jack, and one morning he started to go and seek his fortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一个名叫杰克的男孩，一天早上他开始去寻找他的财富。

paragraphs.text: Well, they went on till it was about dark, and they began to think of some place where they could spend the night. About this time they came in sight of a house, and Jack told them to keep still while he went up and looked in through the window. And there were some robbers counting over their money. Then Jack went back and told them to wait till he gave the word, and then to make all the noise they could. So when they were all ready Jack gave the word, and the cat mewed, and the dog barked, and the goat bleated, and the bull bellowed, and the rooster crowed, and all together they made such a dreadful noise that it frightened the robbers all away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 好吧，他们继续前行，直到天快黑了，他们才开始思考可以过夜的地方。大约在这个时候，他们看到了一栋房子，杰克让他们保持安静，而他则走上楼去透过窗户往里看。还有一些强盗在数钱。然后杰克回去并告诉他们要等他一声令下，然后尽可能地发出声音。当他们准备好后，杰克一声令下，猫喵喵叫，狗吠，山羊咩咩叫，公牛吼叫，公鸡打鸣，它们一起发出如此可怕的声音，把强盗都吓跑了。

paragraphs.text: And then they went in and took possession of the house. Jack was afraid the robbers would come back in the night, and so when it came time to go to bed he put the cat in the rocking-chair, and he put the dog under the table, and he put the goat upstairs, and he put the bull down cellar, and the rooster flew up on to the roof, and Jack went to bed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后他们进去并占领了房子。杰克害怕强盗晚上会回来，所以到了睡觉的时候，他把猫放在摇椅上，把狗放在桌子底下，把山羊放在楼上，把公牛放在地窖里，然后公鸡飞上屋顶，杰克就上床睡觉了。

paragraphs.text: By-and-by the robbers saw it was all dark and they sent one man back to the house to look after their money. Before long he came back in a great fright and told them his story.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 过了一会儿，强盗们发现天已经黑了，他们派了一名男子回屋里看管他们的钱。不久，他惊恐地回来，向他们讲述了他的故事。

paragraphs.text: "I went back to the house," said he, "and went in and tried to sit down in the rocking-chair, and there was an old woman knitting, and she stuck her knitting-needles into me." That was the cat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我回到屋子，”他说，“走进去，想在摇椅上坐下，发现有一个老妇人在织毛衣，她把织针扎进了我的身体里。”那是那只猫，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I went to the table to look after the money and there was a shoemaker under the table, and he stuck his awl into me." That was the dog, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我走到桌子前看钱，桌子底下有一个鞋匠，他把锥子插到了我身上。”那是狗，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go upstairs, and there was a man up there threshing, and he knocked me down with his flail." That was the goat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我开始上楼，有一个人在上面打谷，他用连枷把我打倒了。”那是山羊，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go down cellar, and there was a man down there chopping wood, and he knocked me up with his axe." That was the bull, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我开始走进地窖，发现一个人在下面砍柴，他用斧子把我撞倒了。”你知道，那是公牛。

paragraphs.text: "But I shouldn't have minded all that if it hadn't been for that little fellow on top of the house, who kept a-hollering, 'Chuck him up to me-e! Chuck him up to me-e!'" Of course that was the cock-a-doodle-do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “但如果不是那个在房子顶上的小家伙不停地叫喊着，‘把他扔给我-e！把他扔给我-e！’”当然，那是鸡毛蒜皮的事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: THE PATIENT CAT

paragraphs.text: When the spotted cat first found the nest, there was nothing in it, for it was only just finished. So she said, "I will wait!" for she was a patient cat, and the summer was before her. She waited a week, and then she climbed up again to the top of the tree, and peeped into the nest. There lay two lovely blue eggs, smooth and shining.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当花斑猫第一次找到巢时，里面什么也没有，因为它才刚刚建成。于是她说：“我会等！”因为她是一只有耐心的猫，夏天就在她面前。她等了一个星期，然后又爬到树顶，向鸟巢里窥视。里面下了两个可爱的蓝色蛋，光滑闪亮。

paragraphs.text: The spotted cat said, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait." So she waited; and while she was waiting, she caught mice and rats, and washed herself and slept, and did all that a spotted cat should do to pass the time away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 花猫说：“鸡蛋也许不错，但幼鸟更好。我会等。”于是她等待着；在等待的时候，她捉老鼠，洗澡，睡觉，做着一只花猫该做的一切来打发时间。

paragraphs.text: When another week had passed, she climbed the tree again and peeped into the nest. This time there were five eggs. But the spotted cat said again, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait a little longer!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 又过了一个星期，她又爬上树，向鸟巢里窥视。这次有五个鸡蛋。但花猫又说道：“鸡蛋或许不错，但幼鸟更好。我再等一会儿吧！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited a little longer and then went up again to look. Ah! there were five tiny birds, with big eyes and long necks, and yellow beaks wide open. Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch, and licked her nose and purred, for she was very happy. "It is worth while to be patient!" she said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她又等了一会儿，然后又上去查看。啊!有五只小鸟，眼睛很大，脖子很长，黄色的嘴巴张得很大。然后花猫就坐在树枝上，舔着鼻子，发出咕噜咕噜的声音，因为她很高兴。 “忍耐是值得的！”她说。

paragraphs.text: But when she looked again at the young birds, to see which one she should take first, she saw that they were very thin,--oh, very, very thin they were! The spotted cat had never seen anything so thin in her life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但是，当她再次看着幼鸟，看看应该先拿哪一只时，她发现它们非常瘦——哦，非常非常瘦！斑猫这辈子从来没有见过这么瘦的东西。

paragraphs.text: "Now," she said to herself, "if I were to wait only a few days longer, they would grow fat. Thin birds may be good, but fat birds are much better. I will wait!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在，”她对自己说，“如果我再等几天，它们就会长胖了。瘦的鸟可能很好，但胖的鸟更好。我会等！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited; and she watched the father-bird bringing worms all day long to the nest, and said, "Aha! they must be fattening fast! they will soon be as fat as I wish them to be. Aha! what a good thing it is to be patient."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她等待着；她看着鸟爸爸整天把虫子带到巢里，说道：“啊哈！它们一定长肥得很快！它们很快就会像我希望的那样胖起来。啊哈！有耐心真是太好了。”

paragraphs.text: At last, one day she thought, "Surely, now they must be fat enough! I will not wait another day. Aha! how good they will be!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，有一天她想：“当然，现在它们一定已经够胖了！我不会再等了。啊哈！它们会多棒啊！”

paragraphs.text: So she climbed up the tree, licking her chops all the way and thinking of the fat young birds. And when she reached the top and looked into the nest, it was empty!!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她爬上树，一路舔着排骨，想着那些胖乎乎的小鸟。当她到达山顶并向鸟巢内看去时，里面空无一人！

paragraphs.text: Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch and spoke thus, "Well, of all the horrid, mean, ungrateful creatures I ever saw, those birds are the horridest, and the meanest, and the most ungrateful! Mi-a-u-ow!!!!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后花斑猫在树枝上坐下，说道：“嗯，在我见过的所有可怕、卑鄙、忘恩负义的生物中，那些鸟是最可怕、最卑鄙、最忘恩负义的！米啊啊啊啊啊啊啊！！！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Prince: To the Magnificent Lorenzo Di Piero De' Medici

paragraphs.text: Those who strive to obtain the good graces of a prince are accustomed to come before him with such things as they hold most precious, or in which they see him take most delight; whence one often sees horses, arms, cloth of gold, precious stones, and similar ornaments presented to princes, worthy of their greatness.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那些努力获得王子恩宠的人习惯于带着自己最珍贵的东西或他们看到王子最高兴的东西来到他面前。人们经常看到马匹、武器、金布、宝石和类似的装饰品被赠送给王子，以配得上他们的伟大。

paragraphs.text: Desiring therefore to present myself to your Magnificence with some testimony of my devotion towards you, I have not found among my possessions anything which I hold more dear than, or value so much as, the knowledge of the actions of great men, acquired by long experience in contemporary affairs, and a continual study of antiquity; which, having reflected upon it with great and prolonged diligence, I now send, digested into a little volume, to your Magnificence.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 因此，我希望向您展示我对您的忠诚，但在我的财产中，我没有发现任何东西比通过对当代事务的长期经验和对古代的不断研究而获得的伟人行为的知识更珍贵或更有价值。经过长时间的辛勤思考，我现在将其消化成一小册，寄给陛下。

paragraphs.text: And although I may consider this work unworthy of your countenance, nevertheless I trust much to your benignity that it may be acceptable, seeing that it is not possible for me to make a better gift than to offer you the opportunity of understanding in the shortest time all that I have learnt in so many years, and with so many troubles and dangers; which work I have not embellished with swelling or magnificent words, nor stuffed with rounded periods, nor with any extrinsic allurements or adornments whatever, with which so many are accustomed to embellish their works; for I have wished either that no honour should be given it, or else that the truth of the matter and the weightiness of the theme shall make it acceptable.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 虽然我可能认为这项工作不值得您的赏识，但我非常相信您的仁慈，它可能会被接受，因为对我来说，没有什么比为您提供一个更好的礼物了，让您有机会在最短的时间内了解我在这么多年、经历了这么多麻烦和危险中所学到的一切；我没有用浮夸或华丽的文字来修饰这些作品，也没有塞满圆润的句号，也没有用任何外在的诱惑或装饰来修饰，而许多人习惯用这些来修饰他们的作品。因为我希望要么不给予它任何荣誉，要么事情的真相和主题的重要性应该使它可以接受。

paragraphs.text: Nor do I hold with those who regard it as a presumption if a man of low and humble condition dare to discuss and settle the concerns of princes; because, just as those who draw landscapes place themselves below in the plain to contemplate the nature of the mountains and of lofty places, and in order to contemplate the plains place themselves upon high mountains, even so to understand the nature of the people it needs to be a prince, and to understand that of princes it needs to be of the people.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我也不同意那些认为地位卑微的人敢于讨论和解决君主的事情的人；因为，正如画山水的人，置身于平地之中，才能观山色、高处之性，置高山之上，才能观平地，同样，要了解民性，就必须是君主，要了解君主性，就必须要在民中。

paragraphs.text: Take then, your Magnificence, this little gift in the spirit in which I send it; wherein, if it be diligently read and considered by you, you will learn my extreme desire that you should attain that greatness which fortune and your other attributes promise. And if your Magnificence from the summit of your greatness will sometimes turn your eyes to these lower regions, you will see how unmeritedly I suffer a great and continued malignity of fortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那么，陛下，本着我送出这份小礼物的精神，请收下它吧；其中，如果你认真阅读和思考，你就会了解到我的极度渴望，即你应该获得财富和其他属性所承诺的伟大。如果您的伟大顶峰有时会将您的目光转向这些较低的地区，您就会看到我是多么不值得地遭受巨大而持续的不幸。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Stories from English History: THE WOOSTER (OHIO) SCHOOL

paragraphs.text: Four summers ago the writer called a meeting in one of the rooms of Taylor Hall—one of the University buildings used by the Summer School.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 四年前的夏天，作者在泰勒大厅（暑期学校使用的大学建筑之一）的一个房间里召开了一次会议。

paragraphs.text: A tentative organization was formed and meetings held until permanent organization was effected. The meetings were then held in the evening on the campus in front of Kanke Hall or the Memorial Chapel. A Kindergartner was also engaged by the management of the school to tell stories for a day or two. Each summer since a similar arrangement has been made and now a regular story-hour in the Model School is planned for in addition to weekly meetings of the League. This meets on the Library steps Saturday evenings at 6:30. Old familiar college and folk songs are sung, then the story-teller or story-tellers tell their stories, other songs follow and then all go home happier and better.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 成立了临时组织并举行了会议，直到永久组织成立为止。会议随后于晚上在校园坎克大厅或纪念教堂前举行。学校管理层还聘请了一名幼儿园小朋友讲故事一两天。自从做出了类似的安排以来，每年夏天，除了每周的联盟会议外，还计划在模范学校定期讲故事。该会议每周六晚上 6:30 在图书馆台阶举行。唱着熟悉的大学歌曲和民歌，然后讲故事的人讲述他们的故事，其他歌曲随之而来，然后大家都更快乐、更好地回家。

paragraphs.text: The story hour is an event here—several times within the past ten summers five or six hundred listeners were seated in a circle round the story-teller and we may safely predict that the League has a permanent place in the Summer School program. Commercial reasons, if no other, will compel it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 故事时间是这里的一项活动——在过去的十个夏天里，有好几次，五六百名听众围坐在讲故事的人周围，我们可以有把握地预测，联盟在暑期学校项目中占有永久的地位。如果没有其他原因，商业原因也会迫使它这样做。

paragraphs.text: The attendance at the story hour is not confined to students in the Summer School—many of the leading citizens and summer visitors make it convenient to drop around at that time and join the magic circle. We have been able to discover some splendid story-tellers each summer, and the great myths and classics of the race have been splendidly told and added much to the lives of many.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 参加故事时间的人并不局限于暑期学校的学生——许多知名市民和暑期游客都方便在这个时间顺便逛逛，加入魔法圈。每年夏天我们都会发现一些出色的故事讲述者，伟大的神话和种族经典被精彩地讲述，并为许多人的生活增添了很多色彩。

paragraphs.text: Kipling, Van Dyke, Dickens and other masters have been quoted and several interesting, original stories and adaptations of translations have been given. The type of story has been the very highest. Much folk-lore study and legendary investigation has been the outcome of it all. And if the writer had done nothing else during the four years he was a member of the Faculty of Wooster Summer School than to organize and keep alive the Story Tellers' League, he would have no reason to be ashamed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 书中引用了吉卜林、范戴克、狄更斯等大师的作品，并给出了一些有趣的原创故事和翻译改编。故事的类型已经是最高的了。许多民间传说研究和传奇调查都是这一切的结果。如果作者在伍斯特暑期学校的四年里除了组织和维持讲故事者联盟之外没有做任何其他事情，他就没有理由感到羞耻。

paragraphs.text: It is now finally established on a high level, and will live as long as the Summer School. It will serve as the inspiration to many teachers to learn the fine art, thus placing within the reach of the little children of the Republic the golden key to the world's great Literatures, and will give to teachers the secret of making children feel at home when at school and keeping them in touch with the heart strings of the future citizens of our country. So mote it be.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在它终于在高水平上建立起来，并将与暑期学校一样长久。它将激励许多教师学习美术，从而将世界伟大文学的金钥匙放在共和国的孩子们触手可及的地方，并将向教师们传授让孩子们在学校有宾至如归的感觉并让他们与我国未来公民的心弦保持联系的秘诀。就这样吧。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: FAMOUS PASSAGES FROM DOCTOR WATTS

paragraphs.text: O 'tis a lovely thing for youth To walk betimes in wisdom's way; To fear a lie, to speak the truth, That we may trust to all they say.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 及时走在智慧的道路上，对青春来说是一件可爱的事情；害怕谎言，说真话，这样我们就可以相信他们所说的一切。

paragraphs.text: But liars we can never trust, Though they should speak the thing that's true; And he that does one fault at first, And lies to hide it, makes it two. (From "Against Lying")
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但我们永远不能相信说谎者，尽管他们说的是真的；谁一开始就犯了一个错误，然后用谎言来掩盖它，那么他就会犯下两个错误。 （摘自《反对说谎》）

paragraphs.text: Whatever brawls disturb the street, There should be peace at home; Where sisters dwell and brothers meet, Quarrels should never come.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 街上有什么争吵，家里就应该有平安；姐妹居住、兄弟相聚的地方，决不应该发生争吵。

paragraphs.text: Birds in their little nests agree: And 'tis a shameful sight, When children of one family Fall out, and chide, and fight. (From "Love between Brothers and Sisters")
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小巢里的鸟儿都同意：当一个家庭的孩子们闹翻、责骂、打架时，这是一种可耻的景象。 （摘自《兄弟姐妹之爱》）

paragraphs.text: How proud we are! how fond to show Our clothes, and call them rich and new! When the poor sheep and silk-worm wore That very clothing long before.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们多么自豪啊！多么喜欢展示我们的衣服，并称它们又富又新！当可怜的羊和蚕很久以前就穿着那件衣服时。

paragraphs.text: The tulip and the butterfly Appear in gayer coats than I; Let me be dressed fine as I will, Flies, worms, and flowers exceed me still.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 郁金香和蝴蝶穿着比我更鲜艳的外衣；让我穿得漂漂亮亮吧，苍蝇、虫子、花儿都比我还厉害。

paragraphs.text: Then will I set my heart to find Inward adornings of the mind; Knowledge and virtue, truth and grace, These are the robes of richest dress. (From "Against Pride in Clothes")
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后我会下定决心去寻找心灵的内在装饰；知识与美德，真理与恩典，这些是最华丽的衣袍。 （摘自《反对衣着骄傲》）

paragraphs.text: Let dogs delight to bark and bite, For God hath made them so; Let bears and lions growl and fight, For 'tis their nature to.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 让狗欢喜地吠叫和咬人吧，因为上帝使它们如此；让熊和狮子咆哮和战斗吧，因为这是它们的本性。

paragraphs.text: But, children, you should never let Such angry passions rise; Your little hands were never made To tear each other's eyes. (From "Against Quarreling and Fighting")
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但是，孩子们，你们永远不应该让这种愤怒的情绪升起；你们的小手从来就不是用来撕裂彼此的眼睛的。 （摘自《反对争吵打架》）

paragraphs.text: Most of the work of Henry Wadsworth Longfellow (1807-1882) is within the range of children's interests and comprehension. Three poems are given here, "The Skeleton in Armor," as representative of Longfellow's large group of narrative poems, "The Day Is Done," as an expression of the value of poetry in everyday life, and "The Psalm of Life," as the finest and most popular example of his hortatory poems.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亨利·沃兹沃斯·朗费罗（Henry Wadsworth Longfellow，1807-1882）的大部分作品都在儿童的兴趣和理解范围之内。这里给出了三首诗，《穿盔甲的骷髅》，作为朗费罗大量叙事诗的代表，《日子已经过去》，作为日常生活中诗歌价值的表达，以及《生命的诗篇》，作为他的劝诫诗中最优秀和最受欢迎的例子。

paragraphs.text: "The Skeleton in Armor" is one of Longfellow's first and best American art ballads. In Newport, Rhode Island, is an old stone tower known as the "Round Tower," which some people think was built by the Northmen, though it probably was not. In 1836 workmen unearthed a strange skeleton at Fall River, Massachusetts. It was wrapped in bark and coarse cloth. On the breast was a plate of brass, and around the waist was a belt of brass tubes. Apparently it was not the skeleton of an Indian, and people supposed it might have been that of one of the old Norsemen. Longfellow used these two historic facts as a basis for the plot of his poem, which he wrote in 1840.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 《穿盔甲的骷髅》是朗费罗第一首也是最好的美国艺术民谣之一。在罗德岛州的纽波特，有一座古老的石塔，被称为“圆塔”，有些人认为它是由北方人建造的，尽管它可能不是。 1836 年，工人们在马萨诸塞州福尔里弗发现了一具奇怪的骨架。它被树皮和粗布包裹着。胸前挂着一块铜板，腰间系着一条铜管带。显然，这不是印第安人的骨骼，人们猜测它可能是一位古老的挪威人的骨骼。朗费罗以这两个历史事实作为他于 1840 年写的诗的情节基础。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 12. ALTRUISTIC STORIES

paragraphs.text: Stories of unselfish heroism appeal to every age, but they find their strongest interest for the spirit of youth during the years of middle adolescence. Such stories of self-sacrifice may be selected from the Bible, history, fiction, or modern life. They not only show what is noble action, but touch the soul with the contagion of self-sacrificing deeds. From the Ethical Index, on page 291, under Altruism, Loyalty, Self-sacrifice, and such synonyms, a list of altruistic stories may be made.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 无私英雄主义的故事对每个年龄段都有吸引力，但他们对青春期中期的青春精神最感兴趣。这些自我牺牲的故事可以选自圣经、历史、小说或现代生活。他们不仅展示了什么是高尚的行为，而且以自我牺牲的行为感染着灵魂。从《道德索引》第 291 页，在利他主义、忠诚、自我牺牲和此类同义词下，可以列出一份利他故事列表。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 8. THE CROW AND THE PITCHER

paragraphs.text: Once a Crow who was very thirsty found a pitcher with a little water at the bottom which he was unable to reach. He tried to overturn the pitcher but it was too heavy. "Ah! Ah! I know what I'll do," he said. So he gathered up pebbles from the ground, and one after another dropped them into the pitcher until the water gradually reached the top. Then the wise Crow was able to drink all the water he wanted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只非常口渴的乌鸦发现了一个水罐，底部有一点水，但他够不到。他试图打翻水罐，但它太重了。 “啊！啊！我知道我要做什么，”他说。于是，他从地上捡起小石子，一个又一个地扔进水罐里，直到水逐渐到达顶部。然后聪明的乌鸦就能够喝到他想要喝的水了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A very young Fox, who had never before seen a Lion, happened to meet one in the forest. A single look was enough to send the Fox off at top speed for the nearest hiding place.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只非常年轻的狐狸以前从未见过狮子，但碰巧在森林里遇到了一只狮子。只要看一眼就足以让狐狸以最快的速度逃往最近的藏身之处。

paragraphs.text: The second time the Fox saw the Lion he stopped behind a tree to look at him a moment before slinking away. But the third time, the Fox went boldly up to the Lion and, without turning a hair, said, "Hello, there, old top."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸第二次看到狮子时，他在一棵树后面停下来看了他一会儿，然后就溜走了。但第三次，狐狸勇敢地走到狮子面前，头也不回地说道：“你好，老头儿。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: When you have finished reading, write the answers to these questions. If you can not answer immediately, find the answer somewhere in the story, but do not read it all again; read only enough to get the correct answer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 读完后，写下这些问题的答案。如果你不能立即回答，请在故事中的某个地方找到答案，但不要再读一遍；只阅读足以获得正确答案的内容。

paragraphs.text: 1. What two reasons can you find to account for the greater loss by fire in the United States than in Europe?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 你能找到哪两个原因来解释美国的火灾损失比欧洲更大？

paragraphs.text: 2. Which of these two reasons can be the more quickly removed? Which one can you help to remove?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 这两个原因哪一个可以更快地排除？你可以帮忙删除哪一个？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 14. THE CONCEITED GRASSHOPPER

paragraphs.text: One day a very young Grasshopper and an old Rooster met out in a field. "I can jump higher than anybody," chirped the Grasshopper. "All right; let me see you do it," said the Rooster, at the same time opening his mouth wide as if he meant to yawn. "Here I go, then," cried the Grasshopper. He jumped so high he landed right in the mouth of the Rooster, who gulped him down. That was the end of the boasting Grasshopper.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只年幼的蚱蜢和一只老公鸡在田野里相遇。 “我比任何人都跳得更高。”蚱蜢叽叽喳喳地说。 “好吧，让我看看你怎么做，”公鸡说着，同时张大了嘴，好像要打哈欠。 “那我走了，”蚱蜢喊道。他跳得太高了，正好落在公鸡的嘴里，公鸡把他吞了下去。自吹自擂的蚱蜢就这样结束了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Sixth Eclectic Reader: EXAMPLES. (23)

paragraphs.text: 3. As he spoke without fear of consequences', so his actions were marked with the most unbending resolution,
paragraphs.translation_zh: 3. 由于他说话时不担心后果，因此他的行动也表现出最不屈不挠的决心，

paragraphs.text: 5. If then, his Providence' out of our evil, seek to bring forth good', our labor must be to prevent that end.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 5. 如果他的旨意“从我们的邪恶中寻求良善”，我们的努力就必须阻止这一结果的发生。

paragraphs.text: 6. He', born for the universe', narrowed his mind, And to party gave up what was meant for mankind.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 6.他“为宇宙而生”，狭隘了自己的心胸，为党放弃了对人类的意义。

paragraphs.text: Exception 1.--Relative emphasis often reverses this and the first rule, because emphasis is here expressed in part by changing the usual inflections.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 例外 1——相对强调常常颠倒这一规则和第一条规则，因为这里的强调部分是通过改变通常的词形变化来表达的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE PEACOCK AND THE CRANE

paragraphs.text: A Peacock, puffed up with vanity, met a Crane one day, and to impress him spread his gorgeous tail in the Sun.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只自负的孔雀遇到了一只鹤，为了给鹤留下深刻的印象，孔雀在阳光下展开了它华丽的尾巴。

paragraphs.text: "Look," he said. "What have you to compare with this? I am dressed in all the glory of the rainbow, while your feathers are gray as dust!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看，”他说。 “你有什么可与此相比的？我身着彩虹般的光彩，而你的羽毛却灰如尘土！”

paragraphs.text: "Follow me if you can," he said. But the Peacock stood where he was among the birds of the barnyard, while the Crane soared in freedom far up into the blue sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果可以的话，跟我来，”他说。但孔雀站在谷仓里的鸟儿中间，而鹤则自由地飞向蓝天。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: How do you like to go up in a swing, Up in the air so blue? Oh, I do think it the pleasantest thing Ever a child can do!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你喜欢荡秋千吗，在如此蔚蓝的天空中？哦，我确实认为这是孩子能做的最愉快的事情！

paragraphs.text: Up in the air and over the wall, Till I can see so wide, Rivers and trees and cattle and all Over the countryside--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在空中，越过墙，直到我能看到如此广阔，河流、树木、牛群以及整个乡村——

paragraphs.text: Till I look down on the garden green, Down on the roof so brown-- Up in the air I go flying again, Up in the air and down!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 直到我俯视绿色的花园， 俯视棕色的屋顶—— 在空中我再次飞翔， 在空中又向下！

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 7. THE FOX AND THE GRAPES

paragraphs.text: A Fox who was hungry saw some large, juicy grapes on a vine high up in a tree. "How good they will taste," said he; "I am going to have some of them." Then he gave a run and leaped as high as he could, but the grapes were still far above his head. He could not reach them no matter how high he jumped. At last he trotted off in a rage, muttering, "I know those are sour grapes and not worth eating."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只饥饿的狐狸看到高高的藤蔓上有一些又大又多汁的葡萄。 “它们尝起来多美味啊，”他说。 “我要一些。”然后他跑了起来，尽可能地跳高，但葡萄仍然离他头顶很远。无论他跳得多高，他都够不到他们。最后他怒气冲冲地跑开了，嘟囔道：“我知道那是酸葡萄心理，不值得吃。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Fifth Eclectic Reader: Passion and Grief

paragraphs.text: "Come back! come back!" he cried, in grief. "Across this stormy water, And I'll forgive your Highland chief, My daughter! O, my daughter!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “回来！回来！”他悲伤地哭了起来。 “跨越这暴风雨的水域，我会原谅你的高地酋长，我的女儿！哦，我的女儿！”

paragraphs.text: Plaintive I have lived long enough: my way of life Is fallen into the sear, the yellow leaf: And that which should accompany old age, As honor, love, obedience, troops of friends, I must not look to have.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哀怨的我已经活得够久了：我的生活方式已落入焦灼，黄叶之中：而那些应该伴随老年的东西，如荣誉、爱、服从、朋友的军队，我一定不希望拥有。

paragraphs.text: Calm A very great portion of this globe is covered with water, which is called sea, and is very distinct from rivers and lakes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 平静 地球的很大一部分被水覆盖，称为海洋，与河流和湖泊截然不同。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: FABLES AND SYMBOLIC STORIES

paragraphs.text: The only elaborate and scholarly study in English. Vol. I of a reprint of _Caxton's Aesop_. Published in 1889 in a limited edition and not easily accessible.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 唯一详尽且学术性的英语研究。卷。我是卡克斯顿的《伊索寓言》的重印本。该书于 1889 年出版，限量版，不易获取。

paragraphs.text: Eighty-two selected fables. The Introduction is a summary of all the essential conclusions reached in the study above.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 精选寓言八十二篇。引言是上述研究中得出的所有基本结论的总结。

paragraphs.text: These three books are excellent for simplified versions of the eastern group. Those desiring to get closer to the sources may refer to Cowell , _The Jataka, or Stories of the Buddha's Former Births_; Rhys-Davids, _Buddhist Birth Stories_; Keith-Falconer, _Bidpai's Fables_.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这三本书非常适合东方组的简化版本。那些想要更接近来源的人可以参考《考威尔》、《本生经》或《佛陀前世故事》；里斯-戴维斯，《佛教诞生故事》；基思-法尔科纳，《比德派寓言》。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: DICK WHITTINGTON--II

paragraphs.text: The things were sold, and when the ship came back, each person had the money for what he had sent.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 东西都卖了，船回来的时候，每个人都拿到了自己所寄东西的钱。

paragraphs.text: He had gone but a short way when he heard the sound of the six great bells of Bow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他刚走了没多久，就听到了弓六大钟的声音。

paragraphs.text: "It is strange that the bells should speak to me," said Dick, "but if I am to be Lord Mayor of London, I will gladly turn back."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “钟声竟然对我说话，这很奇怪，”迪克说，“但如果我成为伦敦市长，我会很乐意回去。”

paragraphs.text: "I hope they have not missed me," said Dick, as he gently opened the door and stole softly in.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我希望他们没有错过我，”迪克一边说，一边轻轻地打开门，轻轻地溜了进去。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: ALL THINGS BEAUTIFUL

paragraphs.text: All things bright and beautiful, All creatures great and small, All things wise and wonderful, The Lord God made them all.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一切明亮而美丽的事物，一切伟大和渺小的生物，一切智慧而美妙的事物，主上帝创造了它们。

paragraphs.text: Each little flower that opens, Each little bird that sings, He made their glowing colors, He made their tiny wings.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每朵开放的小花，每只歌唱的小鸟，他创造了它们绚丽的色彩，他创造了它们微小的翅膀。

paragraphs.text: The cold wind in the winter, The pleasant summer sun, The ripe fruits in the garden, He made them every one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天的寒风，夏天的宜人阳光，花园里成熟的果实，每一个都是他创造的。

paragraphs.text: He gave us eyes to see them, And lips that we might tell How great is God Almighty, Who hath made all things well.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他给了我们眼睛来观察它们，给了我们嘴唇让我们可以讲述全能的上帝是多么伟大，他使万物变得美好。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: BED IN SUMMER

paragraphs.text: In winter I get up at night And dress by yellow candle-light. In summer, quite the other way, I have to go to bed by day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天我晚上起床，在黄色烛光下穿衣。在夏天，情况恰恰相反，我白天必须上床睡觉。

paragraphs.text: I have to go to bed and see The birds still hopping on the tree, Or hear the grown-up people's feet Still going past me in the street.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我必须上床睡觉，看看鸟儿仍在树上跳跃，或者听到大人的脚步声仍在街上从我身边经过。

paragraphs.text: And does it not seem hard to you, When all the sky is clear and blue, And I should like so much to play, To have to go to bed by day?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当所有的天空都晴朗而蔚蓝时，我会非常喜欢玩耍，而白天却必须上床睡觉，这对你来说不似乎很难吗？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had stolen a Lamb and was carrying it off to his lair to eat it. But his plans were very much changed when he met a Lion, who, without making any excuses, took the Lamb away from him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼偷了一只羔羊，并把它带到自己的巢穴里吃掉。但当他遇到一只狮子时，他的计划发生了很大的变化，狮子没有任何借口，就把羔羊从他身边带走了。

paragraphs.text: The Lion looked back, but as the Wolf was too far away to be taught a lesson without too much inconvenience, he said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狮子回头看了一眼，但由于狼距离太远，给教训带来了很大不便，他说道：

paragraphs.text: "Your property? Did you buy it, or did the Shepherd make you a gift of it? Pray tell me, how did you get it?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你的财产？是你买的，还是牧羊人送给你的礼物？请告诉我，你是怎么得到它的？”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Discussion: 1. What became of the arrow? Of the song? 2. Where was the arrow found? When? 3. Where was the Song found? 4. Point out lines that rime. 5. What is Longfellow's purpose in this poem? 6. Why is the poet's song compared to the flight of an arrow? 7. A poet once said, "Let me make the Songs of a nation, and I care not who makes the laws." What did he mean? 8. What was the Song doing "in the heart of a friend"?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论： 1. 箭头变成了什么？歌曲的？ 2. 箭头在哪里找到的？什么时候？ 3. 这首歌曲是在哪里发现的？ 4. 指出有韵律的线条。 5.朗费罗这首诗的目的是什么？ 6.为什么诗人把歌曲比作箭的飞行？ 7. 一位诗人曾经说过：“让我创作一个国家的歌曲，我不在乎谁制定法律。”他是什么意思？ 8. 宋词“在朋友心里”在做什么？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: How to Tell Stories to Children: "DEAR ME, WHERE IS MY TOADSTOOL?"

paragraphs.text: From these four instances we may, perhaps, deduce certain general principles of adaptation which have at least proved valuable to those using them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从这四个例子中，我们或许可以推断出某些适应的一般原则，这些原则至少被证明对那些使用它们的人来说是有价值的。

paragraphs.text: These are suggestions which the practised story-teller will find trite. But to others they may prove a fair foundation on which to build a personal method to be developed by experience. I have given them a tabular arrangement below.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 对于经验丰富的讲故事者来说，这些建议会显得陈词滥调。但对其他人来说，它们可能是建立通过经验发展的个人方法的公平基础。我在下面给了他们一个表格安排。

paragraphs.text: The preliminary step in all cases is _Analysis of the Story._ The aim, then, is to _reduce_ a long story or to _amplify_ a short one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 所有案例的第一步都是分析故事。那么，我们的目标就是缩短长篇故事或放大短篇故事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE KID AND THE WOLF

paragraphs.text: A frisky young Kid had been left by the herdsman on the thatched roof of a sheep shelter to keep him out of harm's way. The Kid was browsing near the edge of the roof, when he spied a Wolf and began to jeer at him, making faces and abusing him to his heart's content.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 牧民将一只活泼的小孩子留在了羊棚的茅草屋顶上，以免他受到伤害。孩子正在屋顶边缘附近闲逛，这时他发现了一只狼，开始嘲笑他，做鬼脸，尽情地辱骂他。

paragraphs.text: "I hear you," said the Wolf, "and I haven't the least grudge against you for what you say or do. When you are up there it is the roof that's talking, not you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我听到你的声音了，”狼说，“我对你的言行没有丝毫怨恨。当你在上面时，是屋顶在说话，而不是你。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE PLANE TREE

paragraphs.text: Two Travellers, walking in the noonday sun, sought the shade of a widespreading tree to rest. As they lay looking up among the pleasant leaves, they saw that it was a Plane Tree.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两个旅人在正午的阳光下行走，寻找一棵大树的阴凉处休息。当他们躺在宜人的树叶中抬头一看时，他们发现那是一棵梧桐树。

paragraphs.text: "How useless is the Plane!" said one of them. "It bears no fruit whatever, and only serves to litter the ground with leaves."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “飞机多么无用啊！”其中一位说道。 “它不会结出任何果实，只会在地上散落叶子。”

paragraphs.text: "Ungrateful creatures!" said a voice from the Plane Tree. "You lie here in my cooling shade, and yet you say I am useless! Thus ungratefully, O Jupiter, do men receive their blessings!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “忘恩负义的生物！”梧桐树上传来一个声音。 “你躺在我凉爽的树荫下，却说我毫无用处！朱庇特啊，人们就是这样忘恩负义地接受他们的祝福！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: THE ARROW AND THE SONG

paragraphs.text: I shot an arrow into the air; It Fell to earth, I knew not where; For, so swiftly it flew, the sight Could not follow it in its flight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我向空中射了一箭；它掉到地上，我不知道在哪里；因为它飞得如此之快，视线无法追随它的飞行。

paragraphs.text: I breathed a Song into the air; It fell to earth, I knew not where; For who has sight so keen and strong That it can follow the flight of Song?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我向空气中吹出一首歌曲；它掉到了地上，我不知道在哪里；谁的视力如此敏锐和强大，能够追随宋的飞行？

paragraphs.text: Long, long afterwards, in an oak I found the arrow, still unbroke; And the song, from beginning to end, I found again in the heart of a friend.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 很久很久以后，我在一棵橡树里发现了那支箭，它仍然完好无损。而这首歌，从头到尾，我又在朋友的心里找到了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 2. MISSIONARY STORIES

paragraphs.text: Numerous short and simple stories of heroic lives have recently been written in a very attractive way for boys and girls. These hero stories are for telling, not reading, in home, Sunday-school classes and opening exercises, junior mission circles, or young people's missionary meetings. A few of the best are: "Fifty Missionary Heroes Every Boy and Girl Should Know," by Julia H. Johnson; "Love Stories of Great Missionaries," by Belle M. Brain; "The White Man at Work," and "The Splendid Quest," by Matthews (suitable for children eight to fifteen).
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最近，许多关于英雄生活的短小而简单的故事以非常吸引男孩和女孩的方式写成。这些英雄故事适合在家庭、主日学课程和开场练习、初级传教圈或年轻人的传教会议上讲述，而不是阅读。其中最好的一些是：朱莉娅·H·约翰逊（Julia H. Johnson）的《每个男孩和女孩都应该知道的五十个传教士英雄》； 《伟大传教士的爱情故事》，作者：Belle M. Brain；马修斯的《工作中的白人》和《辉煌的探索》（适合八到十五岁的孩子）。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ALFRED TENNYSON

paragraphs.text: What does little birdie say, In her nest at peep of day? "Let me fly," says little birdie, "Mother, let me fly away." "Birdie, rest a little longer, Till the little wings are stronger." So she rests a little longer, Then she flies away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小鸟在黎明时分在她的巢里说什么？ “让我飞吧，”小鸟说，“妈妈，让我飞走吧。” “小鸟，再休息一会儿，直到小翅膀更加坚强。”于是她休息了一会儿，然后就飞走了。

paragraphs.text: What does little baby say, In her bed at peep of day? Baby says, like little birdie, "Let me rise and fly away." "Baby, sleep a little longer, Till the little limbs are stronger." If she sleeps a little longer, Baby too shall fly away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小宝贝在凌晨时分在床上说什么？宝宝像小鸟一样说：“让我起来飞走吧。” “宝贝，再睡一会儿吧，等小四肢更加有力了。”如果她再睡一会儿，宝宝也会飞走的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: IF EVER I SEE

paragraphs.text: If ever I see, On bush or tree, Young birds in their pretty nest; I must not, in play, Steal the birds away, To grieve their mother's breast.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果我看见，在灌木丛或树上，幼鸟在它们美丽的巢中；我决不能在玩耍时，偷走鸟儿，让它们的母亲的胸膛悲伤。

paragraphs.text: My mother, I know, Would sorrow so, Should I be stolen away; So I'll speak to the birds In my softest words, Nor hurt them in my play.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我知道，如果我被偷走，我的母亲会如此悲伤；所以我要用我最温柔的话语对鸟儿说话，也不用我的游戏伤害它们。

paragraphs.text: And when they can fly In the bright blue sky, They'll warble a song to me; And then if I'm sad It will make me glad To think they are happy and free.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当它们能飞翔在明亮的蓝天时，它们将为我吟唱一首歌；然后，如果我感到悲伤，想到他们是快乐和自由的，我就会感到高兴。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: LITTLE BIRDIE

paragraphs.text: What does little birdie say, In her nest at peep of day? Let me fly, says little birdie, Mother, let me fly away. Birdie, rest a little longer, Till the little wings are stronger. So she rests a little longer, Then she flies away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小鸟在黎明时分在她的巢里说什么？让我飞吧，小鸟说，妈妈，让我飞走吧。小鸟，再休息一会儿，直到小翅膀更加坚强。于是她休息了一会儿，然后就飞走了。

paragraphs.text: What does little baby say, In her bed at peep of day? Baby says, like little birdie, Let me rise and fly away. Baby, sleep a little longer, Till the little limbs are stronger. If she sleeps a little longer, Baby too shall fly away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小宝贝在凌晨时分在床上说什么？宝贝像小鸟一样说，让我起来飞走吧。宝贝，再睡一会儿，直到小四肢更加有力。如果她再睡一会儿，宝宝也会飞走的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 3. SELECT THE STORY

paragraphs.text: Select your story with some definite purpose in mind--pure enjoyment or some definite ethical principle, and let the aim be clearly in mind in the preparation for telling it. Select your story also with the child's story-interests in mind, as presented in Chapter II. Make sure also that it is suitable in length and in style. Children who are accustomed to hearing stories can listen a longer time than those whose ears and brains are quite untrained. With very young children five minutes gives room for a really stirring tale.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 选择你的故事时要有明确的目的——纯粹的享受或某种明确的道德原则，并在准备讲述它时清楚地记住目标。选择故事时还要考虑到孩子的故事兴趣，如第二章所述。还要确保它的长度和样式合适。习惯听故事的孩子比那些耳朵和大脑未经训练的孩子听故事的时间更长。对于年幼的孩子来说，五分钟的时间可以为一个真正激动人心的故事提供空间。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FLIES AND THE HONEY

paragraphs.text: A jar of honey was upset and the sticky sweetness flowed out on the table. The sweet smell of the honey soon brought a large number of Flies buzzing around. They did not wait for an invitation. No, indeed; they settled right down, feet and all, to gorge themselves. The Flies were quickly smeared from head to foot with honey. Their wings stuck together. They could not pull their feet out of the sticky mass. And so they died, giving their lives for the sake of a taste of sweetness.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一罐蜂蜜被打翻，粘稠的甜味流到了桌子上。蜂蜜的甜香很快就引来了一大群苍蝇，嗡嗡地飞来飞去。他们没有等待邀请。不，确实如此；他们立即坐下来，连脚一起吃。很快，苍蝇从头到脚都被涂上了蜂蜜。他们的翅膀粘在一起了。他们无法将脚从粘稠的物体中拔出来。于是他们就死了，为了尝到甜头而献出自己的生命。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FISHERMAN AND THE LITTLE FISH

paragraphs.text: A poor Fisherman, who lived on the fish he caught, had bad luck one day and caught nothing but a very small fry. The Fisherman was about to put it in his basket when the little Fish said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位贫穷的渔夫靠捕到的鱼为生，有一天运气不好，只捕到了一条很小的鱼苗。渔夫正要把它放进篮子里，小鱼说：

paragraphs.text: "Please spare me, Mr. Fisherman! I am so small it is not worth while to carry me home. When I am bigger, I shall make you a much better meal."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “渔夫先生，请放过我吧！我这么小，不值得背我回家。等我长大了，我会给你做一顿更好的饭菜。”

paragraphs.text: "How foolish I should be," he said, "to throw you back. However small you may be, you are better than nothing at all."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我真是太愚蠢了，”他说，“把你扔了回去。不管你有多小，你总比什么都没有好。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE QUACK TOAD

paragraphs.text: An old Toad once informed all his neighbors that he was a learned doctor. In fact he could cure anything. The Fox heard the news and hurried to see the Toad. He looked the Toad over very carefully.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只老蟾蜍曾经告诉他所有的邻居，他是一位博学的医生。事实上他可以治愈任何事情。狐狸听到这个消息，赶紧去看蟾蜍。他非常仔细地打量着蟾蜍。

paragraphs.text: "Mr. Toad," he said, "I've been told that you cure anything! But just take a look at yourself, and then try some of your own medicine. If you can cure yourself of that blotchy skin and that rheumatic gait, someone might believe you. Otherwise, I should advise you to try some other profession."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “蟾蜍先生，”他说，“有人告诉我，你可以治愈任何疾病！但是你看看你自己，然后尝试一下你自己的药物。如果你能治愈自己的斑点皮肤和风湿步态，有人可能会相信你。否则，我应该建议你尝试其他职业。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE BOY AND THE FILBERTS

paragraphs.text: A Boy was given permission to put his hand into a pitcher to get some filberts. But he took such a great fistful that he could not draw his hand out again. There he stood, unwilling to give up a single filbert and yet unable to get them all out at once. Vexed and disappointed he began to cry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个男孩被允许把手伸进水罐里去拿一些榛子。但他握得太紧了，以至于他无法再抽出手来。他站在那里，不愿意放弃一根榛子，但又无法一下子把它们全部拿出来。他既烦恼又失望，开始哭泣。

paragraphs.text: "My boy," said his mother, "be satisfied with half the nuts you have taken and you will easily get your hand out. Then perhaps you may have some more filberts some other time."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我的孩子，”他的母亲说，“只要拿了一半的坚果就够了，这样你就可以很容易地把手伸出来了。那么也许下次你可能会得到更多的榛子。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: ENGLISH VARIANTS OF CINDERELLA

paragraphs.text: _Remarks._--In going over these various versions, the first and perhaps most striking thing that comes out is the substantial agreement of the variants in each _language_. The English--_i.e._, Scotch, variants go together; the Gaelic ones agree to differ from the English. I can best display this important agreement and difference by the accompanying two tables, which give, in parallel columns, Miss Cox's abstracts of her tabulations, in which each incident is shortly given in technical phraseology. It is practically impossible to use the long tabulations for comparative purposes without some such shorthand.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 备注——在审查这些不同的版本时，首先出现的也许是最引人注目的事情是每种语言的变体的基本一致。英语（即苏格兰威士忌）的变体是在一起的；盖尔人同意与英国人不同。我可以通过随附的两个表格最好地展示这一重要的一致和差异，这两个表格在平行的列中给出了考克斯小姐的表格摘要，其中每个事件都简短地用技术术语给出了。如果没有这样的速记，实际上不可能将长列表用于比较目的。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: TOM THUMB--III

paragraphs.text: Then Tom's mother took him in her apron and ran with him to the house, but he was not hurt in the least.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后汤姆的妈妈把他围在围裙里，带着他跑进了房子，但他没有受到丝毫伤害。

paragraphs.text: As they were passing over the sea, Tom got away and fell into the water, where a great fish swallowed him at one mouthful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他们经过大海时，汤姆逃脱并掉进了水里，一条大鱼把他吞了下去。

paragraphs.text: Soon after this the fish was caught, and it was such a big one that it was sent at once to King Arthur.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久之后，这条鱼就被捕获了，而且这条鱼很大，所以它立即被送到了亚瑟王那里。

paragraphs.text: When the cook cut open the fish, out jumped Tom Thumb. Tom was brought before the king, and his story was told.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当厨师切开鱼时，大拇指汤姆跳了出来。汤姆被带到国王面前，并讲述了他的故事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: TRY THIS

paragraphs.text: This nonsense test must be worked out carefully or it may fool you. You will need only a small piece of paper for your answers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个无意义的测试必须仔细进行，否则它可能会欺骗你。您只需要一张小纸即可获得答案。

paragraphs.text: 1. If your name is Geraldine, or if you are not yet past 37 years of age, or
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 如果您的名字是 Geraldine，或者您尚未超过 37 岁，或者

paragraphs.text: sign your name anywhere on your paper. Then, if you have signed your name, never mind the second paragraph, but skip to the third.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在纸上的任意位置签上您的名字。然后，如果您签了名字，请不要介意第二段，而是跳到第三段。

paragraphs.text: 2. Rub out your name and write the name of the first president of the United States in its stead. Then take the remaining paragraph.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 擦掉你的名字并写上美国第一任总统的名字。然后取剩下的段落。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ISAAC WATTS

paragraphs.text: How doth the little busy bee Improve each shining hour, And gather honey all the day From every opening flower!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 忙碌的小蜜蜂如何改善每一个闪亮的时刻，并整天从每一朵开放的花朵中采集蜂蜜！

paragraphs.text: How skilfully she builds her cell, How neat she spreads the wax! And labors hard to store it well With the sweet food she makes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她建造她的牢房多么熟练，她涂蜡多么整齐！并努力地把它和她做的甜食一起储存起来。

paragraphs.text: In works of labor or of skill, I would be busy too; For Satan finds some mischief still For idle hands to do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 无论是体力工作还是技术工作，我也会很忙。因为撒旦仍然发现一些恶作剧可以让闲着的人去做。

paragraphs.text: In books, or work, or healthful play, Let my first years be past, That I may give for every day Some good account at last.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在书籍、工作或健康的游戏中， 让我的最初几年成为过去， 以便我最终可以为每一天提供一些好的记录。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Children's Hour, Volume 7: (The NISTER PICTURE-BOOKS are sold in this country by DUTTON.)

paragraphs.text: CALDECOTT, RANDOLPH--Toy Books. Warne, (4 vols.) $1.25 each. The separate stories are sold at $0.25 each, and comprise, among a large number, the following: The Farmer's Boy; A Frog He Would A-Wooing Go; Hey Diddle Diddle and Baby Bunting; The House That Jack Built; The Milk Maid; The Queen of Hearts; Ride a Cock Horse; Sing a Song of Sixpence; John Gilpin.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 兰道夫·卡尔德科特——玩具书籍。 Warne，（4 卷）每本 1.25 美元。这些独立的故事每篇售价为 0.25 美元，其中包括以下内容：《农夫的男孩》；《农夫的男孩》；一只青蛙，他会向他求爱；嘿迪德尔迪德尔和婴儿彩旗；杰克建造的房子；挤奶女工；红心皇后；骑公鸡马；唱一首六便士之歌；约翰·吉尔平.

paragraphs.text: Mother Hubbard; This Little Pig; Aladdin; Beauty and the Beast; Bluebeard; The Forty Thieves; The Frog Prince; Goody Two Shoes; Sleeping Beauty; The Fairy Ship; Baby's A B C. Lane, $0.25 each.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哈伯德妈妈；这只小猪；阿拉丁；美女和野兽;蓝胡子；四十大盗；青蛙王子；古迪两双鞋；睡美人;仙女船； Baby's A B C. Lane，每个 0.25 美元。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: English Fairy Tales: MOUSE AND MOUSER

paragraphs.text: CAT (_gruffly_). I'll wear' em and tear 'em, good body, good body. I'll wear 'em and tear 'em, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫（粗声地）。我会穿上它们，撕裂它们，好身体，好身体。我会穿上它们并撕裂它们，好身体。

paragraphs.text: CAT (_snarling_). The more meat you had, good body, good body, The more meat you had, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫（咆哮）。肉越多，身体越好，身体越好， 肉越多，身体越好。

paragraphs.text: MOUSE. I put it in the window to cool, my lady, I put it in the window to cool.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老鼠。我把它放在窗户上降温，女士，我把它放在窗户上降温。

paragraphs.text: CAT. (_sharply_). The faster you'd eat it, good body, good body, The faster you'd eat it, good body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 猫。 （尖锐地）。吃得越快，身体好，身体好， 吃得越快，身体好。

paragraphs.text: MOUSE (_timidly_). The cat came and ate it, my lady, my lady, The cat came and ate it, my lady.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老鼠（胆怯地）。猫来了，吃了它，我的女士，我的女士， 猫来了，吃了它，我的女士。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE SHEEP

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had been hurt in a fight with a Bear. He was unable to move and could not satisfy his hunger and thirst. A Sheep passed by near his hiding place, and the Wolf called to him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼在与一只熊的战斗中受伤了。他无法动弹，也无法满足自己的饥渴。一只羊从他藏身的地方附近经过，狼呼唤着他。

paragraphs.text: "Please fetch me a drink of water," he begged, "that might give me strength enough so I can get me some solid food."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “请给我喝点水，”他恳求道，“这可能会给我足够的力量，这样我就可以吃点固体食物了。”

paragraphs.text: "Solid food!" said the Sheep. "That means me, I suppose. If I should bring you a drink, it would only serve to wash me down your throat. Don't talk to me about a drink!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “固体食物！”羊说。 “我想，那指的是我。如果我给你拿一杯饮料，那只会让我咽下去。别跟我谈论饮料！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: 2. GREAT SMALL

paragraphs.text: Complete the exercise by selecting the opposites from each remaining group, and writing them after the proper figure on your paper. When you have finished put down your pencil and wait quietly for the others.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从剩下的每一组中选择相反的图形，并将它们写在纸上正确的图形后面，以完成练习。当你完成后，放下铅笔，静静地等待其他人。

paragraphs.text: 1. HIGH (sky, low, above, deep). 2. GREAT (less, large, small, beautiful). 3. HILL (mountain, valley, high, river). 4. MANY (few, more, plenty, less). 5. GRADUAL (quick, slowly, sudden, degree). 6. WOUND (sword, nurse, heal, bind). 7. LIGHT (bright, sun, shadow, darkness). 8. STRAIGHT (long, uneven, twist, crooked). 9. LAND (plain, water, farm, river). 10. SPRING (fall, cool, October, green).
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 高（天空、低、上方、深）。 2. 伟大（少、大、小、漂亮）。 3. HILL（山、谷、高、河）。 4.很多（少、多、多、少）。 5. 渐进（快、慢、突然、程度）。 6.伤口（剑、护士、治愈、捆绑）。 7. 光（明亮、阳光、阴影、黑暗）。 8. 直（长、参差不齐、扭曲、弯曲）。 9. 土地（平原、水域、农场、河流）。 10. 春天（秋天，凉爽，十月，绿色）。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 6. THE DOVE AND THE ANT

paragraphs.text: Once a little Ant went down to the river to drink. He fell into the water and began to drown. Just then a Dove, perched on a tree, saw him and quickly dropped down a leaf, which served as a little boat on which the Ant sailed safely to the shore. "Thank you," said the Ant as he shook his wet feet, "I shall not forget this." Next day a Hunter was aiming his bow and arrow straight at the Dove when the Ant bit his foot, making the man jump, and the Dove flew away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只小蚂蚁到河边去喝水。他掉进水里，开始淹死。就在这时，一只栖息在树上的鸽子看到了他，很快就从一片叶子上掉了下来，这片叶子成了一只小船，蚂蚁乘着它安全地驶到了岸边。 “谢谢你，”蚂蚁摇着湿漉漉的脚说道，“我不会忘记这一点。”第二天，一位猎人正用弓箭瞄准鸽子，蚂蚁咬了他的脚，使猎人跳了起来，鸽子飞走了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND HIS SHADOW

paragraphs.text: A Wolf left his lair one evening in fine spirits and an excellent appetite. As he ran, the setting sun cast his shadow far out on the ground, and it looked as if the wolf were a hundred times bigger than he really was.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，一只狼离开了他的巢穴，心情很好，胃口也很好。当他奔跑的时候，夕阳将他的影子远远地投射在地上，看起来狼比他实际的体型大了一百倍。

paragraphs.text: "Why," exclaimed the Wolf proudly, "see how big I am! Fancy _me_ running away from a puny Lion! I'll show him who is fit to be king, he or I."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哎呀，”狼骄傲地喊道，“看看我有多大！想象一下我从一只弱小的狮子身边逃跑吧！我会让他知道谁适合当国王，是他还是我。”

paragraphs.text: Just then an immense shadow blotted him out entirely, and the next instant a Lion struck him down with a single blow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，一道巨大的阴影将他完全遮蔽，下一瞬间，一只狮子一击将他击倒。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: We built a ship upon the stairs All made of the back-bedroom chairs, And filled it full of sofa pillows To go a-sailing on the billows.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们在楼梯上造了一艘船，全是后卧室的椅子，里面装满了沙发枕头，要在波涛上航行。

paragraphs.text: We took a saw and several nails, And water in the nursery pails; And Tom said, "Let us also take An apple and a slice of cake;"-- Which was enough for Tom and me To go a-sailing on, till tea.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们拿了一把锯子和几颗钉子，还有苗圃里的水；汤姆说：“我们也带一个苹果和一块蛋糕吧；”——这足以让汤姆和我继续航行，直到喝茶。

paragraphs.text: We sailed along for days and days, And had the very best of plays; But Tom fell out and hurt his knee, So there was no one left but me.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们航行了几天又几天， 并享受了最好的表演；但是汤姆摔倒了，伤到了膝盖，所以除了我以外就没有人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: A GOOD PLAY

paragraphs.text: We built a ship upon the stairs, All made of the back-bedroom chairs, And filled it full of sofa pillows, To go a-sailing on the billows.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们在楼梯上造了一艘船，全部用后卧室的椅子组成，里面装满了沙发枕头，要在波涛上航行。

paragraphs.text: We took a saw and several nails, And water in the nursery pails; And Tom said, "Let us also take An apple and a slice of cake,"-- Which was enough for Tom and me To go a-sailing on, till tea.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们拿了一把锯子和几颗钉子，还有苗圃里的水；汤姆说：“我们也带一个苹果和一块蛋糕吧。”——这足以让汤姆和我继续航行，直到喝茶。

paragraphs.text: We sailed along for days and days, And had the very best of plays; But Tom fell out and hurt his knee, So there was no one left but me.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们航行了几天又几天， 并享受了最好的表演；但是汤姆摔倒了，伤到了膝盖，所以除了我以外就没有人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: Ella Higginson

paragraphs.text: I know a place where the sun is like gold, And the cherry blooms burst with snow; And down underneath is the loveliest nook, Where the four-leaf clovers grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我知道一个地方，阳光如金，樱花盛开，雪花缭绕；下面是最可爱的角落，四叶草生长的地方。

paragraphs.text: One leaf is for hope, and one is for faith, And one is for love, you know; But God put another in for luck-- If you search, you will find where they grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一片叶子代表希望，一片叶子代表信仰，一片叶子代表爱，你知道；但上帝为了好运而安排了另一个——如果你寻找，你就会找到它们生长的地方。

paragraphs.text: But you must have hope, and you must have faith; You must love and be strong; and so, If you work, if you wait, you will find the place Where the four-leaf clovers grow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但你必须有希望，你必须有信心；你必须爱并坚强；所以，只要你努力，如果你等待，你就会找到四叶草生长的地方。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: JUPITER AND THE MONKEY

paragraphs.text: There was once a baby show among the Animals in the forest. Jupiter provided the prize. Of course all the proud mammas from far and near brought their babies. But none got there earlier than Mother Monkey. Proudly she presented her baby among the other contestants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 森林里的动物们曾经举办过一场婴儿表演。木星提供了奖品。当然，来自四面八方的骄傲妈妈们都带着她们的孩子来了。但没有人比猴妈妈更早到达那里。她自豪地将自己的孩子展示在其他参赛者中。

paragraphs.text: As you can imagine, there was quite a laugh when the Animals saw the ugly flat-nosed, hairless, pop-eyed little creature.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你可以想象，当动物们看到这个丑陋的扁鼻子、无毛、睁大眼睛的小生物时，大家都大笑起来。

paragraphs.text: "Laugh if you will," said the Mother Monkey. "Though Jupiter may not give him the prize, I know that he is the prettiest, the sweetest, the dearest darling in the world."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果你愿意的话，就笑吧。”猴妈妈说。 “虽然朱庇特可能不会给他奖品，但我知道他是世界上最漂亮、最可爱、最可爱的宝贝。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: A FAIRY

paragraphs.text: Ralph Waldo Emerson (1803-1882) is America's greatest spiritual teacher. His essays, such as "Self-Reliance" and "The American Scholar," are his chief claim to fame. The two brief poems given here are well known. "Fable" should be studied along with No. 236, since they emphasize the same lesson that size is after all a purely relative matter. "Concord Hymn" is a splendidly dignified expression of the debt of gratitude we owe to the memory of those who made our country possible. Of course no reader will fail to notice the famous last two lines of the first stanza.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 拉尔夫·沃尔多·爱默生（1803-1882）是美国最伟大的精神导师。他的文章，如《自力更生》和《美国学者》，是他成名的主要原因。这里给出的两首短诗是众所周知的。 《寓言》应该与第236号一起研究，因为它们强调同样的教训，即大小毕竟是纯粹相对的问题。 《协和赞美诗》以庄严而庄严的方式表达了我们对那些使我们的国家成为可能的人们的感激之情。当然，没有读者不会注意到第一节著名的最后两行。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: THE MAN AND THE SATYR

paragraphs.text: A Man and a Satyr having struck up an acquaintance, sat down together to eat. The day being wintry and cold, the Man put his fingers to his mouth and blew upon them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个人和一个色狼认识了，坐在一起吃饭。那天天气寒冷，男人把手指放在嘴边，对着手指吹气。

paragraphs.text: In a little while some hot food was placed before them, and the Man, raising the dish to his mouth, again blew upon it. "And what's the meaning of that, now?" said the Satyr.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不一会儿，一些热腾腾的食物就摆在了他们面前，男人把盘子举到嘴边，又对着它吹了一口气。 “那现在这有什么意义呢？”萨特说。

paragraphs.text: "Nay, then," said the Satyr, "from this moment I renounce your friendship, for I will have nothing to do with one who blows hot and cold with the same mouth."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，那么，”萨特说，“从这一刻起，我断绝了你的友谊，因为我不会和一个用同一张嘴忽冷忽热的人有任何关系。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: TOM THUMB--IV

paragraphs.text: The king grew very fond of Tom and his wise sayings. He took Tom with him wherever he went.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王非常喜欢汤姆和他的智慧名言。无论他走到哪里，他都带着汤姆。

paragraphs.text: If it began to rain, Tom would creep into the king's pocket and sleep until the rain was over.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果开始下雨，汤姆就会爬进国王的口袋里睡觉，直到雨停。

paragraphs.text: The king and queen never tired of seeing him ride his queer little horse and bravely wave his sword.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王和王后乐此不疲地看着他骑着那匹奇怪的小马，勇敢地挥舞着剑。

paragraphs.text: The king ran to help poor Tom, but the little mouse was dead, and Tom was scratched and bitten.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王跑去帮助可怜的汤姆，但小老鼠死了，汤姆也被抓伤和咬伤。

paragraphs.text: No indeed! he was soon well again, and fought many brave battles and did many brave deeds to please the king.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 确实不！他很快就康复了，并进行了许多英勇的战斗，做了许多英勇的事迹来取悦国王。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ELIZA LEE FOLLEN

paragraphs.text: O look at the moon! She is shining up there; O mother, she looks Like a lamp in the air.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哦，看月亮！她在那儿闪闪发光；哦，母亲，她看起来就像空中的一盏灯。

paragraphs.text: Last week she was smaller, And shaped like a bow; But now she's grown bigger, And round as an O.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 上周她变小了，形状像弓；但现在她长大了，圆得像个O形。

paragraphs.text: Pretty moon, pretty moon, How you shine on the door, And make it all bright On my nursery floor!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 美丽的月亮，美丽的月亮，你如何照在门上，让我的婴儿房地板上都明亮起来！

paragraphs.text: You shine on my playthings, And show me their place, And I love to look up At your pretty bright face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你照耀着我的玩具，让我看到它们的位置，我喜欢抬头看着你漂亮明亮的脸。

paragraphs.text: And there is a star Close by you, and maybe That small twinkling star Is your little baby.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你附近有一颗星星，也许那颗闪烁的小星星就是你的小宝贝。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Biography. James Whitcomb Riley (1852-1916) was a native of Indiana. Most of his life was spent in Indianapolis, where he lived on the quiet Lockerbie Street which he celebrated in one of his poems. He is called "The Hoosier Poet." He wrote several volumes of poems, the first being The Old Swimmin' Hole and 'Leven More Poems. The school children of Indiana celebrated Riley's birthday on October 7, 1911, and have each year since made this a festival day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 传记。詹姆斯·惠特科姆·莱利（James Whitcomb Riley，1852-1916 年）是印第安纳州人。他一生的大部分时间是在印第安纳波利斯度过的，他住在安静的洛克比街，他在一首诗中歌颂了这条街。他被称为“印第安人诗人”。他写了几卷诗，第一本是《老游泳洞》和《更多诗》。 1911 年 10 月 7 日，印第安纳州的学童们庆祝了莱利的生日，此后每年都将这一天定为节日。

paragraphs.text: Discussion. Because of the many figurative expressions used in this selection it should be read and studied in class.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论。由于本选集使用了许多比喻表达，因此应在课堂上阅读和研究。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 8. LET CHILDREN REPRODUCE YOUR STORIES

paragraphs.text: Children should be given an opportunity to tell and retell the stories heard. Children like to create, and whether it be with sand, wood, or words, the underlying processes are the same. For a child to retell a story means that he enters into the spirit of it, that he sees clearly the mental picture, that he feels the atmosphere and life of the story. In this way imagination, memory, language, and reason are enriched and, at the same time, the ethical principle of the story is more clearly impressed on the child's mind, to be assimilated at pleasure.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 应该给孩子们讲述和复述听到的故事的机会。孩子们喜欢创造，无论是用沙子、木头还是文字，其基本过程都是一样的。对孩子来说，复述一个故事意味着他进入了故事的精神，他看清了心理画面，他感受到了故事的气氛和生命。这样，想象力、记忆力、语言和理性都得到了丰富，同时故事的伦理原则也更加清晰地印在孩子的脑海里，并能愉快地被吸收。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: CHRISTINA G. ROSSETTI

paragraphs.text: The city mouse lives in a house;-- The garden mouse lives in a bower; He's friendly with the frogs and toads, And sees the pretty plants in flower.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 城市老鼠住在房子里；——花园老鼠住在凉亭里；——他对青蛙和蟾蜍很友好，并且看到美丽的植物开花。

paragraphs.text: The city mouse eats bread and cheese;-- The garden mouse eats what he can; We will not grudge him seeds and stocks, Poor little timid furry man.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 城市老鼠吃面包和奶酪；——花园老鼠吃他能吃的；——我们不会吝惜他的种子和股票，可怜的胆怯的毛茸茸的小男人。

paragraphs.text: The most famous use of this fable in literature is found in the _Satires_ of the great Roman poet, Horace (B.C. 65-8). He is regarded as one of the most polished of writers, and the ancient world's most truthful painter of social life and manners. Horace had a country seat among the Sabine hills to which he could retire from the worries and distractions of the world. His delight in his Sabine farm is shown clearly in his handling of the story. The passage is a part of Book II, Satire 6, and is in Conington's translation. Some well-known appearances of this same fable in English poetry may be found in Prior and Montagu's _City Mouse and Country Mouse_ and in Pope's _Imitations of Horace_.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个寓言在文学中最著名的运用出现在伟大的罗马诗人贺拉斯（Horace，公元前 65-8 年）的讽刺诗中。他被认为是最优秀的作家之一，也是古代世界最真实地描绘社会生活和礼仪的画家。贺拉斯在萨宾山间有一座乡间别墅，在那里他可以远离世俗的忧虑和干扰。他对萨宾农场的喜悦在他对故事的处理中清晰可见。这段文字是第二册讽刺书 6 的一部分，由科宁顿翻译。这个寓言在英国诗歌中的一些著名出现可以在普赖尔和蒙塔古的《城市老鼠和乡村老鼠》以及蒲柏的《模仿贺拉斯》中找到。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: CHARLES KINGSLEY

paragraphs.text: Three fishers went sailing out into the west,-- Out into the west as the sun went down; Each thought of the woman who loved him the best, And the children stood watching them out of the town; For men must work, and women must weep; And there's little to earn, and many to keep, Though the harbor bar be moaning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 三个渔夫向西航行，——太阳落山时向西航行；每个人都想起最爱他的女人， 孩子们站着看着他们出城；因为男人必须工作，女人必须哭泣；虽然港口酒吧在呻吟，但赚的钱很少，留的却很多。
