paragraphs.text: The Tortoise said she would not say a word; she would not so much as move her mouth; and she was very grateful. So the Ducks brought a strong little stick and took hold of the ends, while the Tortoise bit firmly on the middle. Then the two Ducks rose slowly in the air and flew away with their burden.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌龟说她不会说话。她连嘴都不敢动。她非常感激。于是，鸭子拿了一根坚固的小棍子，抓住了两端，而乌龟则牢牢地咬住了中间。然后两只鸭子慢慢地升到空中，带着它们的担子飞走了。

paragraphs.text: When they were above the treetops, the Tortoise wanted to say, "How high we are!" But she remembered, and kept still. When they passed the church steeple she wanted to say, "What is that which shines?" But she remembered, and held her peace. Then they came over the village square, and the people looked up and saw them. "Look at the Ducks carrying a Tortoise!" they shouted; and every one ran to look. The Tortoise wanted to say, "What business is it of yours?" But she didn't. Then she heard the people shout, "Isn't it strange! Look at it! Look!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他们飞到树顶上时，乌龟想说：“我们有多高啊！”但她记住了，并保持沉默。当他们经过教堂的尖塔时，她想说：“那闪闪发光的东西是什么？”但她记得，并保持沉默。然后他们来到了村庄广场上，人们抬头看到了他们。 “看鸭子驮着乌龟！”他们喊道；每个人都跑去看。乌龟想说：关你什么事？但她没有。然后她就听见人们喊道：“奇怪吗？快看！快看！”

paragraphs.text: The Tortoise forgot everything except that she wanted to say, "Hush, you foolish people!" She opened her mouth,-- and fell to the ground. And that was the end of the Tortoise.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌龟忘记了一切，只记得说：“嘘，你们这些愚蠢的人们！”她张开嘴——然后倒在了地上。这就是乌龟的结局。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: Stupid's Cries

paragraphs.text: There was once a little boy, and his mother sent him to buy a sheep's head and pluck; afraid he should forget it, the lad kept saying all the way along:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一个小男孩，他的妈妈派他去买一个羊头和拔毛。小伙子生怕自己忘记了，一路上一直说：

paragraphs.text: Trudging along, he came to a stile; but in getting over he fell and hurt himself, and beginning to blubber, forgot what he was sent for. So he stood a little while to consider: at last he thought he recollected it, and began to repeat:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他艰难地前行，来到了一个栅栏前。但在克服的过程中，他摔倒了，受伤了，开始哭泣，忘记了他被派去做什么。于是他站了一会儿想了想，最后他以为自己记起来了，于是开始重复道：

paragraphs.text: Away he went again, and came to where a man had a pain in his liver, bawling out:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他又走了，来到一个人肝部疼痛的地方，大声喊道：

paragraphs.text: The youngster strode along, uttering these words, till he reached a field where a hind was sowing wheat:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 年轻人一边大步走着，一边说着这些话，直到他来到一片田野，那里有一只母鹿正在播种麦子：

paragraphs.text: Off the child scampered with these words in his mouth till he reached a churchyard and met a funeral, but he went on with his:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 孩子嘴里念叨着这些话，一路蹦蹦跳跳，直到到达教堂墓地并遇到葬礼，但他继续说：

paragraphs.text: Away went the boy, and met a dog and a cat going to be hung, but his cry rang out:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 男孩走了，遇见一只狗和一只猫要被吊死，但他的叫声却响起：

paragraphs.text: "A dog and a cat agoing to be hung! A dog and a cat agoing to be hung!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一只狗和一只猫即将被绞死！一只狗和一只猫即将被绞死！”

paragraphs.text: This the poor fellow did, till he overtook a man and a woman going to be married. "Oh! oh!" he shouted:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个可怜的家伙就这么做了，直到他追上了一对即将结婚的男人和女人。 “噢！噢！”他喊道：

paragraphs.text: "A dog and a cat agoing to be hung! A dog and a cat agoing to be hung!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一只狗和一只猫即将被绞死！一只狗和一只猫即将被绞死！”

paragraphs.text: The man was enraged, as we may well think, gave him many a thump, and ordered him to repeat:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们很可能会想到，这个人很生气，狠狠地捶了他一下，并命令他重复一遍：

paragraphs.text: This he did, jogging along, till he came to two labourers who had fallen into a ditch. The lad kept bawling out:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他这样做了，一路小跑，直到找到两个掉进沟里的工人。小伙子不停地叫喊着：

paragraphs.text: This vexed one of the folk so sorely that he used all his strength, scrambled out, beat the crier, and told him to say.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这让其中一个人非常恼火，他使出全身的力气，爬了出来，殴打了那个喊叫的人，并让他说出来。

paragraphs.text: "The one is out, I wish the other was! The one is out, I wish the other was!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一个已经出来了，我希望另一个也出来了！那个已经出来了，我希望另一个也出来了！”

paragraphs.text: On went young 'un till he found a fellow with only one eye; but he kept up his song:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 年轻的他继续前行，直到找到一个只有一只眼睛的家伙；但他继续唱着他的歌：

paragraphs.text: "The one is out, I wish the other was! The one is out, I wish the other was!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一个已经出来了，我希望另一个也出来了！那个已经出来了，我希望另一个也出来了！”

paragraphs.text: "The one side gives good light, I wish the other did! The one side gives good light, I wish the other did!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一侧光线很好，我希望另一侧也能这样！一侧光线很好，我希望另一侧也能这样！”

paragraphs.text: So he did, to be sure, till he came to a house, one side of which was on fire. The people here thought it was he who had set the place a-blazing, and straightway put him in prison. The end was, the judge put on his black cap, and condemned him to die.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可以肯定的是，他确实这么做了，直到他来到一所房子，房子的一侧着火了。这里的人们认为是他放火烧了这里，并立即把他关进了监狱。最终，法官给他戴上黑帽子，判处他死刑。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE BOY AND THE ROBBERS

paragraphs.text: In Persia, when Cyrus the Great was king, boys were taught to tell the truth. This was one of their first lessons at home and at school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在波斯，居鲁士大帝统治时期，男孩们被教导说实话。这是他们在家里和学校的第一堂课之一。

paragraphs.text: When Otanes was twelve years old, his parents wished to send him to a distant city to study in a famous school that was there. It would be a long journey and a dangerous one. So it was arranged that the boy should travel with a small company of merchants who were going to the same place. "Good-by, Otanes! Be always brave and truthful," said his father. "Farewell, my child! Love that which is beautiful. Despise that which is base," said his mother.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯十二岁时，他的父母希望送他到一个遥远的城市去那里的一所著名学校学习。这将是一段漫长而危险的旅程。于是安排这个男孩和一小群要去同一个地方的商人一起旅行。 “再见，奥塔内斯！永远勇敢和诚实，”他的父亲说。 “永别了，我的孩子！热爱美丽的事物，鄙视卑鄙的事物，”他的母亲说道。

paragraphs.text: The little company began its long journey. Some of the men rode on camels, some on horses. They went but slowly, for the sun was hot and the way was rough.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这家小公司开始了漫长的旅程。这些人有的骑骆驼，有的骑马。他们走得很慢，因为太阳很热，路又崎岖不平。

paragraphs.text: Suddenly, towards evening, a band of robbers swooped down upon them. The merchants were not fighting men. They could do nothing but give up all their goods and money.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 傍晚时分，突然，一群强盗向他们袭来。商人不是战士。他们别无选择，只能放弃所有的财产和金钱。

paragraphs.text: "Well, boy, what have you got?" asked one of the robbers, as he pulled Otanes from his horse.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “嗯，孩子，你有什么？”一名强盗一边问道，一边把奥塔内斯拉下马。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, well! You can't make me believe that," said the robber; and he hurried away to rob one of the rich merchants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦，好吧！你不能让我相信这一点，”强盗说。他急忙跑去抢劫一位富商。

paragraphs.text: "You are a brave lad to be joking with robbers" said the man; and he also hurried on to a more promising field.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你真是一个勇敢的小伙子，竟然能和强盗开玩笑。”那人说道。而他也匆匆走向了更有前途的领域。

paragraphs.text: At length the chief of the band called to Otanes and said, "Young fellow, have you anything worth taking?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，乐队队长对奥塔内斯喊道：“年轻人，你有什么值得拿走的东西吗？”

paragraphs.text: Otanes answered, "I have already told two of your men that I have forty pieces of gold in my hat. But they wouldn't believe me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯回答说：“我已经告诉你们的两个手下，我的帽子里有四十块金子。但他们不相信我。”

paragraphs.text: "Why did you tell us where to find it?" he asked. "No one would have thought that a child like you had gold about him."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你为什么告诉我们在哪里可以找到它？”他问道。 “没有人会想到像你这样的孩子身上竟然有金子。”

paragraphs.text: "If I had answered your questions differently, I should have told a lie," said Otanes; "and none but cowards tell lies"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯说：“如果我以不同的方式回答你的问题，我就会撒谎。” “除了胆小鬼，没有人会说谎”

paragraphs.text: The robber chief was struck by this answer. He thought of the number of times that he himself had been a coward. Then he said, "You are a brave boy, and you may keep your gold. Here it is. Mount your horse, and my own men will ride with you and see that you reach the end of your journey in safety."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 强盗头子被这个答案震惊了。他多次想起自己也曾是个胆小鬼。然后他说：“你是个勇敢的孩子，你可以保留你的金子。它就在这儿。骑上你的马，我的人会和你一起骑马，确保你安全到达旅程的终点​​。”

paragraphs.text: Otanes, in time, became one of the famous men of his country. He was the advisor and friend of two of the kings who succeeded Cyrus.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯最终成为了他的国家的名人之一。他是继居鲁士之后的两位国王的顾问和朋友。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: THE CAT AND THE MOUSE

paragraphs.text: "No," said the cat, "I'll not give you your tail till you go to the cow and fetch me some milk."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，”猫说，“除非你去奶牛那里给我拿些牛奶，否则我不会把尾巴给你。”

paragraphs.text: "Pray, cow, give me milk, that I may give cat milk, that cat may give me my own tail again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “祈祷，牛，给我牛奶，这样我就可以给猫牛奶，那只猫可以再次给我自己的尾巴。”

paragraphs.text: "No," said the cow, "I will give you no milk till you go to the farmer and fetch me some hay."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，”奶牛说，“除非你去农夫那里给我拿些干草，否则我不会给你牛奶。”

paragraphs.text: "Pray, farmer, give me hay, that I may give cow hay, that cow may give me milk, that I may give cat milk, that cat may give me my own tail again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “祈祷，农夫，给我干草，这样我就可以给牛干草，那头牛可以给我牛奶，我可以给猫牛奶，那只猫可以再次给我自己的尾巴。”

paragraphs.text: "No," said the farmer, "I'll give you no hay till you go to the butcher and fetch me some meat."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，”农夫说，“除非你去屠夫给我拿点肉，否则我不会给你干草。”

paragraphs.text: "Pray, butcher, give me meat, that I may give farmer meat, that farmer may give me hay, that I may give cow hay, that cow may give me milk, that I may give cat milk, that cat may give me my own tail again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “祈祷，屠夫，给我肉，这样我可以给农民肉，农民可以给我干草，我可以给牛干草，那头牛可以给我牛奶，我可以给猫牛奶，那只猫可以再次给我自己的尾巴。”

paragraphs.text: "No," said the butcher, "I'll give you no meat till you go to the baker and fetch me some bread."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，”屠夫说，“除非你去面包店给我拿些面包，否则我不会给你肉。”

paragraphs.text: "Pray, baker, give me bread, that I may give butcher bread, that butcher may give me meat, that I may give farmer meat, that farmer may give me hay, that I may give cow hay, that cow may give me milk, that I may give cat milk, that cat may give me my own tail again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “祈祷，面包师，给我面包，我可以给屠夫面包，屠夫可以给我肉，我可以给农民肉，农民可以给我干草，我可以给牛干草，牛可以给我牛奶，我可以给猫牛奶，猫可以再次给我自己的尾巴。”

paragraphs.text: "Yes," said the baker, "I'll give you some bread, But if you eat my meal, I'll cut off your head."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是的，”面包师说，“我会给你一些面包，但如果你吃了我的饭，我就会砍下你的头。”

paragraphs.text: Then the baker gave mouse bread, and mouse gave butcher bread, and butcher gave mouse meat, and mouse gave farmer meat, and farmer gave mouse hay, and mouse gave cow hay, and cow gave mouse milk, and mouse gave cat milk, and cat gave mouse her own tail again.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后面包师给了老鼠面包，老鼠给了屠夫面包，屠夫给了老鼠肉，老鼠给了农民肉，农民给了老鼠干草，老鼠给了牛干草，牛给了老鼠牛奶，老鼠给了猫牛奶，猫又给了老鼠自己的尾巴。

paragraphs.text: The following story is in the most familiar version of Halliwell's collection. Another much-used form of the story may be found in Lang's _Green Fairy Book_, in which the pigs are distinctly characterized and given the names of Browny, Whitey, and Blacky. Jacobs uses the Halliwell version in his _English Fairy Tales_, but prefixes to it an opening formula which seems to have been much in use by old story-tellers as a way of beginning almost any oral story for children:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 以下故事出自哈利韦尔作品集中最熟悉的版本。这个故事的另一种常用形式可以在朗的《绿色童话书》中找到，书中对猪进行了清晰的描述，并给它们起了名字：布朗尼、怀特和布莱基。雅各布斯在他的《英国童话故事》中使用了哈利韦尔版本，但在其前面加上了一个开头公式，这个开头公式似乎被老讲故事的人广泛使用，作为几乎所有儿童口头故事的开头方式：

paragraphs.text: "Once upon a time when pigs spoke rhyme And monkeys chewed tobacco, And hens took snuff to make them tough, And ducks went quack, quack, quack, O!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “从前，猪会说押韵，猴子嚼烟草，母鸡吸鼻烟使它们变得坚韧，鸭子嘎嘎嘎嘎地叫，哦！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: MERCURY AND THE WOODMAN

paragraphs.text: A poor Woodman was cutting down a tree near the edge of a deep pool in the forest. It was late in the day and the Woodman was tired. He had been working since sunrise and his strokes were not so sure as they had been early that morning. Thus it happened that the axe slipped and flew out of his hands into the pool.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个贫穷的樵夫正在森林里一个深池边砍伐一棵树。天色已晚，樵夫也累了。他从日出起就一直在工作，他的中风并不像那天清晨那么确定。结果斧子滑倒了，从他手里飞进了水池里。

paragraphs.text: The Woodman was in despair. The axe was all he possessed with which to make a living, and he had not money enough to buy a new one. As he stood wringing his hands and weeping, the god Mercury suddenly appeared and asked what the trouble was. The Woodman told what had happened, and straightway the kind Mercury dived into the pool. When he came up again he held a wonderful golden axe.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 樵夫陷入了绝望。这把斧子是他谋生的唯一工具，而且他没有足够的钱去买一把新的。当他站着绞着双手哭泣时，墨丘利神突然出现并询问出了什么问题。樵夫讲述了发生的事情，善良的水星立刻跳进了池子里。当他再次出现时，他手里拿着一把精美的金斧头。

paragraphs.text: Mercury laid the golden axe on the bank and sprang back into the pool. This time he brought up an axe of silver, but the Woodman declared again that his axe was just an ordinary one with a wooden handle.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 墨丘利将金斧头放在岸边，然后跳回池中。这次他拿出一把银斧头，但樵夫又说他的斧头只是一把普通的木柄斧头。

paragraphs.text: Mercury dived down for the third time, and when he came up again he had the very axe that had been lost.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 墨丘利第三次潜入水中，当他再次上来时，他拿到了丢失的斧头。

paragraphs.text: The poor Woodman was very glad that his axe had been found and could not thank the kind god enough. Mercury was greatly pleased with the Woodman's honesty.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可怜的樵夫很高兴他的斧子被发现了，他对仁慈的上帝感激不尽。水星对樵夫的诚实非常满意。

paragraphs.text: "I admire your honesty," he said, "and as a reward you may have all three axes, the gold and the silver as well as your own."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我钦佩你的诚实，”他说，“作为奖励，你可以获得所有三把斧子，金子和银子，还有你自己的。”

paragraphs.text: The happy Woodman returned to his home with his treasures, and soon the story of his good fortune was known to everybody in the village. Now there were several Woodmen in the village who believed that they could easily win the same good fortune. They hurried out into the woods, one here, one there, and hiding their axes in the bushes, pretended they had lost them. Then they wept and wailed and called on Mercury to help them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 快乐的樵夫带着他的财宝回到了家，很快他的好运气的故事就被村里的每个人都知道了。现在村里有好几个樵夫，他们相信自己也能轻易获得同样的好运。他们急忙跑进树林里，这里一把，那里一把，把斧子藏在灌木丛里，假装丢失了。然后他们哭泣哀号，请求水星帮助他们。

paragraphs.text: And indeed, Mercury did appear, first to this one, then to that. To each one he showed an axe of gold, and each one eagerly claimed it to be the one he had lost. But Mercury did not give them the golden axe. Oh no! Instead he gave them each a hard whack over the head with it and sent them home. And when they returned next day to look for their own axes, they were nowhere to be found.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 事实上，水星确实出现了，先是出现在这个，然后又出现在那个。他向每个人展示一把金斧子，每个人都急切地声称这是他失去的那把斧子。但水星并没有给他们金斧子。哦不！相反，他用它狠狠地敲了一下他们每个人的头，然后把他们送回家。当他们第二天返回寻找自己的斧头时，却找不到他们。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: A Narrow Escape

paragraphs.text: "At another time, when I was hunting in Ceylon, I was terrified to see a gigantic lion approaching, with the evident intention of devouring me. My gun was only loaded with bird-shot, and I had no other about me. The savage animal shook his head several times, uttered a loud roar, and prepared to spring. I turned to flee, and--my flesh creeps even now at the recollection of it--there, on the bank of a river that lay behind me, was a huge crocodile with his terrible jaws open ready to swallow me!
paragraphs.translation_zh: “还有一次，我在锡兰打猎，看到一头巨大的狮子正在逼近，显然是要吃掉我。我的枪里只有鸟弹，而我身上却没有其他东西。这只野蛮的动物摇了几下头，发出一声巨响，准备跳起来。我转身逃跑，然后——现在回想起来，我的毛骨悚然——在那里，在一条河边，在我身后，是一条巨大的鳄鱼，张开可怕的血盆大口，准备将我吞掉！

paragraphs.text: "Imagine, gentlemen, the horror of my situation--before me the lion, behind me the crocodile, on my left a rushing torrent, and on the right an abyss full of poisonous snakes! I gave myself up for lost, and fell to the ground in an almost fainting condition, expecting nothing better than to meet with a horrible death from one or the other of these terrible animals.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “先生们，想象一下我的处境有多恐怖吧——我前面是狮子，后面是鳄鱼，我的左边是急流，右边是充满毒蛇的深渊！我已经放弃了自己，几乎昏倒在地，除了这些可怕的动物中的一种或另一种可怕的死亡之外，没有什么比这更好的了。

paragraphs.text: "After waiting a few seconds I heard a violent noise, different from any that had fallen on my ears before. I ventured to raise my head, and what do you think had happened?
paragraphs.translation_zh: “等了几秒钟后，我听到了一阵剧烈的噪音，与之前落在我耳边的声音不同。我壮着胆子抬起头，你猜发生了什么？

paragraphs.text: "The lion had, in his eagerness, jumped clean over me into the crocodile's jaws; the head of the one stuck in the throat of the other, and they were struggling to free themselves. I quickly sprang to my feet, drew out my hunting-knife, and with one blow severed the lion's head. Then, with the butt-end of my gun, I rammed the head farther into the throat of the crocodile, and destroyed him by suffocation. The hide of the crocodile, which was exactly forty feet in length, I had stuffed, and it now forms one of the chief attractions in the museum at Amsterdam, where the superintendent relates the story to all spectators, with harrowing additions.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “那头狮子急切地从我身上跳到鳄鱼的嘴里，一个的头卡在了另一个的喉咙里，它们正在挣扎着挣脱。我迅速跳起来，拔出我的猎刀，一击砍下了狮子的头。然后，我用枪的枪托将头进一步撞入鳄鱼的喉咙，把他窒息而死。鳄鱼皮有四十英尺长，我已经把它做成了标本，现在它成为阿姆斯特丹博物馆的主要景点之一，馆长向所有观众讲述这个故事，并添加了一些令人痛心的内容。

paragraphs.text: "One of these is that the lion jumped right through the crocodile, but as soon as the head appeared, Monsieur the Famous Baron (as he is pleased to call me) cut it off, and three feet of the crocodile's tail as well, whereupon the crocodile turned round, snatched the knife out of my hand, and swallowed it so greedily that it pierced his heart and killed him!
paragraphs.translation_zh: “其中之一是，狮子直接从鳄鱼身上跳了过去，但头一出现，著名男爵先生（他很乐意这样称呼我）就把它砍了下来，鳄鱼的尾巴也砍掉了三英尺，鳄鱼随即转身，从我手中夺走了刀，贪婪地吞了下去，以至于刺穿了他的心脏，杀死了他！

paragraphs.text: "I need not tell you how annoyed I was by these exaggerations. In this age of doubt people who do not know me might possibly be led to disbelieve the real facts when they are mixed up with such absurd inventions.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我不用告诉你我对这些夸张的说法有多么恼火。在这个充满怀疑的时代，不认识我的人如果与这些荒谬的发明混在一起，可能会不相信真实的事实。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 1. THE BOY HERO OF HOLLAND

paragraphs.text: Once there was a good boy who had a kind-hearted mother. One afternoon she said: "Here, Peter, are some cakes I want you to take to the poor old blind man who is very ill, and who lives a mile and a half away from town. If you go quickly and do not stop to play, you will be home before it is dark." Peter took the cakes to the poor old blind man, who said, "You are a kind-hearted boy; thank your mother for me." Light-hearted because he had made the blind man happy, Peter was walking home when suddenly he noticed a little stream of water trickling through the great bank on the side of the road. This was in Holland, where much of the land is below the level of the sea, and where dikes are built by the people to keep back the sea. Every boy in Holland knows the danger of even a small leak in the dike. Peter understood at once that this tiny stream would soon make a large hole and the whole city would be flooded. In a moment he saw what he must do. He climbed down the side of the dike and thrust his chubby little hand and finger into the tiny hole and stopped the flowing of the water. Then he cried out for help, but no one heard him; no one came to help. It grew dark, and cold; he was hungry; his arm ached and it began to grow stiff and numb. He shouted again: "O mother! mother!" But his mother thought Peter must be spending the night with the blind man, and did not know of his danger. Peter thought how warm and cozy all at home were sleeping in their beds, and he said to himself, "I will not let them be drowned!" So that good boy stayed there all night long, holding back the water. Early next morning, a minister on his way to visit the sick, heard a groan, saw the boy, and called out to him, "What is the matter, my boy? Are you hurt? Why are you sitting there?" When Peter told him what he had done, the minister said, "I will hold my hand there while you run quickly to the town and get help." Very soon men came and repaired the leak in the dike, but all knew that Peter, by his courage and faithfulness, had saved the town of Haarlem that night.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一个好孩子，他有一个善良的妈妈。一天下午，她说：“彼得，这里有一些蛋糕，我想让你给那个可怜的盲人老人带去，他病得很重，住在离城镇一英里半的地方。如果你走得快，不停地玩，天黑之前你就能回家。”彼得把蛋糕拿给可怜的老盲人，老盲人说：“你是一个心地善良的孩子，替我感谢你的妈妈。”彼得因为让盲人高兴而心情轻松，他走在回家的路上，突然发现路边的大河岸上有一股小溪流淌。这是在荷兰，那里的大部分土地都在海平面以下，人们修建堤坝来阻挡海水。荷兰的每个男孩都知道堤坝哪怕是很小的泄漏也会带来危险。彼得立刻明白，这条小溪很快就会形成一个大洞，整个城市都会被淹没。刹那间，他明白了自己必须做什么。他爬下堤岸，把胖乎乎的小手和手指伸进小洞里，止住了水流。然后他大声呼救，但没有人听到他的声音。没有人来帮忙。天色渐暗，寒冷。他饿了；他的手臂疼痛，开始变得僵硬和麻木。他又喊道：“妈妈啊！妈妈！”但他的母亲认为彼得一定是和盲人一起过夜，并不知道他有危险。彼得想到家里的所有人都睡在床上是多么温暖和舒适，他对自己说：“我不会让他们被淹死的！”于是，那个好孩子就在那里守了一夜，挡住了水。第二天一早，一位牧师在去看望病人的路上，听到一声呻吟，看到了那个男孩，就对他喊道：“怎么了，我的孩子？你受伤了吗？你为什么坐在那里？”当彼得告诉他他所做的事情时，牧师说：“我会握住我的手，而你则赶快跑到镇上寻求帮助。”很快，人们就赶来修复了堤坝的漏洞，但所有人都知道彼得凭借他的勇气和忠诚，那天晚上拯救了哈勒姆镇。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: English Fairy Tales: THE CAULD LAD OF HILTON

paragraphs.text: At Hilton Hall, long years ago, there lived a Brownie that was the contrariest Brownie you ever knew. At night, after the servants had gone to bed, it would turn everything topsy-turvy, put sugar in the salt-cellars, pepper into the beer, and was up to all kinds of pranks. It would throw the chairs down, put tables on their backs, rake out fires, and do as much mischief as could be. But sometimes it would be in a good temper, and then!--"What's a Brownie?" you say. Oh, it's a kind of a sort of a Bogle, but it isn't so cruel as a Redcap! What! you don't know what's a Bogle or a Redcap! Ah, me! what's the world a-coming to? Of course a Brownie is a funny little thing, half man, half goblin, with pointed ears and hairy hide. When you bury a treasure, you scatter over it blood drops of a newly slain kid or lamb, or, better still, bury the animal with the treasure, and a Brownie will watch over it for you, and frighten everybody else away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多年前，在希尔顿大厅，住着一个布朗尼，这是你所知道的最相反的布朗尼。晚上，仆人上床睡觉后，它会把一切搞得天翻地覆，把糖放进盐窖里，把胡椒粉放进啤酒里，搞出各种恶作剧。它会把椅子扔倒，把桌子放在背上，扫火，并尽可能地恶作剧。但有时脾气很好，然后！——“什么是布朗尼？”你说。哦，它是一种博格尔，但它不像红帽那么残忍！什么！你不知道什么是博格尔或红帽！啊，我！世界将会怎样？当然，布朗尼是一种有趣的小东西，半人半妖，长着尖尖的耳朵和毛茸茸的皮肤。当你埋葬宝藏时，你可以在上面撒上刚被杀的小山羊或羔羊的血滴，或者更好的是，将动物与宝藏一起埋葬，布朗尼会为你看守它，并吓跑其他人。

paragraphs.text: Where was I? Well, as I was a-saying, the Brownie at Hilton Hall would play at mischief, but if the servants laid out for it a bowl of cream, or a knuckle cake spread with honey, it would clear away things for them, and make everything tidy in the kitchen. One night, however, when the servants had stopped up late, they heard a noise in the kitchen, and, peeping in, saw the Brownie swinging to and fro on the Jack chain, and saying:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我当时在哪儿？嗯，正如我刚才所说，希尔顿大厅的布朗尼蛋糕会恶作剧，但如果仆人为它准备一碗奶油，或者涂上蜂蜜的指节蛋糕，它就会为他们清理东西，让厨房里的一切都变得整洁。然而，有一天晚上，仆人们睡得很晚，听到厨房里有吵闹声，他们向里面一看，看到布朗尼在杰克链上来回摆动，说道：

paragraphs.text: "Woe's me! woe's me! The acorn's not yet Fallen from the tree, That's to grow the wood, That's to make the cradle, That's to rock the bairn, That's to grow to the man, That's to lay me. Woe's me! woe's me!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我真可怜！我真可怜！橡子还没有从树上掉下来，那是为了长出木头，那是为了制造摇篮，那是为了摇动婴儿，那是为了长成男人，那是为了生下我。我真可怜！我真可怜！”

paragraphs.text: So they took pity on the poor Brownie, and asked the nearest henwife what they should do to send it away. "That's easy enough," said the henwife, and told them that a Brownie that's paid for its service, in aught that's not perishable, goes away at once. So they made a cloak of Lincoln green, with a hood to it, and put it by the hearth and watched. They saw the Brownie come up, and seeing the hood and cloak, put them on, and frisk about, dancing on one leg and saying:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他们同情可怜的布朗尼，并询问最近的母鸡应该怎样做才能把它送走。 “这很容易，”母鸡主妇说，并告诉他们，一块付费的布朗尼蛋糕，只要是不易腐烂的东西，就会立刻消失。于是他们用林肯绿色做了一件斗篷，上面还戴着兜帽，放在炉边看着。他们看到布朗尼走了过来，看到了兜帽和斗篷，就把它们戴上，然后跳来跳去，单腿跳舞，说：

paragraphs.text: "I've taken your cloak, I've taken your hood; The Cauld Lad of Hilton will do no more good."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我拿走了你的斗篷，我拿走了你的兜帽；希尔顿的那个小伙子不会再做任何好事了。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: HOW A PRINCE LEARNED TO READ

paragraphs.text: A thousand years ago boys and girls did not learn to read. Books were very scarce and very precious, and only a few men could read them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一千年前，男孩和女孩都没有学会读书。书籍非常稀缺而且非常珍贵，只有少数人能读懂它们。

paragraphs.text: Each book was written with a pen or a brush. The pictures were painted by hand, and some of them were very beautiful. A good book would sometimes cost as much as a good house.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每本书都是用钢笔或毛笔写的。这些图画是手绘的，有些画得很漂亮。一本好书有时和一栋好房子一样值钱。

paragraphs.text: In those times there were even some kings who could not read. They thought more of hunting and fighting than of learning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在那个时代，甚至有一些国王不识字。他们更多地考虑狩猎和战斗而不是学习。

paragraphs.text: There was one such king who had four sons, Ethelbald, Ethelbert, Ethelred, and Alfred. The three older boys were sturdy, half-grown lads; the youngest, Alfred, was a slender, fair-haired child.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有这样一位国王，他有四个儿子：埃塞尔博尔德、埃塞尔伯特、埃塞尔雷德和阿尔弗雷德。三个年纪较大的男孩都是半成年的壮实小伙子。最小的孩子阿尔弗雷德是个身材苗条、金发的孩子。

paragraphs.text: One day when they were with their mother, she showed them a wonderful book that some rich friend had given her. She turned the leaves and showed them the strange letters. She showed them the beautiful pictures, and told them how they had been drawn and painted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，当他们和母亲在一起时，她给他们看了一本好书，是一位富有的朋友送给她的。她翻动树叶，给他们看那些奇怪的字母。她向他们展示了美丽的图画，并告诉他们这些图画是如何绘制和绘制的。

paragraphs.text: They admired the book very much, for they had never seen anything like it. "But the best part of it is the story which it tells," said their mother. "If you could only read, you might learn that story and enjoy it. Now I have a mind to give this book to one of you"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们非常欣赏这本书，因为他们从未见过这样的书。 “但它最精彩的部分是它讲述的故事，”他们的母亲说。 “如果你能读，你可能会学到这个故事并享受它。现在我想把这本书送给你们中的一个人”

paragraphs.text: "If I were a priest or a monk" said Ethelbald, "I would learn to read. But I am a prince, and it is foolish for princes to waste their time with such things."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 埃塞尔博德说：“如果我是一名牧师或修道士，我就会学习读书。但我是王子，王子在这些事情上浪费时间是愚蠢的。”

paragraphs.text: A few weeks passed by. Then, one morning, Alfred went into his mother's room with a smiling, joyous face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几个星期过去了。然后，一天早上，阿尔弗雷德满脸笑容地走进他母亲的房间。

paragraphs.text: Alfred opened it with careful fingers. Then he began with the first word on the first page and read the first story aloud without making one mistake.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿尔弗雷德小心翼翼地用手指打开了它。然后他从第一页的第一个字开始，大声朗读第一个故事，没有出错。

paragraphs.text: "I asked the monk, Brother Felix, to teach me," said Alfred. "And every day since you showed me the book, he has given me a lesson. It was no easy thing to learn these letters and how they are put together to make words. Now, Brother Felix says I can read almost as well as he."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我请修道士菲利克斯修士来教我，”阿尔弗雷德说。 “自从你给我看这本书以来，他每天都给我上一课。学习这些字母以及它们如何组合成单词并不是一件容易的事。现在，菲利克斯弟兄说我的阅读能力几乎和他一样好。”

paragraphs.text: But his mother kissed him and gave him the beautiful book. "The prize is yours, Alfred," she said. "I am sure that whether you grow up to be a monk or a king, you will be a wise and noble man."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但他的母亲吻了他并给了他那本美丽的书。 “奖品是你的，阿尔弗雷德，”她说。 “我确信，无论你长大后成为僧侣还是国王，你都会成为一个明智而高贵的人。”

paragraphs.text: And Alfred did grow up to become the wisest and noblest king that England ever had. In history he is called Alfred the Great.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿尔弗雷德确实成长为英格兰有史以来最明智、最高贵的国王。历史上他被称为阿尔弗雷德大帝。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE KING AND THE PAGE

paragraphs.text: Many years ago there was a king of Prussia, whose name was Frederick; and because he was very wise and very brave, people called him Frederick the Great. Like other kings, he lived in a beautiful palace and had many officers and servants to wait upon him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多年前，普鲁士有一位国王，名叫腓特烈。由于他非常聪明、非常勇敢，人们称他为腓特烈大帝。和其他国王一样，他住在一座美丽的宫殿里，有许多官员和仆人伺候他。

paragraphs.text: Among the servants there was a little page whose name was Carl. It was Carl's duty to sit outside of the king's bedroom and be ready to serve him at any time.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 仆人中有一个小侍从，名叫卡尔。卡尔的职责是坐在国王的卧室外面，随时准备为他服务。

paragraphs.text: One night the king sat up very late, writing letters and sending messages; and the little page was kept busy running on errands until past midnight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，国王熬夜写信、发信息，睡得很晚。这个小侍从一直忙着办事，直到午夜过后。

paragraphs.text: The next morning the king wished to send him on another errand. He rang the little bell which was used to call the page, but no page answered.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天早上，国王想派他去执行另一项任务。他按响了用来呼叫传呼的小铃，但没有传呼应答。

paragraphs.text: "I wonder what can have happened to the boy," he said; and he opened the door and looked out. There, sitting in his chair, was Carl, fast asleep. The poor child was so tired after his night's work that he could not keep awake.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我想知道这个男孩发生了什么事，”他说；他打开门向外看去。卡尔坐在椅子上，熟睡着。这个可怜的孩子经过一夜的工作后太累了，以至于无法保持清醒。

paragraphs.text: The king was about to waken him roughly, when he saw a piece of paper on the floor beside him. He picked it up and read it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王正要粗暴地叫醒他，这时他看到身旁的地板上有一张纸。他拿起它读了起来。

paragraphs.text: _Dearest Carl; You are a good boy to send me all your wages, for now I can pay the rent and buy some warm clothing for your little sister. I thank you for it, and pray that God will bless you. Be faithful to the king and do your duty._
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最亲爱的卡尔；你真是个好孩子，把你的工资都寄给我了，现在我可以付房租了，也可以给你妹妹买一些保暖的衣服了。我为此感谢你，并祈祷上帝保佑你。忠于国王并履行你的职责。

paragraphs.text: The king went back to the room on tiptoe. He took ten gold pieces from his table and wrapped them in the little letter. Then he went out again, very quietly, and slipped them all into the boy's pocket.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王踮起脚尖回到房间。他从桌上取出十枚金币，将它们包裹在小信中。然后他又悄悄出去，把它们全塞进男孩的口袋里。

paragraphs.text: He put his hand in his pocket, and was surprised to find the gold pieces wrapped in his mother's letter. Then his eyes overflowed with tears, and he fell on his knees before the king.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他把手伸进口袋，惊讶地发现母亲的信中包裹着金币。然后他的眼里充满了泪水，跪在国王面前。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, your Majesty!" cried Carl. "Have mercy on me. It is true that I have been asleep, but I know nothing about this money. Some one is trying to ruin me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦，陛下！”卡尔喊道。 “可怜可怜我吧，我确实睡着了，但我对这笔钱一无所知，有人想毁了我。”

paragraphs.text: "Have courage, my boy," said the king. "I know how you must have been overwearied with long hours of watching. And people say that fortune comes to us in our sleep. You may send the gold pieces to your mother with my compliments; and tell her that the king will take care of both her and you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “要有勇气，我的孩子，”国王说。 “我知道你长时间观看一定已经疲惫不堪了。人们都说，好运会在睡梦中降临到我们身上。你可以把金币送给你母亲，表达我的问候；并告诉她，国王会照顾她和你。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Children's Hour, Volume 3: BETHLEHEM]

paragraphs.text: No other visitor is allowed to enter the house before the "Polaznik," the Christmas guest, has come. The part is usually taken by some boy from a neighbor's family, who comes very early and brings with him a woollen glove full of wheat. When at his knock the door is opened, he showers the wheat over those around the brightly burning fire and into all corners of the room with the greeting, "Christ is born." The mother of the family throws a handful of wheat at him and all the others shout, "In truth, He is born!" The guest then walks straight to the fire, and with the heavy shovel strikes the burning log with all his force repeatedly, so that thousands of sparks rise high in the chimney, while he says, "May you have this year so many oxen, so many horses, so many sheep, so many pigs, so many beehives of honey, so much good luck, so much success and happiness." After this good wish he kisses his host, drops to his knees before the Christmas log on the fire, kisses one end of it, which sticks out of the fireplace into the room, and places a coin upon it as his gift. As he rises, a woman offers him a low wooden chair, but just as he seats himself draws it away so that he sits down hard upon the ground, and is thus supposed to fix to it firmly every good wish he has spoken. Finally he is wrapped in a thick blanket, and with it around him sits quietly for a few minutes while the young folks who are to tend the flocks and herds in the coming year come to the hearth and kiss each other solemnly across the Christmas log. The wearing of the blanket is said to insure thick cream in the next year, and the shepherds' kisses will make for peace and plenty among the cattle.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在圣诞节客人“Polaznik”到来之前，其他访客不得进入房屋。这个角色通常由邻居家的男孩来扮演，他来得很早，还带着一只装满小麦的羊毛手套。当他敲门时，门被打开，他将小麦洒在明亮燃烧的火周围的那些人身上，并洒到房间的各个角落，并问候道：“基督诞生了。”一家人的母亲向他扔了一把小麦，其他人都喊道：“他真的出生了！”客人径直走到火边，用重铲使劲反复敲打着燃烧的木头，烟囱里顿时冒出万千火花，边说边说：“今年多牛，多马，多羊，多猪，多蜂巢，万事如意，万事如意，幸福快乐。”在这个良好的祝愿之后，他亲吻了主人，跪在火上的圣诞木头前，亲吻了木头从壁炉伸出到房间里的一端，并在上面放了一枚硬币作为礼物。当他站起来时，一个女人给了他一把矮木椅，但当他坐下时，她把椅子拉开，让他坐在地上，因此应该牢牢地固定住他所说的每一个美好​​愿望。最后，他裹着一条厚毯子，围着毯子静静地坐了几分钟，而来年要照顾牛羊的年轻人则来到壁炉边，隔着圣诞木头庄严地亲吻对方。据说，穿上毯子可以确保来年有浓稠的奶油，而牧羊人的亲吻将使牛群获得和平与丰裕。

paragraphs.text: Before the chief meal of the day, all the members of the family gather about its head, each with a lighted candle in hand, while he prays briefly. Then they turn and kiss each other with such greetings as: "Peace of God be with us!" "Christos is born!" "In truth, He is born!" "Therefore let us bow before Christos and His birth." And toward the end of the meal all stand to drink "to the glory of God and of the birth of Christ," which marks the end of the Christmas celebration.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在当天的主餐之前，所有家庭成员都会聚集在他的头周围，每人手里都拿着一支点燃的蜡烛，而他则进行简短的祈祷。然后他们转身亲吻对方，并问候道：“愿上帝保佑我们！” “克里斯托出生了！” “事实上，他已经诞生了！” “因此，让我们向克里斯托和他的诞生鞠躬。”用餐结束时，所有人起立举杯，“为了上帝的荣耀和基督的诞生”，这标志着圣诞节庆祝活动的结束。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 4. LOUISA MAY ALCOTT, AUTHOR

paragraphs.text: Louisa May Alcott was born in Germantown, Pa., November 29, 1832. Her father was a cultured school-teacher and her mother of an old aristocratic family. Louisa was the eldest of four daughters, whose happy life she pictures in "Little Women," herself being "Jo." She was a wild, happy-hearted, enthusiastic girl, preferring whistling and romping and boys' games rather than girls'. Their home was frequently visited by such literary people as Emerson, Thoreau, and Hawthorne, who were her father's friends. At eight years of age she wrote this poem of eight lines:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 路易莎·梅·奥尔科特 (Louisa May Alcott) 1832 年 11 月 29 日出生于宾夕法尼亚州日耳曼敦。她的父亲是一位有教养的教师，母亲则出身于一个古老的贵族家庭。路易莎是四个女儿中的长女，她在《小妇人》中描绘了她们幸福的生活，而她自己就是“乔”。她是一个狂野、快乐、热情的女孩，比起女孩，她更喜欢吹口哨、嬉闹和男孩的游戏。她父亲的朋友爱默生、梭罗和霍桑等文学家经常来他们家拜访。她八岁时写下了这首八行诗：

paragraphs.text: Welcome, welcome, little stranger, Fear no harm and fear no danger; We are glad to see you here, For you sing, "Sweet spring is near." Now the white snow melts away; Now the flowers blossom gay. Come, dear bird, and build your nest For we love our robin best.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 欢迎，欢迎，小陌生人，无惧伤害，无惧危险；我们很高兴在这里见到你，因为你唱着“甜蜜的春天即将来临”。现在白雪融化了；现在，花朵盛开。来吧，亲爱的鸟儿，筑巢吧，因为我们最爱我们的知更鸟。

paragraphs.text: Her mother preserved this poem, and told her if she kept on she might one day be a second Shakespeare. She was fond of telling fairy stories to amuse her sisters and friends, and often turned the old tales into little plays which the children acted in a barn. One of these plays was "Jack and the Bean Stalk." A squash vine, placed in the barn, was the bean-stalk, and when it was cut down the boy who played Giant, would come tumbling down from the hay-loft. At thirteen she wrote the beautiful poem, "My Kingdom." After she became a school-teacher she was always helping somebody, taking care of an invalid or the poor, or sewing to help her mother. She continued to write stories. Some of the stories were rejected and the publisher advised her to stick to her school-teaching. Returning from the Civil War, where she had been a valued nurse to the wounded soldiers, she presented, through her father, several short stories to a publisher, who rejected them, with the advice that she write a story for girls. She thought she could not do that, and wrote "Little Women" to prove that she could not, but it is perhaps the best-loved girls' story ever written. Then she wrote "Little Men," of which fifty thousand copies were ordered before it was printed. She received one hundred thousand dollars for her books. Her life-desire was now realized in having money enough to make her family comfortable. Her father died in 1888, and she followed him only three days after. Miss Louisa May Alcott, besides being a writer, was also an earnest advocate of woman suffrage and temperance.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她的母亲保留了这首诗，并告诉她，如果她坚持下去，有一天她可能会成为第二个莎士比亚。她喜欢讲童话故事来逗她的姐妹和朋友们开心，经常把古老的故事编成小剧，让孩子们在谷仓里表演。其中一部戏剧是《杰克与豆茎》。谷仓里的一棵南瓜藤就是豆秆，当它被砍倒时，扮演巨人的男孩就会从干草棚上滚下来。十三岁时，她写下了美丽的诗《我的王国》。当她成为一名教师后，她总是帮助别人，照顾病人或穷人，或者缝纫来帮助她的母亲。她继续写故事。有些故事被拒绝了，出版商建议她坚持学校的教学。内战结束后，她曾是受伤士兵的重要护士，她通过父亲向出版商提交了几篇短篇小说，但出版商拒绝了这些故事，并建议她为女孩们写一篇故事。她认为自己做不到，并写了《小妇人》来证明她做不到，但这也许是有史以来最受欢迎的女孩故事。然后她写了《小人物》，在印刷之前就已经订购了五万本。她的书收到了十万美元。现在，她的生活愿望已经实现，有了足够的钱让家人过上舒适的生活。她的父亲于 1888 年去世，三天后她就跟随他去世了。路易莎·梅·奥尔科特小姐除了是一名作家之外，还是女性选举权和禁酒的热心倡导者。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: The Blinded Giant

paragraphs.text: At Dalton, near Thirsk, in Yorkshire, there is a mill. It has quite recently been rebuilt; but when I was at Dalton, six years ago, the old building stood. In front of the house was a long mound which went by the name of "the giant's grave," and in the mill you can see a long blade of iron something like a scythe-blade, but not curved, which was called "the giant's knife," because of a very curious story which is told of this knife. Would you like to hear it? Well, it isn't very long.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在约克郡瑟斯克附近的道尔顿，有一家磨坊。它最近才被重建；但六年前，当我在道尔顿时，那座老建筑依然矗立着。房子前面有一个长长的土堆，名字叫“巨人坟墓”，在磨坊里你可以看到一把长长的铁刀，类似镰刀刀刃，但不是弯曲的，被称为“巨人刀”，因为这把刀有一个非常奇怪的故事。你想听听吗？嗯，时间不是很长。

paragraphs.text: There once lived a giant at this mill who had only one eye in the middle of his forehead, and he ground men's bones to make his bread. One day he captured on Pilmoor a lad named Jack, and instead of grinding him in the mill he kept him grinding as his servant, and never let him get away. Jack served the giant seven years, and never was allowed a holiday the whole time. At last he could bear it no longer. Topcliffe fair was coming on, and Jack begged that he might be allowed to go there.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个磨坊里曾经住着一个巨人，他额头中间只有一只眼睛，他把人的骨头磨碎来制作面包。有一天，他在皮尔莫尔捕获了一个名叫杰克的小伙子，并没有把他放在磨坊里磨碎，而是让他作为仆人磨碎，并且从不让他逃脱。杰克为巨人服务了七年，自始至终都没有被允许休假。终于，他再也无法忍受了。托普克利夫集市即将举行，杰克请求允许他去那里。

paragraphs.text: "I've been grinding and grinding these seven years," said Jack, "and not a holiday have I had. I'll have one now, whatever you say."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “这七年来我一直在磨磨蹭蹭，”杰克说，“我没有休过假。不管你说什么，我现在就休个假。”

paragraphs.text: Well, the day was hot, and after dinner the giant lay down in the mill with his head on a sack and dozed. He had been eating in the mill, and had laid down a great loaf of bone bread by his side, and the knife I told you about was in his hand, but his fingers relaxed their hold of it in sleep. Jack seized the knife, and holding it with both his hands drove the blade into the single eye of the giant, who woke with a howl of agony, and starting up, barred the door. Jack was again in difficulties, for he couldn't get out, but he soon found a way out of them. The giant had a favourite dog, which had also been sleeping when his master was blinded. So Jack killed the dog, skinned it, and threw the hide over his back.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 天很热，晚饭后，巨人躺在磨坊里，头枕在麻袋上打瞌睡。他一直在磨坊里吃饭，并在身旁放了一大条骨面包，我告诉你的那把刀就在他手里，但他的手指在睡梦中放松了对它的握持。杰克抓住了那把刀，用双手握住它，把刀刃刺进了巨人的单眼，巨人在一声痛苦的嚎叫中醒来，猛地站起来，把门锁上了。杰克再次陷入困境，因为他无法摆脱困境，但他很快就找到了摆脱困境的方法。巨人有一只最喜欢的狗，当他的主人失明时，它也在睡觉。于是杰克杀了狗，剥了皮，然后把皮扔到了背上。

paragraphs.text: "At him, Truncheon," said the giant; "at the little wretch that I've fed these seven years, and now has blinded me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “对他来说，警棍，”巨人说道。 “我养了这个小可怜七年，现在却让我失明了。”

paragraphs.text: "Bow, wow," says Jack, and ran between the giant's legs on all-fours, barking till he got to the door. He unlatched it and was off, and never more was seen at Dalton Mill.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “鞠躬，哇，”杰克说，然后四肢着地跑到巨人的两腿之间，一直叫到门口。他打开门闩就走了，道尔顿米尔再也没有看到更多的人了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 16. THE CRIPPLE AT THE BEAUTIFUL GATE

paragraphs.text: One afternoon two friends were walking along a street in Jerusalem on their way to the evening sacrifice in the temple. At one of the entrances--the Beautiful Gate (so named because of its snow-white marble steps leading up to its great door of costly brass)--sat a poor lame man, begging. His feet and ankles were so crippled that he had never been able to walk or even to stand. His friends carried this helpless cripple and laid him every morning at this temple entrance to beg charity from those who went to pray. As soon as the man saw these two friends, Peter and John, he cried piteously, "Give charity!" Standing still and looking him quietly in the eye, the two friends said, "Look on us!" He looked up at once most expectantly. Peter said, "Silver and gold have I none, but what I have, I give thee. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk!" Taking him by the hand Peter lifted him up, and immediately his feet and ankle-bones became strong. The man leaped up and went into the temple with Peter and John, walking and leaping and praising God. All the people were amazed, seeing him leaping and hearing his shouts of joy as he held fast to his two friends. A great throng gathered about them in the large open court, called Solomon's porch. Peter, seeing the throng, began to tell them about the wonderful Prince of Life, Jesus, whom they had put to death. Such preaching within the temple courts aroused the people and offended the priests, and the chief officer seized Peter and John and cast them into prison. This caused hundreds of the people to declare themselves Christians. The next morning when Peter and John were brought before the council and questioned, the officers said, "We will let you go if you will promise not to speak or teach in this name again." They answered: "Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you, rather than unto God, judge ye; for we cannot but speak the things we have seen and heard." So, failing to frighten them, the officers were compelled to let them go. And being let go, they returned at once to the Christian company, and told what the Jewish officers had said to them. And the cripple who had been healed at the Beautiful Gate was a happy Christian in that company, and was one of the loudest in giving true praise to God that day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天下午，两个朋友沿着耶路撒冷的一条街道行走，前往圣殿参加晚祭。在其中一个入口——美丽之门（因其雪白的大理石台阶通向昂贵的黄铜大门而得名）——坐着一个可怜的跛子，正在乞讨。他的脚和脚踝严重残疾，无法行走，甚至无法站立。他的朋友们每天早上都会抬着这个无助的瘸子，把他放在这座寺庙的入口处，向那些去祈祷的人乞求施舍。那人一看到彼得和约翰这两个朋友，就可怜兮兮地喊道：“施舍吧！”两个朋友静静地站着，静静地看着他的眼睛，说道：“看着我们！”他立刻满怀期待地抬起头来。彼得说：“金银我都没有，但我把我所有的都给你。奉拿撒勒人耶稣基督的名，你行走吧！”彼得拉着他的手把他举了起来，他的脚和踝骨立刻变得坚强起来。那人就跳起来，和彼得、约翰一起走进圣殿，边走边跳，赞美神。所有人都惊讶不已，看到他跳跃着，听到他紧紧抓住两个朋友的欢呼声。在称为所罗门门廊的大露天庭院里，一大群人聚集在他们周围。彼得看到这群人，就开始向他们讲述奇妙的生命之君耶稣，他被他们处死了。圣殿院内的这种讲道激怒了民众，并冒犯了祭司，大官抓住了彼得和约翰，将他们投入监狱。这导致数百人宣布自己是基督徒。第二天早上，当彼得和约翰被带到公会面前接受审问时，军官们说：“如果你们保证不再以这个名字说话或教导，我们就释放你们。”他们回答说：“在上帝看来，听从你们而不是听从上帝是否正确，你们自己判断吧；因为我们不能不说出我们所看到和听到的事情。”于是，警察们没能吓住他们，只得将他们放了。被释放后，他们立即回到基督徒队伍中，并告诉了犹太军官对他们所说的话。在美丽之门被治愈的瘸子是那群人中一位快乐的基督徒，并且是那天最大声地真正赞美上帝的人之一。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: FRANKLIN WRITES FOR THE NEWSPAPER

paragraphs.text: Here is Benjamin Franklin's own account of his first attempt at writing. You see, he did not have a chance to learn to write, as you do, by practice in composition in school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 以下是本杰明·富兰克林对他第一次写作尝试的描述。你看，他没有机会像你一样，在学校里通过作文练习来学习写作。

paragraphs.text: My brother had, in 1720 or 1721, begun to print a newspaper. It was the second that appeared in America, and was called the "New England Courant". The only one before it was the "Boston News-Letter". I remember his being _dissuaded_ by some of his friends from the undertaking, as not likely to succeed, one newspaper being, in their judgment, enough for America. At this time (1771) there are not less than five and twenty. He went on, however, with the undertaking; and after having worked in composing the types and printing off the sheets, I was employed to carry the papers through the streets to the customers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我的兄弟在 1720 年或 1721 年开始印刷报纸。这是美国第二份报纸，被称为“新英格兰新闻报”。之前唯一的一份是《波士顿新闻通讯》。我记得他的一些朋友劝阻他放弃这项事业，因为不太可能成功，在他们看来，一份报纸对美国来说就足够了。此时（1771年）不少于五、二十人。然而，他还是继续了他的事业。在完成排版和打印纸张后，我受雇将纸张穿过街道运送给顾客。

paragraphs.text: He had some ingenious men among his friends, who amused themselves by writing little pieces for this paper, which gained it credit and made it more in demand; and these gentlemen often visited us. Hearing their conversations, and their accounts of the _approbation_ their papers were received with, I was excited to try my hand among them; but being still a boy, and suspecting that my brother would object to printing anything of mine in his paper if he knew it to be mine, I contrived to _disguise_ my hand; and, writing an _anonymous_ paper, I put it in at night under the door of the printing-house. It was found in the morning and communicated to his writing friends when they called in as usual. They read it, and commented on it in my hearing; and I had the _exquisite_ pleasure of finding it met with their approbation, and that in their different guesses at the author, none were named but men of some _character_ among us for learning and _ingenuity_. I suppose now that I was rather lucky in my judges and that perhaps they were not really so very good ones as I then _esteemed_ them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的朋友中有一些聪明人，他们通过为这份报纸写一些小文章来取乐，这为这份报纸赢得了声誉，并使其更受欢迎。这些先生们经常来拜访我们。听到他们的谈话，以及他们对论文获得认可的描述，我很高兴能在他们中间尝试一下。但当时我还是个孩子，我怀疑如果我哥哥知道我的任何东西是我的，他会反对在他的报纸上印上我的任何东西，所以我设法掩盖了我的手。我写了一篇匿名论文，晚上把它放在印刷厂的门下。这是早上发现的，当他的写作朋友像往常一样打电话来时，他们就将其传达给了他们。他们读了它，并在我的听证会上发表了评论；我非常高兴地发现它得到了他们的认可，而且在他们对作者的不同猜测中，除了我们当中一些具有学识和创造力的人之外，没有一个人被点名。我现在想，我对我的法官们相当幸运，也许他们并不像我当时所尊敬的那样非常好。

paragraphs.text: Encouraged, however, by this, I wrote and conveyed in the same way to the press several more papers which were equally approved; and I kept my secret till my small fund of sense for such performances was pretty well exhausted, and then I _discovered_ it, when I began to be considered a little more by my brother's acquaintance, and in a manner that did not quite please him, as he thought, probably with reason, that it tended to make me too vain.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然而，受此鼓舞，我又以同样的方式撰写并转交了几篇同样获得认可的论文；我一直保守着我的秘密，直到我对这种表演的一点理智都耗尽了，然后当我哥哥的熟人开始更多地考虑我时，我发现了这一点，并且以一种不太让他高兴的方式，正如他认为的那样，可能是有道理的，这往往会让我变得太虚荣。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE HORSESHOE NAILS

paragraphs.text: "Shoe him quickly, for the king wishes to ride him to battle," said the groom who had brought him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “快给他穿鞋，国王要骑着他去打仗。”带他来的马夫说道。

paragraphs.text: "Most certainly, and very soon, too," answered the man. "The king's enemies are even now advancing, and all are ready for the fight. To- day will decide whether Richard or Henry shall be king of England."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当然，而且很快，”那人回答道。 “国王的敌人现在还在逼近，所有人都做好了战斗的准备。今天将决定理查还是亨利将成为英格兰国王。”

paragraphs.text: The smith went on with his work. From a bar of iron he made four horseshoes. These he hammered and shaped and fitted to the horse's feet. Then he began to nail them on.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 铁匠继续他的工作。他用一根铁棒制作了四个马蹄铁。他将它们锤击成型并安装到马脚上。然后他开始把它们钉上。

paragraphs.text: But after he had nailed on two shoes, he found that he had not nails enough for the other two. "I have only six nails," he said, "and it will take a little time to hammer out ten more."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但当他钉完两只鞋后，他发现自己的钉子不够钉另外两只了。 “我只有六颗钉子，”他说，“还需要一点时间才能再钉出十颗。”

paragraphs.text: "Oh, well," said the groom, "won't six nails do? Put three in each shoe. I hear the trumpets now. King Richard will be impatient."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “噢，好吧，”新郎说，“六颗钉子不行吗？在每只鞋子里放三颗。我现在听到喇叭声了。理查德国王会不耐烦的。”

paragraphs.text: "Three nails in each shoe will hold them on," said the smith. "Yes, I think we may risk it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “每只鞋上有三颗钉子就能把它们固定住，”铁匠说。 “是的，我想我们可以冒险。”

paragraphs.text: The battle had been raging for some time. King Richard rode hither and thither, cheering his men and fighting his foes. His enemy, Henry, who wished to be king, was pressing him hard.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 战斗已经激烈了一段时间。理查国王策马驰骋，为他的部下欢呼，并与敌人作战。他的敌人亨利一心想当国王，对他施加了很大的压力。

paragraphs.text: Far away, at the other side of the field, King Richard saw his men falling back. Without his help they would soon be beaten. So he spurred his horse to ride to their aid.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 远处，在战场的另一边，理查国王看到他的部下正在向后退去。如果没有他的帮助，他们很快就会被打败。于是他策马前去救援。

paragraphs.text: He was hardly halfway across the stony field when one of the horse's shoes flew off. The horse was lamed on a rock. Then another shoe came off. The horse stumbled, and his rider was thrown heavily to the ground.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他刚穿过石地的一半时，一只马蹄飞落了。那匹马被跛在一块岩石上。然后另一只鞋掉了。马匹绊倒了，骑手重重地摔在地上。

paragraphs.text: Before the king could rise, his frightened horse, although lame, had galloped away. The king looked, and saw that his soldiers were beaten, and that the battle was everywhere going against him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王还没站起来，他那匹受惊的马虽然跛了，但已经飞奔而去了。国王一看，发现他的士兵都被打败了，而且到处都是对他不利的战斗。

paragraphs.text: He waved his sword in the air. He shouted, "A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse." But there was no horse for him. His soldiers were intent on saving themselves. They could not give him any help.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他在空中挥舞着剑。他喊道：“一匹马！一匹马！我的王国是一匹马。”但他没有马。他的士兵一心想自救。他们无法给他任何帮助。

paragraphs.text: "For the want of a nail the shoe was lost; For the want of a shoe the horse was lost; For the want of a horse the battle was lost; For the failure of battle the kingdom was lost;-- And all for the want of a horseshoe nail."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “因为缺少一颗钉子，所以失去了鞋子；因为缺少了鞋子，所以失去了马；因为缺少一匹马，所以战斗失败了；因为战斗的失败，失去了王国；——而这一切都是因为缺少一颗马蹄钉。”

paragraphs.text: Richard the Third was one of England's worst kings. Henry, the Duke of Richmond, made war upon him and defeated him in a great battle.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 理查三世是英格兰最糟糕的国王之一。里士满公爵亨利向他宣战，并在一场大战中击败了他。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: THE HOME OF ROBIN IN SHERWOOD FOREST

paragraphs.text: Many hundreds of years ago, when the Plantagenets were kings, England was so covered with woods that a squirrel was said to be able to hop from tree to tree from the Severn to the Humber.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 数百年前，当金雀花王朝还是国王时，英格兰被树林覆盖，据说松鼠能够从塞文河到亨伯河从一棵树跳到另一棵树。

paragraphs.text: It must have been very different-looking from the country we travel through now; but still there were roads that ran from north to south and from east to west, for the use of those who wished to leave their homes, and at certain times of the year these roads were thronged with people. Pilgrims going to some holy shrine passed along, merchants taking their wares to Court, Abbots and Bishops ambling by on palfreys to bear their part in the King's Council, and, more frequently still, a solitary Knight, seeking adventures.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它一定与我们现在所经过的国家有很大不同。但仍然有从北到南、从东到西的道路，供那些想要离开家园的人使用，并且在一年中的某些时候，这些道路上挤满了人。前往圣地的朝圣者经过，商人将货物带到宫廷，修道院院长和主教骑着小马漫步经过，在国王的议会中发挥作用，更常见的是，孤独的骑士寻求冒险。

paragraphs.text: Besides the broad roads there were small tracks and little green paths, and these led to clumps of low huts, where dwelt the peasants, charcoal-burners, and plowmen, while here and there some larger clearing than usual told that the house of a yeoman was near.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 除了宽阔的道路之外，还有小路和小绿道，通向一丛丛低矮的小屋，那里住着农民、烧炭的人和犁地的人，而到处都有比平时更大的空地，表明附近有一个自耕农的房子。

paragraphs.text: Now and then as you passed through the forest you might ride by a splendid abbey, and catch a glimpse of monks in long black or white gowns, fishing in the streams and rivers that abound in this part of England, or casting nets in the fish ponds which were in the midst of the abbey gardens. Or you might chance to see a castle with round turrets and high battlements, circled by strong walls, and protected by a moat full of water.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当你穿过森林时，你可能会时不时地骑马经过一座辉煌的修道院，瞥见穿着黑色或白色长袍的僧侣，他们在英格兰这一地区盛产的溪流和河流中钓鱼，或者在修道院花园中央的鱼塘里撒网。或者您可能有机会看到一座拥有圆形塔楼和高城垛的城堡，周围有坚固的城墙，并有一条充满水的护城河保护。

paragraphs.text: This was the sort of England into which the famous Robin Hood was born. We know very little about him, who he was, or where he lived, except that for some reason he had offended the King, who had declared him an outlaw, so that any man might kill him and never pay a penalty for it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这就是著名的罗宾汉出生的英格兰。我们对他、他是谁、他住在哪里知之甚少，只知道出于某种原因，他冒犯了国王，国王宣布他为亡命之徒，以便任何人都可以杀死他，并且永远不会为此付出代价。

paragraphs.text: But, outlaw or not, the poor people loved him and looked on him as their friend, and many a stout fellow came to join him, and led a merry life in the greenwood, with moss and fern for bed, and for meat the King's deer, which it was death to slay.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但是，不管是不是亡命之徒，穷人都爱他，视他为朋友，许多粗壮的家伙也来加入他的行列，在绿林里过着快乐的生活，以苔藓和蕨类植物为床，以国王的鹿为食，杀死它就是死亡。

paragraphs.text: Peasants of all sorts, tillers of the land, yeomen, and, as some say, Knights, went on their ways freely, for of them Robin took no toll; but rich men with moneybags well filled trembled as they drew near to Sherwood Forest--who was to know whether behind every tree there did not lurk Robin Hood or some of his men?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 各种各样的农民、耕种者、自耕农，还有一些人所说的骑士，都自由地前行，因为罗宾没有对他们造成任何伤害。但是，当他们接近舍伍德森林时，钱袋子装得满满的富人都在颤抖——谁知道每棵树后面是否潜伏着罗宾汉或他的一些手下呢？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 9. AUDUBON, NATURALIST

paragraphs.text: Every boy who loves out-of-door life should know the story of John James Audubon. He was born on a farm in Louisiana, May 4, 1780. His parents were French and when very young he was taken to France where he attended school. His favorite study was of animals and birds. He often roamed the woods, bringing home birds' nests and eggs, curious rocks, and bits of moss. His father bought him a picture-book of birds. The delighted boy painted these copies, but saw they were not like real birds. Later he took lessons of the great French painter, David, who taught him to draw and paint things as they are. Returning to America, his father gave him a large farm in Pennsylvania where his studies of birds led him to decide to write a book on bird life, and illustrate it by his own drawings. This was a great task, but when this young man decided to do anything he never allowed difficulties to stand in his way. So he began his work and studied and painted year after year. He had to live much of the time in the woods, studying how the birds lived and built their nests. Sometimes he went by boat down the river; sometimes he went on horseback. Often he tramped alone through the trackless woods. Many nights he slept out-of-doors. He lost all his money and was obliged to stop his work and paint portraits and sell his choice drawings for a living. His heroic wife took up school-teaching to help him out with his work. One day while traveling he left his paintings of nearly a thousand birds in a wooden box in the home of a friend. Two months later, when he returned and opened the box, he found two large rats had got into the box and cut up all the paintings with their sharp teeth, making a nest for their young among the gnawed pieces. He said, "I will make better paintings!" It took him four long years to complete his pictures, but at last the great book was completed and published and praised throughout France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个热爱户外生活的男孩都应该知道约翰·詹姆斯·奥杜邦的故事。 1780 年 5 月 4 日，他出生在路易斯安那州的一个农场。他的父母是法国人，他很小的时候就被带到法国并在那里上学。他最喜欢的研究是动物和鸟类。他经常在树林里漫步，带回家鸟巢和鸟蛋、奇怪的岩石和一些苔藓。他父亲给他买了一本鸟类图画书。男孩很高兴地画了这些复制品，但发现它们并不像真正的鸟。后来他师从法国伟大画家大卫，大卫教他画画并描绘事物的本来面目。回到美国后，他的父亲在宾夕法尼亚州给了他一个大农场，在那里他对鸟类的研究使他决定写一本关于鸟类生活的书，并用自己的图画来说明。这是一项艰巨的任务，但是当这个年轻人决定做任何事情时，他从不让困难阻碍他。于是他开始了他的工作，年复一年地学习、绘画。他大部分时间都住在树林里，研究鸟儿如何生活和筑巢。有时他乘船顺河而下；有时他骑马。他常常独自一人在无路可走的树林里行走。许多个夜晚他都睡在户外。他失去了所有的钱，被迫停止工作，画肖像并出售他选择的画作谋生。他英勇的妻子开始在学校教书，以帮助他完成工作。有一天，他在旅行时将近千只鸟的画落在一位朋友家中的一个木箱里。两个月后，当他回来打开盒子时，发现两只大老鼠钻进了盒子，用锋利的牙齿把所有的画都咬碎了，并在啃碎的碎片中为幼崽筑巢。他说：“我会画出更好的画！”他花了四年的时间才完成了他的画作，但这本伟大的书最终完成并出版，并在法国、英国和美国受到赞扬。

paragraphs.text: The Society for the Protection of Our Feathered Friends was organized by this great naturalist, who spent the rest of his life for this great object. At present there are few places where boys and girls have not heard of Audubon. He died at his beautiful home on the Hudson River, greatly honored and beloved in France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 保护我们的羽毛朋友协会是由这位伟大的博物学家组织的，他为这个伟大的目标度过了余生。目前，几乎每个地方的男孩和女孩都没有听说过奥杜邦。他在哈德逊河畔美丽的家中去世，在法国、英国和美国受到极大的尊敬和爱戴。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: English Fairy Tales: HOW JACK WENT TO SEEK HIS FORTUNE

paragraphs.text: Once on a time there was a boy named Jack, and one morning he started to go and seek his fortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一个名叫杰克的男孩，一天早上他开始去寻找他的财富。

paragraphs.text: Well, they went on till it was about dark, and they began to think of some place where they could spend the night. About this time they came in sight of a house, and Jack told them to keep still while he went up and looked in through the window. And there were some robbers counting over their money. Then Jack went back and told them to wait till he gave the word, and then to make all the noise they could. So when they were all ready Jack gave the word, and the cat mewed, and the dog barked, and the goat bleated, and the bull bellowed, and the rooster crowed, and all together they made such a dreadful noise that it frightened the robbers all away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 好吧，他们继续前行，直到天快黑了，他们才开始思考可以过夜的地方。大约在这个时候，他们看到了一栋房子，杰克让他们保持安静，而他则走上楼去透过窗户往里看。还有一些强盗在数钱。然后杰克回去并告诉他们要等他一声令下，然后尽可能地发出声音。当他们准备好后，杰克一声令下，猫喵喵叫，狗吠，山羊咩咩叫，公牛吼叫，公鸡打鸣，它们一起发出如此可怕的声音，把强盗都吓跑了。

paragraphs.text: And then they went in and took possession of the house. Jack was afraid the robbers would come back in the night, and so when it came time to go to bed he put the cat in the rocking-chair, and he put the dog under the table, and he put the goat upstairs, and he put the bull down cellar, and the rooster flew up on to the roof, and Jack went to bed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后他们进去并占领了房子。杰克害怕强盗晚上会回来，所以到了睡觉的时候，他把猫放在摇椅上，把狗放在桌子底下，把山羊放在楼上，把公牛放在地窖里，然后公鸡飞上屋顶，杰克就上床睡觉了。

paragraphs.text: By-and-by the robbers saw it was all dark and they sent one man back to the house to look after their money. Before long he came back in a great fright and told them his story.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 过了一会儿，强盗们发现天已经黑了，他们派了一名男子回屋里看管他们的钱。不久，他惊恐地回来，向他们讲述了他的故事。

paragraphs.text: "I went back to the house," said he, "and went in and tried to sit down in the rocking-chair, and there was an old woman knitting, and she stuck her knitting-needles into me." That was the cat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我回到屋子，”他说，“走进去，想在摇椅上坐下，发现有一个老妇人在织毛衣，她把织针扎进了我的身体里。”那是那只猫，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I went to the table to look after the money and there was a shoemaker under the table, and he stuck his awl into me." That was the dog, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我走到桌子前看钱，桌子底下有一个鞋匠，他把锥子插到了我身上。”那是狗，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go upstairs, and there was a man up there threshing, and he knocked me down with his flail." That was the goat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我开始上楼，有一个人在上面打谷，他用连枷把我打倒了。”那是山羊，你知道。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go down cellar, and there was a man down there chopping wood, and he knocked me up with his axe." That was the bull, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我开始走进地窖，发现一个人在下面砍柴，他用斧子把我撞倒了。”你知道，那是公牛。

paragraphs.text: "But I shouldn't have minded all that if it hadn't been for that little fellow on top of the house, who kept a-hollering, 'Chuck him up to me-e! Chuck him up to me-e!'" Of course that was the cock-a-doodle-do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “但如果不是那个在房子顶上的小家伙不停地叫喊着，‘把他扔给我-e！把他扔给我-e！’”当然，那是鸡毛蒜皮的事。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: THE PATIENT CAT

paragraphs.text: When the spotted cat first found the nest, there was nothing in it, for it was only just finished. So she said, "I will wait!" for she was a patient cat, and the summer was before her. She waited a week, and then she climbed up again to the top of the tree, and peeped into the nest. There lay two lovely blue eggs, smooth and shining.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当花斑猫第一次找到巢时，里面什么也没有，因为它才刚刚建成。于是她说：“我会等！”因为她是一只有耐心的猫，夏天就在她面前。她等了一个星期，然后又爬到树顶，向鸟巢里窥视。里面下了两个可爱的蓝色蛋，光滑闪亮。

paragraphs.text: The spotted cat said, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait." So she waited; and while she was waiting, she caught mice and rats, and washed herself and slept, and did all that a spotted cat should do to pass the time away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 花猫说：“鸡蛋也许不错，但幼鸟更好。我会等。”于是她等待着；在等待的时候，她捉老鼠，洗澡，睡觉，做着一只花猫该做的一切来打发时间。

paragraphs.text: When another week had passed, she climbed the tree again and peeped into the nest. This time there were five eggs. But the spotted cat said again, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait a little longer!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 又过了一个星期，她又爬上树，向鸟巢里窥视。这次有五个鸡蛋。但花猫又说道：“鸡蛋或许不错，但幼鸟更好。我再等一会儿吧！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited a little longer and then went up again to look. Ah! there were five tiny birds, with big eyes and long necks, and yellow beaks wide open. Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch, and licked her nose and purred, for she was very happy. "It is worth while to be patient!" she said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她又等了一会儿，然后又上去查看。啊!有五只小鸟，眼睛很大，脖子很长，黄色的嘴巴张得很大。然后花猫就坐在树枝上，舔着鼻子，发出咕噜咕噜的声音，因为她很高兴。 “忍耐是值得的！”她说。

paragraphs.text: But when she looked again at the young birds, to see which one she should take first, she saw that they were very thin,--oh, very, very thin they were! The spotted cat had never seen anything so thin in her life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但是，当她再次看着幼鸟，看看应该先拿哪一只时，她发现它们非常瘦——哦，非常非常瘦！斑猫这辈子从来没有见过这么瘦的东西。

paragraphs.text: "Now," she said to herself, "if I were to wait only a few days longer, they would grow fat. Thin birds may be good, but fat birds are much better. I will wait!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在，”她对自己说，“如果我再等几天，它们就会长胖了。瘦的鸟可能很好，但胖的鸟更好。我会等！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited; and she watched the father-bird bringing worms all day long to the nest, and said, "Aha! they must be fattening fast! they will soon be as fat as I wish them to be. Aha! what a good thing it is to be patient."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她等待着；她看着鸟爸爸整天把虫子带到巢里，说道：“啊哈！它们一定长肥得很快！它们很快就会像我希望的那样胖起来。啊哈！有耐心真是太好了。”

paragraphs.text: At last, one day she thought, "Surely, now they must be fat enough! I will not wait another day. Aha! how good they will be!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，有一天她想：“当然，现在它们一定已经够胖了！我不会再等了。啊哈！它们会多棒啊！”

paragraphs.text: So she climbed up the tree, licking her chops all the way and thinking of the fat young birds. And when she reached the top and looked into the nest, it was empty!!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她爬上树，一路舔着排骨，想着那些胖乎乎的小鸟。当她到达山顶并向鸟巢内看去时，里面空无一人！

paragraphs.text: Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch and spoke thus, "Well, of all the horrid, mean, ungrateful creatures I ever saw, those birds are the horridest, and the meanest, and the most ungrateful! Mi-a-u-ow!!!!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后花斑猫在树枝上坐下，说道：“嗯，在我见过的所有可怕、卑鄙、忘恩负义的生物中，那些鸟是最可怕、最卑鄙、最忘恩负义的！米啊啊啊啊啊啊啊！！！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Prince: To the Magnificent Lorenzo Di Piero De' Medici

paragraphs.text: Those who strive to obtain the good graces of a prince are accustomed to come before him with such things as they hold most precious, or in which they see him take most delight; whence one often sees horses, arms, cloth of gold, precious stones, and similar ornaments presented to princes, worthy of their greatness.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那些努力获得王子恩宠的人习惯于带着自己最珍贵的东西或他们看到王子最高兴的东西来到他面前。人们经常看到马匹、武器、金布、宝石和类似的装饰品被赠送给王子，以配得上他们的伟大。

paragraphs.text: Desiring therefore to present myself to your Magnificence with some testimony of my devotion towards you, I have not found among my possessions anything which I hold more dear than, or value so much as, the knowledge of the actions of great men, acquired by long experience in contemporary affairs, and a continual study of antiquity; which, having reflected upon it with great and prolonged diligence, I now send, digested into a little volume, to your Magnificence.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 因此，我希望向您展示我对您的忠诚，但在我的财产中，我没有发现任何东西比通过对当代事务的长期经验和对古代的不断研究而获得的伟人行为的知识更珍贵或更有价值。经过长时间的辛勤思考，我现在将其消化成一小册，寄给陛下。

paragraphs.text: And although I may consider this work unworthy of your countenance, nevertheless I trust much to your benignity that it may be acceptable, seeing that it is not possible for me to make a better gift than to offer you the opportunity of understanding in the shortest time all that I have learnt in so many years, and with so many troubles and dangers; which work I have not embellished with swelling or magnificent words, nor stuffed with rounded periods, nor with any extrinsic allurements or adornments whatever, with which so many are accustomed to embellish their works; for I have wished either that no honour should be given it, or else that the truth of the matter and the weightiness of the theme shall make it acceptable.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 虽然我可能认为这项工作不值得您的赏识，但我非常相信您的仁慈，它可能会被接受，因为对我来说，没有什么比为您提供一个更好的礼物了，让您有机会在最短的时间内了解我在这么多年、经历了这么多麻烦和危险中所学到的一切；我没有用浮夸或华丽的文字来修饰这些作品，也没有塞满圆润的句号，也没有用任何外在的诱惑或装饰来修饰，而许多人习惯用这些来修饰他们的作品。因为我希望要么不给予它任何荣誉，要么事情的真相和主题的重要性应该使它可以接受。

paragraphs.text: Nor do I hold with those who regard it as a presumption if a man of low and humble condition dare to discuss and settle the concerns of princes; because, just as those who draw landscapes place themselves below in the plain to contemplate the nature of the mountains and of lofty places, and in order to contemplate the plains place themselves upon high mountains, even so to understand the nature of the people it needs to be a prince, and to understand that of princes it needs to be of the people.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我也不同意那些认为地位卑微的人敢于讨论和解决君主的事情的人；因为，正如画山水的人，置身于平地之中，才能观山色、高处之性，置高山之上，才能观平地，同样，要了解民性，就必须是君主，要了解君主性，就必须要在民中。

paragraphs.text: Take then, your Magnificence, this little gift in the spirit in which I send it; wherein, if it be diligently read and considered by you, you will learn my extreme desire that you should attain that greatness which fortune and your other attributes promise. And if your Magnificence from the summit of your greatness will sometimes turn your eyes to these lower regions, you will see how unmeritedly I suffer a great and continued malignity of fortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那么，陛下，本着我送出这份小礼物的精神，请收下它吧；其中，如果你认真阅读和思考，你就会了解到我的极度渴望，即你应该获得财富和其他属性所承诺的伟大。如果您的伟大顶峰有时会将您的目光转向这些较低的地区，您就会看到我是多么不值得地遭受巨大而持续的不幸。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: Old Mother Wiggle-Waggle

paragraphs.text: The fox and his wife they had a great strife, They never ate mustard in all their whole life; They ate their meat without fork or knife And loved to be picking a bone, e-ho!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸和他的妻子发生了很大的争执，他们一生没有吃过芥末。他们吃肉时不用叉子或刀，而且喜欢挑骨头，e-ho！

paragraphs.text: The fox went out, one still, clear night, And he prayed the moon to give him light, For he'd a long way to travel that night, Before he got back to his den-o!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸出去了，一个宁静、晴朗的夜晚，他祈祷月亮给他光明，因为那天晚上他要走很长的路，然后才能回到他的书房！

paragraphs.text: The fox when he came to yonder stile, He lifted his lugs and he listened a while! "Oh, ho!" said the fox, "it's but a short mile From this unto yonder wee town, e-ho!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸来到那边的阶梯时，举起了耳朵，听了一会儿！ “哦，嗬！”狐狸说：“从这个小镇到那边只有短短一英里，嗬！”

paragraphs.text: And first he arrived at a farmer's yard, Where the ducks and the geese declared it was hard, That their nerves should be shaken and their rest should be marred By the visits of Mister Fox-o!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 首先他到达了一个农夫的院子，那里的鸭子和鹅们宣称这很艰难，他们的神经应该受到动摇，他们的休息应该被狐狸先生的来访所破坏！

paragraphs.text: The fox when he came to the farmer's gate, Who should he see but the farmer's drake; "I love you well for your master's sake, And long to be picking your bones, e-ho!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸到了农家门口，除了农夫的小鸭子，他还能看到谁？ “看在你主人的份上，我很爱你，也很想挖你的骨头，嗬嗬！”

paragraphs.text: The grey goose she ran round the hay-stack, "Oh, ho!" said the fox, "you are very fat; You'll grease my beard and ride on my back From this into yonder wee town, e-ho!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 灰鹅绕着干草堆跑，“哦，嗬！”狐狸说：“你太胖了；你要给我的胡子涂油，然后骑在我的背上，从这里到那边的小镇，嗬嗬！”

paragraphs.text: Then he took the grey goose by her sleeve, And said: "Madam Grey Goose, by your leave I'll take you away without reprieve, And carry you back to my den-o!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后他拉着灰鹅的袖子，说道：“灰鹅女士，请您允许，我会毫不犹豫地带您离开，带您回到我的书房！”

paragraphs.text: And he seized the black duck by the neck, And slung him all across his back, The black duck cried out "quack, quack, quack," With his legs all dangling down-o!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他抓住黑鸭子的脖子，把他整个甩在背上，黑鸭子“嘎嘎嘎嘎”地叫着，双腿都悬空了——哦！

paragraphs.text: Old Mother Wiggle-Waggle hopped out of bed, Out of the window she popped her old head; "Oh! husband, oh! husband, the grey goose is gone, And the fox is off to his den, oh!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老妈妈威格威格从床上跳了起来，她从窗外伸出了她那老头； “噢！老公，噢！老公，灰鹅不见了，狐狸也回洞去了，噢！”

paragraphs.text: Then the old man got up in his red cap, And swore he would catch the fox in a trap; But the fox was too cunning, and gave him the slip, And ran through the town, the town, oh!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后，老人戴上红帽子站起来，发誓他会用陷阱抓住狐狸；但狐狸太狡猾了，溜了他，跑过小镇，小镇，哦！

paragraphs.text: When he got to the top of the hill, He blew his trumpet both loud and shrill, For joy that he was safe and sound Through the town, oh!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他到达山顶时，他大声而尖锐地吹响了喇叭，为他平安无事穿过城镇而高兴，哦！

paragraphs.text: But at last he arrived at his home again, To his dear little foxes, eight, nine, ten, Says he "You're in luck, here's a fine fat duck With his legs all dangling down-o!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但最后他又回到了家，对他亲爱的小狐狸，八、九、十，说：“你们很幸运，这是一只漂亮的肥鸭，他的腿都垂下来了——哦！”

paragraphs.text: So he sat down together with his hungry wife, And they did very well without fork or knife, They never ate a better duck in all their life, And the little ones picked the bones-o!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他和他饥饿的妻子一起坐下来，他们没有叉子或刀就做得很好，他们一生中从未吃过更好的鸭子，小孩子们捡起了骨头——哦！

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 12. ALTRUISTIC STORIES

paragraphs.text: Stories of unselfish heroism appeal to every age, but they find their strongest interest for the spirit of youth during the years of middle adolescence. Such stories of self-sacrifice may be selected from the Bible, history, fiction, or modern life. They not only show what is noble action, but touch the soul with the contagion of self-sacrificing deeds. From the Ethical Index, on page 291, under Altruism, Loyalty, Self-sacrifice, and such synonyms, a list of altruistic stories may be made.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 无私英雄主义的故事对每​​个年龄段都有吸引力，但他们对青春期中期的青春精神最感兴趣。这些自我牺牲的故事可以选自圣经、历史、小说或现代生活。他们不仅展示了什么是高尚的行为，而且以自我牺牲的行为感染着灵魂。从《道德索引》第 291 页，在利他主义、忠诚、自我牺牲和此类同义词下，可以列出一份利他故事列表。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 8. THE CROW AND THE PITCHER

paragraphs.text: Once a Crow who was very thirsty found a pitcher with a little water at the bottom which he was unable to reach. He tried to overturn the pitcher but it was too heavy. "Ah! Ah! I know what I'll do," he said. So he gathered up pebbles from the ground, and one after another dropped them into the pitcher until the water gradually reached the top. Then the wise Crow was able to drink all the water he wanted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只非常口渴的乌鸦发现了一个水罐，底部有一点水，但他够不到。他试图打翻水罐，但它太重了。 “啊！啊！我知道我要做什么，”他说。于是，他从地上捡起小石子，一个又一个地扔进水罐里，直到水逐渐到达顶部。然后聪明的乌鸦就能够喝到他想要喝的水了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A very young Fox, who had never before seen a Lion, happened to meet one in the forest. A single look was enough to send the Fox off at top speed for the nearest hiding place.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只非常年轻的狐狸以前从未见过狮子，但碰巧在森林里遇到了一只狮子。只要看一眼就足以让狐狸以最快的速度逃往最近的藏身之处。

paragraphs.text: The second time the Fox saw the Lion he stopped behind a tree to look at him a moment before slinking away. But the third time, the Fox went boldly up to the Lion and, without turning a hair, said, "Hello, there, old top."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸第二次看到狮子时，他在一棵树后面停下来看了他一会儿，然后就溜走了。但第三次，狐狸勇敢地走到狮子面前，头也不回地说道：“你好，老头儿。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: When you have finished reading, write the answers to these questions. If you can not answer immediately, find the answer somewhere in the story, but do not read it all again; read only enough to get the correct answer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 读完后，写下这些问题的答案。如果你不能立即回答，请在故事中的某个地方找到答案，但不要再读一遍；只阅读足以获得正确答案的内容。

paragraphs.text: 1. What two reasons can you find to account for the greater loss by fire in the United States than in Europe?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 你能找到哪两个原因来解释美国的火灾损失比欧洲更大？

paragraphs.text: 2. Which of these two reasons can be the more quickly removed? Which one can you help to remove?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 这两个原因哪一个可以更快地排除？你可以帮忙删除哪一个？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 14. THE CONCEITED GRASSHOPPER

paragraphs.text: One day a very young Grasshopper and an old Rooster met out in a field. "I can jump higher than anybody," chirped the Grasshopper. "All right; let me see you do it," said the Rooster, at the same time opening his mouth wide as if he meant to yawn. "Here I go, then," cried the Grasshopper. He jumped so high he landed right in the mouth of the Rooster, who gulped him down. That was the end of the boasting Grasshopper.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只年幼的蚱蜢和一只老公鸡在田野里相遇。 “我比任何人都跳得更高。”蚱蜢叽叽喳喳地说。 “好吧，让我看看你怎么做，”公鸡说着，同时张大了嘴，好像要打哈欠。 “那我走了，”蚱蜢喊道。他跳得太高了，正好落在公鸡的嘴里，公鸡把他吞了下去。自吹自擂的蚱蜢就这样结束了。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE HEDGEHOG

paragraphs.text: A Fox, swimming across a river, was barely able to reach the bank, where he lay bruised and exhausted from his struggle with the swift current. Soon a swarm of blood-sucking flies settled on him; but he lay quietly, still too weak to run away from them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狐狸游过一条河，好不容易才到达岸边，他躺在那里，因与湍急的水流搏斗而伤痕累累，精疲力尽。很快，一群吸血苍蝇落在了他身上。但他静静地躺着，仍然太虚弱，无法逃离他们。

paragraphs.text: "No, no!" exclaimed the Fox, "do not disturb them! They have taken all they can hold. If you drive them away, another greedy swarm will come and take the little blood I have left."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，不！”狐狸喊道：“别打扰他们！他们已经夺走了他们所能拥有的一切。如果你把他们赶走，另一群贪婪的蜂群就会过来，夺走我仅剩的一点血。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE PEACOCK AND THE CRANE

paragraphs.text: A Peacock, puffed up with vanity, met a Crane one day, and to impress him spread his gorgeous tail in the Sun.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只自负的孔雀遇到了一只鹤，为了给鹤留下深刻的印象，孔雀在阳光下展开了它华丽的尾巴。

paragraphs.text: "Look," he said. "What have you to compare with this? I am dressed in all the glory of the rainbow, while your feathers are gray as dust!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看，”他说。 “你有什么可与此相比的？我身着彩虹般的光彩，而你的羽毛却灰如尘土！”

paragraphs.text: "Follow me if you can," he said. But the Peacock stood where he was among the birds of the barnyard, while the Crane soared in freedom far up into the blue sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果可以的话，跟我来，”他说。但孔雀站在谷仓里的鸟儿中间，而鹤则自由地飞向蓝天。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON

paragraphs.text: How do you like to go up in a swing, Up in the air so blue? Oh, I do think it the pleasantest thing Ever a child can do!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你喜欢荡秋千吗，在如此蔚蓝的天空中？哦，我确实认为这是孩子能做的最愉快的事情！

paragraphs.text: Up in the air and over the wall, Till I can see so wide, Rivers and trees and cattle and all Over the countryside--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在空中，越过墙，直到我能看到如此广阔，河流、树木、牛群以及整个乡村——

paragraphs.text: Till I look down on the garden green, Down on the roof so brown-- Up in the air I go flying again, Up in the air and down!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 直到我俯视绿色的花园， 俯视棕色的屋顶—— 在空中我再次飞翔， 在空中又向下！

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 7. THE FOX AND THE GRAPES

paragraphs.text: A Fox who was hungry saw some large, juicy grapes on a vine high up in a tree. "How good they will taste," said he; "I am going to have some of them." Then he gave a run and leaped as high as he could, but the grapes were still far above his head. He could not reach them no matter how high he jumped. At last he trotted off in a rage, muttering, "I know those are sour grapes and not worth eating."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只饥饿的狐狸看到高高的藤蔓上有一些又大又多汁的葡萄。 “它们尝起来多美味啊，”他说。 “我要一些。”然后他跑了起来，尽可能地跳高，但葡萄仍然离他头顶很远。无论他跳得多高，他都够不到他们。最后他怒气冲冲地跑开了，嘟囔道：“我知道那是酸葡萄心理，不值得吃。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: DICK WHITTINGTON--II

paragraphs.text: The things were sold, and when the ship came back, each person had the money for what he had sent.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 东西都卖了，船回来的时候，每个人都拿到了自己所寄东西的钱。

paragraphs.text: He had gone but a short way when he heard the sound of the six great bells of Bow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他刚走了没多久，就听到了弓六大钟的声音。

paragraphs.text: "It is strange that the bells should speak to me," said Dick, "but if I am to be Lord Mayor of London, I will gladly turn back."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “钟声竟然对我说话，这很奇怪，”迪克说，“但如果我成为伦敦市长，我会很乐意回去。”

paragraphs.text: "I hope they have not missed me," said Dick, as he gently opened the door and stole softly in.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我希望他们没有错过我，”迪克一边说，一边轻轻地打开门，轻轻地溜了进去。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: ALL THINGS BEAUTIFUL

paragraphs.text: All things bright and beautiful, All creatures great and small, All things wise and wonderful, The Lord God made them all.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一切明亮而美丽的事物，一切伟大和渺小的生物，一切智慧而美妙的事物，主上帝创造了它们。

paragraphs.text: Each little flower that opens, Each little bird that sings, He made their glowing colors, He made their tiny wings.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每朵开放的小花，每只歌唱的小鸟，他创造了它们绚丽的色彩，他创造了它们微小的翅膀。

paragraphs.text: The cold wind in the winter, The pleasant summer sun, The ripe fruits in the garden, He made them every one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天的寒风，夏天的宜人阳光，花园里成熟的果实，每一个都是他创造的。

paragraphs.text: He gave us eyes to see them, And lips that we might tell How great is God Almighty, Who hath made all things well.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他给了我们眼睛来观察它们，给了我们嘴唇让我们可以讲述全能的上帝是多么伟大，他使万物变得美好。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: BED IN SUMMER

paragraphs.text: In winter I get up at night And dress by yellow candle-light. In summer, quite the other way, I have to go to bed by day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 冬天我晚上起床，在黄色烛光下穿衣。在夏天，情况恰恰相反，我白天必须上床睡觉。

paragraphs.text: I have to go to bed and see The birds still hopping on the tree, Or hear the grown-up people's feet Still going past me in the street.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我必须上床睡觉，看看鸟儿仍在树上跳跃，或者听到大人的脚步声仍在街上从我身边经过。

paragraphs.text: And does it not seem hard to you, When all the sky is clear and blue, And I should like so much to play, To have to go to bed by day?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当所有的天空都晴朗而蔚蓝时，我会非常喜欢玩耍，而白天却必须上床睡觉，这对你来说不似乎很难吗？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE LION
