paragraphs.text: The sand of the desert is sodden red,-- Red with the wreck of a square that broke;-- The Gatling's jammed and the Colonel dead, And the regiment blind with dust and smoke. The river of death has brimmed his banks, And England's far, and Honor a name, But the voice of a schoolboy rallies the ranks: "Play up! play up! and play the game!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 沙漠的沙子是湿透的红色，—— 红色的，是一块破碎的广场的残骸；—— 加特林机被卡住了，上校死了， 军团被灰尘和烟雾蒙蔽了。死亡之河已经淹没了他的河岸，英格兰很远，荣耀一个名字，但是一个学生的声音聚集了队伍：“玩起来！玩起来！玩游戏！”

paragraphs.text: This is the word that year by year While in her place the school is set, Every one of her sons must hear, And none that hears it dare forget. This they all with a joyful mind Bear through life like a torch in flame, And falling fling to the host behind: "Play up! play up! and play the game!" --HENRY NEWBOLT.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这是年复一年，当学校在她的地方设立时，她的每个儿子都必须听到的一句话，没有一个听到的人敢忘记。他们都怀着喜悦的心情，像火焰中的火炬一样承受着生命，然后向身后的主人扔去：“玩起来！玩起来！玩游戏！” ——亨利·纽博特。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE MONKEY AND THE DOLPHIN

paragraphs.text: It happened once upon a time that a certain Greek ship bound for Athens was wrecked off the coast close to Piraeus, the port of Athens. Had it not been for the Dolphins, who at that time were very friendly toward mankind and especially toward Athenians, all would have perished. But the Dolphins took the shipwrecked people on their backs and swam with them to shore.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 曾几何时，一艘开往雅典的希腊船只在雅典港口比雷埃夫斯附近的海岸失事。如果没有海豚，当时它们对人类特别是雅典人非常友好，那么所有人都会灭亡。但海豚们把遇难者背在背上，并与他们一起游到岸边。

paragraphs.text: Now it was the custom among the Greeks to take their pet monkeys and dogs with them whenever they went on a voyage. So when one of the Dolphins saw a Monkey struggling in the water, he thought it was a man, and made the Monkey climb up on his back. Then off he swam with him toward the shore.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在，希腊人有一种习俗，每当他们出海时，都会带着他们的宠物猴子和狗。当其中一只海豚看到一只猴子在水里挣扎时，他以为是一个人，并让猴子爬到他的背上。然后他就和他一起游向岸边。

paragraphs.text: "Yes, yes," replied the Monkey. "Indeed, I do. I am with him constantly. Piraeus is my very best friend."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是的，是的。”猴子回答道。 “确实如此。我一直和他在一起。比雷埃夫斯是我最好的朋友。”

paragraphs.text: This answer took the Dolphin by surprise, and, turning his head, he now saw what it was he was carrying. Without more ado, he dived and left the foolish Monkey to take care of himself, while he swam off in search of some human being to save.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个答案让海豚大吃一惊，他转过头，看到了自己手里拿着的东西。他毫不犹豫地潜入水中，留下愚蠢的猴子照顾自己，而他自己则游去寻找可以拯救的人。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: NOTES AND QUESTIONS

paragraphs.text: Biography. Charles Sumner (1811-1874), an American statesman and orator, was born in Boston, Massachusetts. He became United States senator in 1851. "The Flag of Our Country" is taken from an address delivered in 1867 at Cooper Institute in New York.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 传。查尔斯·萨姆纳（Charles Sumner，1811-1874），美国政治家和演说家，出生于马萨诸塞州波士顿。 1851 年，他成为美国参议员。《我们国家的旗帜》取自 1867 年在纽约库珀学院发表的演讲。

paragraphs.text: Discussion. 1. Each paragraph in this selection has a separate message. Does the first paragraph fit America only, or could an Englishman say the same thing about his national flag, and a Frenchman of his? What then is the thing that any flag represents to the citizen of the country to which he belongs? 2. What facts peculiar to America does the second paragraph give you? 3. How many stripes has the flag? 4. How many stars were in the first American flag? How many are there now? 5. What is meant by "union past and present"? 6. "White is for purity"--in what way does this express the ideals of the founders of our country? 7. Do you know the rules for the raising and lowering of the flag? 8. Find in the Glossary the meaning of: rippling; reverence; bunting; proclaim; original; maintain; constituting; valor; cherished. 9. Pronounce: symbolizes; sublimely; alternate; constellation.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 讨论。 1. 此选择中的每个段落都有单独的信息。第一段是否只适合美国，或者一个英国人和一个法国人可以对他的国旗说同样的话吗？那么，任何旗帜对于其所属国家的公民来说代表什么呢？ 2. 第二段给你提供了哪些美国特有的事实？ 3. 国旗有多少条条纹？ 4.第一面美国国旗上有多少颗星星？现在有多少人？ 5.“古今结合”是什么意思？ 6、“白色代表纯洁”——这如何表达了我国开国元勋的理想？ 7. 你知道升旗和降旗的规则吗？ 8. 在术语表中找到以下含义： 涟漪；尊敬;彩旗;宣布;原来的;维持;构成；勇气;珍惜。 9. 发音：象征；崇高地;备用;星座。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: How to Tell Stories to Children: THE BUCKWHEAT

paragraphs.text: Down by the river were fields of barley and rye and golden oats. Wheat grew there, too, and the heaviest and richest ears bent lowest, in humility. Opposite the corn was a field of buckwheat, but the buckwheat never bent; it held its head proud and stiff on the stem.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 河边是大麦、黑麦和金燕麦的田地。那里也长着小麦，最重、最肥沃的麦穗谦卑地弯得最低。玉米的对面是一片荞麦地，但荞麦地从来不弯腰；它把头骄傲地昂在树干上，僵硬地昂着。

paragraphs.text: One day a dreadful storm came. The field-flowers folded their leaves together, and bowed their heads. But the buckwheat stood straight and proud.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一场可怕的暴风雨来了。田野里的花儿把叶子合在一起，低下了头。但荞麦却挺直而骄傲。

paragraphs.text: "Bend your head, as we do!" warned the golden wheat-ears; "the angel of the storm is coming; he will strike you down."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “像我们一样低下你的头！”警告金麦穗； “风暴天使来了，他会击倒你。”

paragraphs.text: Then the old willow-tree spoke: "Close your flowers and bend your leaves. Do not look at the lightning when the cloud bursts. Even men cannot do that; the sight of heaven would strike them blind. Much less can we who are so inferior to them!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老柳树说道：“闭花折叶，云破时莫看雷电，连人都做不到，天见了就会瞎眼，更何况是我们这些不如人的人呢！”

paragraphs.text: "'Inferior,' indeed!" said the buckwheat. "Now I _will_ look!" And he looked straight up, while the lightning flashed across the sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “确实‘低劣’！”荞麦说。 “现在我就看看！”他抬头看去，天空中闪电划过。

paragraphs.text: When the dreadful storm had passed, the flowers and the wheat raised their drooping heads, clean and refreshed in the pure, sweet air. The willow-tree shook the gentle drops from its leaves.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当可怕的暴风雨过去后，花朵和小麦都抬起了低垂的头，在纯净、甜美的空气中显得干净而清新。柳树抖落了叶子上轻轻的水珠。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE ASTROLOGER

paragraphs.text: A man who lived a long time ago believed that he could read the future in the stars. He called himself an Astrologer, and spent his time at night gazing at the sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个很久以前的人相信他可以从星星中预知未来。他自称占星家，晚上花时间凝视天空。

paragraphs.text: One evening he was walking along the open road outside the village. His eyes were fixed on the stars. He thought he saw there that the end of the world was at hand, when all at once, down he went into a hole full of mud and water.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天傍晚，他沿着村外的大路散步。他的眼睛盯着星星。他以为自己看到了世界末日即将来临，突然间，他掉进了一个充满泥土和水的洞里。

paragraphs.text: There he stood up to his ears, in the muddy water, and madly clawing at the slippery sides of the hole in his effort to climb out.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他在泥水里站到了耳朵边，疯狂地抓着洞滑的边缘，努力想爬出来。

paragraphs.text: His cries for help soon brought the villagers running. As they pulled him out of the mud, one of them said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的求救声很快让村民们纷纷逃跑。当他们把他从泥里拉出来时，其中一个人说：

paragraphs.text: "You pretend to read the future in the stars, and yet you fail to see what is at your feet! This may teach you to pay more attention to what is right in front of you, and let the future take care of itself."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你假装在星空中预知未来，却看不到脚下的一切！这也许会教会你更加关注眼前的事情，让未来自然发展。”

paragraphs.text: "What use is it," said another, "to read the stars, when you can't see what's right here on the earth?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当你看不到地球上的情况时，”另一个人说，“看星星有什么用呢？”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE CAT AND THE FOX

paragraphs.text: Once a Cat and a Fox were traveling together. As they went along, picking up provisions on the way--a stray mouse here, a fat chicken there--they began an argument to while away the time between bites. And, as usually happens when comrades argue, the talk began to get personal.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，一只猫和一只狐狸一起旅行。他们一边走，一边捡拾食物——这里有一只流浪老鼠，那里有一只肥鸡——他们开始争吵，以消磨吃饭之间的时间。而且，正如同志们争论时通常发生的那样，谈话开始变得个人化。

paragraphs.text: "You think you are extremely clever, don't you?" said the Fox. "Do you pretend to know more than I? Why, I know a whole sackful of tricks!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你认为你非常聪明，不是吗？”狐狸说。 “你假装知道的比我多吗？哎呀，我知道一大堆把戏！”

paragraphs.text: "Well," retorted the Cat, "I admit I know one trick only, but that one, let me tell you, is worth a thousand of yours!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好吧，”猫反驳道，“我承认我只会一招，但我告诉你，这一招比你的一千招还值钱！”

paragraphs.text: Just then, close by, they heard a hunter's horn and the yelping of a pack of hounds. In an instant the Cat was up a tree, hiding among the leaves.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，附近传来了猎人的号角声和一群猎犬的吠叫声。瞬间，猫就爬上了树，躲在树叶之中。

paragraphs.text: But the Fox had so many plans for escape he could not decide which one to try first. He dodged here and there with the hounds at his heels. He doubled on his tracks, he ran at top speed, he entered a dozen burrows,--but all in vain. The hounds caught him, and soon put an end to the boaster and all his tricks.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但狐狸有太多逃跑的计划，他无法决定先尝试哪一个。他东躲西藏，猎犬跟在他后面。他在轨道上加倍前进，以最快的速度奔跑，进入了十几个洞穴——但一切都是徒劳。猎犬抓住了他，很快就结束了这个吹牛者和他的所有诡计。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE EAGLE AND THE BEETLE

paragraphs.text: A Beetle once begged the Eagle to spare a Hare which had run to her for protection. But the Eagle pounced upon her prey, the sweep of her great wings tumbling the Beetle a dozen feet away. Furious at the disrespect shown her, the Beetle flew to the Eagle's nest and rolled out the eggs. Not one did she spare. The Eagle's grief and anger knew no bounds, but who had done the cruel deed she did not know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一次，一只甲虫恳求老鹰放过一只跑向她寻求保护的野兔。但老鹰猛扑向她的猎物，她巨大的翅膀一扫，就把甲虫打翻了十几英尺远。甲虫对她的不尊重感到愤怒，飞到老鹰的巢里，把蛋滚了出来。她一个也没有放过。老鹰悲痛欲绝，愤怒不已，但她不知道是谁做了这件残忍的事。

paragraphs.text: Next year the Eagle built her nest far up on a mountain crag; but the Beetle found it and again destroyed the eggs. In despair the Eagle now implored great Jupiter to let her place her eggs in his lap. There none would dare harm them. But the Beetle buzzed about Jupiter's head, and made him rise to drive her away; and the eggs rolled from his lap.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二年，老鹰在远处的山崖上筑了巢。但甲虫发现了它并再次毁掉了蛋。绝望中，老鹰恳求伟大的朱庇特让她把蛋放在他的腿上。没有人敢伤害他们。但是甲虫在朱庇特的头上嗡嗡叫，让他站起来把她赶走。鸡蛋从他的腿上滚下来。

paragraphs.text: Now the Beetle told the reason for her action, and Jupiter had to acknowledge the justice of her cause. And they say that ever after, while the Eagle's eggs lie in the nest in spring, the Beetle still sleeps in the ground. For so Jupiter commanded.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在甲虫说出了她行动的原因，朱庇特不得不承认她的事业是正义的。他们说，从此以后，当春天老鹰的蛋躺在巢中时，甲虫仍然睡在地下。朱庇特如此命令。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Children's Hour, Volume 3: Hang up the Baby's Stocking

paragraphs.text: Hang up the baby's stocking: Be sure you don't forget; The dear little dimpled darling! She ne'er saw Christmas yet; But I've told her all about it, And she opened her big blue eyes; And I'm sure she understood it— She looked so funny and wise.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 挂好宝宝的袜子：一定不要忘记；亲爱的小酒窝宝贝！她还没见过圣诞节呢；但我已经告诉她这一切了，她睁开了蓝色的大眼睛；我确信她明白这一点——她看起来很有趣而且很聪明。

paragraphs.text: Dear! what a tiny stocking! It doesn't take much to hold Such little pink toes as baby's Away from the frost and cold; But then for the baby's Christmas It will never do at all; Why, Santa wouldn't be looking For anything half so small.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亲爱的！多么小的一只袜子啊！不需要太多的力气就能抱住婴儿般粉嫩的小脚趾，远离霜冻和寒冷；但对于宝宝的圣诞节来说，这根本就不行；为什么，圣诞老人不会寻找这么小的东西。

paragraphs.text: I know what will do for the baby. I've thought of the very best plan: I'll borrow a stocking of grandma, The longest that ever I can; And you'll hang it by mine, dear mother, Right here in the corner, so! And write a letter to Santa, And fasten it on the toe.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我知道要为宝宝做什么。我已经想到了最好的计划：我会借奶奶的一只袜子，尽可能长的；你会把它挂在我的身边，亲爱的妈妈，就在这个角落里，所以！给圣诞老人写一封信，并把它系在脚趾上。

paragraphs.text: Write, "This is the baby's stocking That hangs in the corner here; You never have seen her, Santa, For she only came this year; But she's just the blessedest baby! And now before you go, Just cram her stocking with goodies, From the top clean down to the toe."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 写下，“这是挂在角落里的婴儿的长袜；圣诞老人，你从未见过她，因为她今年才来；但她只是最受祝福的婴儿！现在在你走之前，只需在她的长袜里塞满好吃的东西，从上到下干净。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: GOOD NIGHT AND GOOD MORNING

paragraphs.text: A fair little girl sat under a tree, Sewing as long as her eyes could see; Then smoothed her work and folded it right, And said, "Dear work, good night, good night!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个美丽的小女孩坐在树下，缝纫着她眼睛所能看到的一切；然后把她的作品抚平，折好，说：“亲爱的作品，晚安，晚安！”

paragraphs.text: Such a number of rooks came over her head, Crying "Caw, caw!" on their way to bed. She said, as she watched their curious flight, "Little black things, good night, good night!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这么多白嘴鸦从她头顶飞过，“哇哇哇”地叫着。在上床睡觉的路上。当她看着它们好奇地飞翔时，她说：“黑色的小东西，晚安，晚安！”

paragraphs.text: The horses neighed, and the oxen lowed, The sheep's "bleat, bleat!" came over the road; All seeming to say, with a quiet delight, "Good little girl, good night, good night!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 马嘶牛鸣，羊咩咩咩！过了马路；一切似乎都带着平静的喜悦在说：“好小姑娘，晚安，晚安！”

paragraphs.text: She did not say to the sun, "Good night!" Though she saw him there like a ball of light, For she knew he had God's time to keep All over the world, and never could sleep.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她没有对太阳说：“晚安！”尽管她看到他就像一个光球， 因为她知道他有上帝的时间来保留整个世界，并且永远无法入睡。

paragraphs.text: The tall pink foxglove bowed his head; The violets curtsied and went to bed; And good little Lucy tied up her hair, And said, on her knees, her favorite prayer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 高大的粉红色毛地黄低下了头；紫罗兰们行了屈膝礼，上床睡觉了。善良的小露西把她的头发扎起来，跪下念出她最喜欢的祈祷词。

paragraphs.text: And while on her pillow she softly lay, She knew nothing more till again it was day; And all things said to the beautiful sun, "Good morning, good morning! our work is begun."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当她轻轻地躺在枕头上时，她什么也不知道，直到天又亮了；万物都对着美丽的太阳说：“早上好，早上好！我们的工作开始了。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Fourth Eclectic Reader: LXXVII. LIVING ON A FARM. (220)

paragraphs.text: 1. How brightly through the mist of years, My quiet country home appears! My father busy all the day In plowing corn or raking hay; My mother moving with delight Among the milk pans, silver-bright; We children, just from school set free, Filling the garden with our glee. The blood of life was flowing warm When I was living on a farm.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1. 透过岁月的迷雾，我宁静的乡村家园显得多么明亮！我的父亲整天忙着犁玉米或耙干草；我的母亲高兴地在奶锅里走来走去，银光闪闪；我们这些刚放学的孩子， 花园里充满了我们的欢乐。当我生活在农场时，生命的血液在温暖地流淌。

paragraphs.text: 2. I hear the sweet churchgoing bell, As o'er the fields its music fell, I see the country neighbors round Gathering beneath the pleasant sound; They stop awhile beside the door, To talk their homely matters o'er The springing corn, the ripening grain, And "how we need a little rain;" "A little sun would do no harm, We want good weather for the farm."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 我听到甜美的教堂钟声，当音乐落在田野上时，我看到周围的乡村邻居聚集在宜人的声音下；他们在门边停了一会儿，谈论他们的家常事，关于正在发芽的玉米，成熟的谷物，以及“我们多么需要一场小雨；” “一点阳光不会有什么坏处，我们希望农场有好天气。”

paragraphs.text: 3. When autumn came, what joy to see The gathering of the husking bee, To hear the voices keeping tune, Of girls and boys beneath the moon, To mark the golden corn ears bright, More golden in the yellow light! Since I have learned the ways of men, I often turn to these again, And feel life wore its highest charm. When I was living on the farm.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 3. 秋天来了，看到剥壳的蜜蜂聚集，听到月下女孩和男孩的声音，多么高兴，把金色的玉米穗标记得明亮，在黄光下更加金黄！自从我学会了人之道，我常常再翻看这些， 并感到生命具有最高的魅力。当我住在农场的时候。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE BIRDS, THE BEASTS, AND THE BAT

paragraphs.text: The Birds and the Beasts declared war against each other. No compromise was possible, and so they went at it tooth and claw. It is said the quarrel grew out of the persecution the race of Geese suffered at the teeth of the Fox family. The Beasts, too, had cause for fight. The Eagle was constantly pouncing on the Hare, and the Owl dined daily on Mice.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 鸟类和野兽向彼此宣战。妥协是不可能的，所以他们全力以赴。据说，这场争吵源于鹅族遭受狐狸家族的迫害。野兽们也有战斗的理由。老鹰不断地扑向野兔，猫头鹰每天都以老鼠为食。

paragraphs.text: It was a terrible battle. Many a Hare and many a Mouse died. Chickens and Geese fell by the score--and the victor always stopped for a feast.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这是一场可怕的战斗。许多野兔和老鼠死了。鸡和鹅都以比分落败——而胜利者总是停下来享用一顿盛宴。

paragraphs.text: Now the Bat family had not openly joined either side. They were a very politic race. So when they saw the Birds getting the better of it, they were Birds for all there was in it. But when the tide of battle turned, they immediately sided with the Beasts.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在蝙蝠家族还没有公开加入任何一方。他们是一个非常政治化的种族。因此，当他们看到鸟队取得了更好的成绩时，他们就成为了鸟队。但当战局逆转时，他们立即站在了野兽一边。

paragraphs.text: When the battle was over, the conduct of the Bats was discussed at the peace conference. Such deceit was unpardonable, and Birds and Beasts made common cause to drive out the Bats. And since then the Bat family hides in dark towers and deserted ruins, flying out only in the night.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 战斗结束后，和平会议上讨论了蝙蝠的行为。这种欺骗行为是不可原谅的，鸟兽们共同努力驱逐蝙蝠。从此以后，蝙蝠家族就躲在黑暗的高塔和荒废的废墟里，只在夜间飞出。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE FROG AND THE MOUSE

paragraphs.text: A young Mouse in search of adventure was running along the bank of a pond where lived a Frog. When the Frog saw the Mouse, he swam to the bank and croaked:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只寻求冒险的小老鼠沿着池塘边奔跑，池塘里住着一只青蛙。当青蛙看到老鼠时，他游到岸边并呱呱叫：

paragraphs.text: The Mouse did not need much coaxing, for he was very anxious to see the world and everything in it. But though he could swim a little, he did not dare risk going into the pond without some help.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老鼠不需要太多的哄，因为他非常渴望看看这个世界和其中的一切。尽管他会游泳，但在没有帮助的情况下，他不敢冒险进入池塘。

paragraphs.text: The Frog had a plan. He tied the Mouse's leg to his own with a tough reed. Then into the pond he jumped, dragging his foolish companion with him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 青蛙有一个计划。他用一根坚韧的芦苇把老鼠的腿绑在自己的腿上。然后他拖着愚蠢的同伴跳进了池塘。

paragraphs.text: The Mouse soon had enough of it and wanted to return to shore; but the treacherous Frog had other plans. He pulled the Mouse down under the water and drowned him. But before he could untie the reed that bound him to the dead Mouse, a Hawk came sailing over the pond. Seeing the body of the Mouse floating on the water, the Hawk swooped down, seized the Mouse and carried it off, with the Frog dangling from its leg. Thus at one swoop he had caught both meat and fish for his dinner.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老鼠很快就受够了，想要返回岸边。但奸诈的青蛙另有计划。他把老鼠拉到水下淹死了。但在他解开绑在死老鼠身上的芦苇之前，一只鹰飞过池塘。鹰看到老鼠的尸体漂浮在水面上，就俯冲下来，抓住了老鼠，把它带走了，青蛙则挂在它的腿上。就这样，他一下子就钓到了肉和鱼作为晚餐。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: CHARLES KINGSLEY

paragraphs.text: "O Mary, go and call the cattle home, And call the cattle home, And call the cattle home Across the sands of Dee!" The western wind was wild and dank with foam, And all alone went she.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “噢，玛丽，去叫牛回家，叫牛回家，叫牛回家，穿过迪伊的沙滩！”西风狂野，湿漉漉的泡沫，她孤身一人。

paragraphs.text: The western tide crept up along the sand, And o'er and o'er the sand, And round and round the sand, As far as eye could see. The rolling mist came down and hid the land: And never home came she.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 西潮沿着沙子爬上来，越过沙子，越过沙子，一圈又一圈地沙子，一望无际。滚滚的雾气降临，遮盖了大地：她再也没有回家。

paragraphs.text: "Oh! is it weed, or fish, or floating hair-- A tress of golden hair, A drownèd maiden's hair Above the nets at sea? Was never salmon yet that shone so fair Among the stakes on Dee."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦！是杂草，还是鱼，还是漂浮的头发——一缕金色的头发，一根被淹死的少女的头发，在海上的渔网上方？在迪伊的木桩中，从来没有鲑鱼如此美丽地闪耀过。”

paragraphs.text: They rowed her in across the sailing foam, The cruel crawling foam, The cruel hungry foam, To her grave beside the sea: But still the boatmen hear her call the cattle home Across the sands of Dee!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们划着她划过航行的泡沫， 残酷爬行的泡沫， 残酷饥饿的泡沫， 到她海边的坟墓： 但船夫仍然听到她呼唤牛回家 穿过迪伊的沙滩！

paragraphs.text: The next two poems, by Alfred Tennyson (1809-1892), are very well-known songs. "What Does Little Birdie Say" is the mother's song in "Sea Dreams." "Sweet and Low" is one of the best of the lyrics in "The Princess," and a favorite among the greatest lullabies.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 接下来的两首诗是阿尔弗雷德·丁尼生（Alfred Tennyson，1809-1892）的一首非常著名的歌曲。 《小鸟说什么》是《海之梦》中母亲的歌曲。 “Sweet and Low”是《公主》中最好的歌词之一，也是最伟大的摇篮曲中最受欢迎的一首。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: Charles Sumner

paragraphs.text: There is the national flag. He must be cold indeed who can look upon its folds, rippling in the breeze, without pride of country. If he be in a foreign land, the flag is companionship and country itself, with all its endearments. Its highest beauty is in what it symbolizes. It is because it represents all, that all gaze at it with delight and reverence.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有国旗。谁能看到它在微风中荡漾的褶皱，而没有国家的自豪感，他一定是真的冷了。如果他在异国他乡，国旗就是陪伴和国家本身，以及所有的爱意。它的最高美在于它所象征的东西。正因为它代表了一切，所以所有人都怀着喜悦和崇敬的目光注视着它。

paragraphs.text: It is a piece of bunting lifted in the air; but it speaks sublimely, and every part has a voice. Its stripes of alternate red and white proclaim the original union of thirteen states to maintain the Declaration of Independence. Its stars of white on a field of blue proclaim that union of states constituting our national constellation, which receives a new star with every new state. The two together signify union past and present.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它是一块举在空中的彩旗；但它说话庄严，每个部分都有声音。其红白相间的条纹宣告了十三个州最初的联盟，以维护《独立宣言》。蓝色背景上的白色星星宣告着构成我们国家星座的国家联盟，每个新国家都会获得一颗新星星。两者合在一起象征着过去和现在的结合。

paragraphs.text: The very colors have a language which was officially recognized by our fathers. White is for purity, red for valor, blue for justice; and all together, bunting, stripes, stars, and colors, blazing in the sky, make the flag of our country to be cherished by all our hearts, to be upheld by all our hands.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 颜色本身就有一种被我们的祖先正式认可的语言。白色代表纯洁，红色代表勇敢，蓝色代表正义；彩旗、条纹、星星和颜色一起在天空中闪耀，使我们国家的国旗受到我们所有人的心的珍惜，受到我们所有人的双手的拥护。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Blue Fairy Book: LIII. THE HEDLEY KOW

paragraphs.text: _Source._--Told to Mrs. Balfour by Mrs. M. of S. Northumberland. Mrs. M.'s mother told the tale as having happened to a person she had known when young: she had herself seen the Hedley Kow twice, once as a donkey and once as a wisp of straw. "Kow" must not be confounded with the more prosaic animal with a "C."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 资料来源：南诺森伯兰郡的 M. 夫人告诉贝尔福夫人。 M夫人的母亲讲述了这个故事，这个故事发生在她年轻时认识的一个人身上：她自己见过赫德利·柯两次，一次是一头驴，一次是一根稻草。 “Kow”不能与带有“C”的更为平淡的动物混淆。

paragraphs.text: _Parallels._--There is a short reference to the Hedley Kow in Henderson, _l.c._, first edition, pp. 234-5. Our story is shortly referred to thus: "He would present himself to some old dame gathering sticks, in the form of a truss of straw, which she would be sure to take up and carry away. Then it would become so heavy that she would have to lay her burden down, on which the straw would become 'quick,' rise upright and shuffle away before her, till at last it vanished from her sight with a laugh and shout." Some of Robin Goodfellow's pranks are similar to those of the Hedley Kow. The old woman's content with the changes is similar to that of "Mr. Vinegar." An ascending scale of changes has been studied by Prof. Crane, _Italian Popular Tales_, p. 373.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 相似之处——Henderson, l.c. 第一版，第 234-5 页有一个简短的参考文献 Hedley Kow。我们的故事是这样简短提到的：“他会以一捆稻草的形式出现在一些老妇人面前，她一定会拿起并带走它。然后它会变得如此沉重，以至于她不得不放下她的负担，稻草会变得‘快速’，直立起来并在她面前拖着走，直到最后它在大笑和叫喊中从她的视线中消失。”罗宾·古德费罗的一些恶作剧与赫德利·柯的恶作剧相似。老妇人对变化的满足感与“醋先生”类似。 Crane 教授在《意大利流行故事》第 14 页中研究了变化的递增规模。 373.

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE ASS

paragraphs.text: An Ass was feeding in a pasture near a wood when he saw a Wolf lurking in the shadows along the hedge. He easily guessed what the Wolf had in mind, and thought of a plan to save himself. So he pretended he was lame, and began to hobble painfully.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一头驴正在树林附近的牧场里吃草，这时他看到一只狼潜伏在树篱旁的阴影里。他很容易就猜到了狼的心思，并想到了一个自救之计。于是他假装自己是瘸子，开始痛苦地一瘸一拐地走。

paragraphs.text: When the Wolf came up, he asked the Ass what had made him lame, and the Ass replied that he had stepped on a sharp thorn.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狼走上前来，问驴子为什么瘸了，驴子回答说是踩到了一根尖刺。

paragraphs.text: "Please pull it out," he pleaded, groaning as if in pain. "If you do not, it might stick in your throat when you eat me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “请把它拔出来，”他恳求道，仿佛痛苦地呻吟着。 “如果你不这样做，当你吃我的时候，它可能会卡在你的喉咙里。”

paragraphs.text: The Wolf saw the wisdom of the advice, for he wanted to enjoy his meal without any danger of choking. So the Ass lifted up his foot and the Wolf began to search very closely and carefully for the thorn.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狼看到了这个建议的智慧，因为他想享受这顿饭而不会有窒息的危险。于是驴抬起脚，狼开始非常仔细地寻找那根刺。

paragraphs.text: Just then the Ass kicked out with all his might, tumbling the Wolf a dozen paces away. And while the Wolf was getting very slowly and painfully to his feet, the Ass galloped away in safety.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，驴子用力一踢，把狼踢飞了十几步。当狼缓慢而痛苦地站起来时，驴却安全地飞奔而去。

paragraphs.text: "Serves me right," growled the Wolf as he crept into the bushes. "I'm a butcher by trade, not a doctor."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我活该，”狼爬进灌木丛时咆哮道。 “我的职业是屠夫，而不是医生。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 11. THE GOOD NEIGHBOR

paragraphs.text: One day as Jesus, the great Teacher, was speaking, a lawyer, who really wanted to know, said, "Who is my neighbor?" and Jesus told him this beautiful story:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，当伟大的导师耶稣讲话时，一位非常想知道的律师说：“谁是我的邻居？”耶稣告诉他这个美丽的故事：

paragraphs.text: Once a man was journeying over a rough and lonely road. A band of robbers sprang upon him, struck him down, stole his money and clothes and left him bleeding and half dead on the road. A temple-priest happened to pass that way, and when he saw the wounded man lying there he said to himself, "No one can see what I do on this lonely road." So he crossed over to the other side, looked at the beautiful scenery, and passed on to the temple to prayer and sacrifice, leaving the poor man uncared for, dying in the road.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，有一个人走在一条崎岖而孤独的路上。一群强盗向他扑来，将他打倒，抢走了他的钱和衣服，把他留在路上，血流不止，半死不活。一位寺庙和尚恰巧经过那条路，当他看到躺在那里的伤者时，他自言自语道：“没有人能看到我在这条孤独的路上所做的事情。”于是他渡到了对岸，观赏了美丽的风景，然后前往寺庙祈祷和祭祀，留下这个可怜的人无人照顾，死在路上。

paragraphs.text: A temple-singer also passed that way. When he saw the wounded man lying there he went up to him, looked carefully at his sad condition, and said to himself, "Poor man, I would help him if I were not so busy." So he passed on to his singing in the temple, leaving the poor man uncared for, dying in the road.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位寺庙歌手也从那条路经过。当他看到受伤的人躺在那里时，他走到他身边，仔细地看着他悲伤的情况，并对自己说：“可怜的人，如果我不那么忙的话，我会帮助他的。”于是他继续在寺庙里唱歌，留下这个可怜的人无人照顾，死在路上。

paragraphs.text: Shortly afterward a Samaritan, a man who was not a Jew, came along the road. He saw the wounded man bleeding and dying, uncared for in the road, and felt sorry for him. He saw that he was a Jew and not one of his own people, but that made no difference. He went up to him, raised the suffering man, and gently poured soothing oil into his wounds, and gave him strengthening wine. Then he helped him upon his mule and walked by its side, while the man rode until they came to a small hotel, where he spent the night taking good care of him. The next morning he said to the hotel-keeper: "Take care of him until he is well. Here is some money, and if you spend more I will repay you when I come this way again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久之后，一个撒玛利亚人，一个不是犹太人的人，沿着路过来。他看到路上受伤的人流血不止，生命垂危，无人照顾，他为他感到难过。他看出自己是犹太人，而不是自己的同胞，但这没有什么区别。他走到他身边，扶起那个受苦的人，轻轻地将舒缓的油倒在他的伤口上，又给了他提神醒脑的酒。然后，他帮他骑上骡子，并在骡子旁边行走，而那人则骑着马，直到他们来到一家小旅馆，他在那里过夜，精心照顾他。第二天早上，他对旅馆老板说：“照顾他，直到他康复为止。这是一些钱，如果你多花一点，我再来这边时还你。”

paragraphs.text: Jesus said to the lawyer, "Which of these three was a neighbor to the wounded man?" The lawyer said, "The man that showed mercy on him!" Jesus said: "Go, and do you be as good a neighbor to all whom you have the power to aid and to help."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 耶稣对律师说：“这三个人中，哪一个是受伤者的邻居？”律师说：“那个对他仁慈的人！”耶稣说：“去吧，成为所有你有能力帮助和帮助的人的好邻居。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: THE LARK AND HER YOUNG ONES

paragraphs.text: A Lark, who had Young Ones in a field of grain which was almost ripe, was afraid that the reapers would come before her young brood were fledged. So every day when she flew off to look for food, she charged them to take note of what they heard in her absence and to tell her of it when she came home.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只百灵鸟在一片即将成熟的麦田里生下了幼崽，她担心收割者会在她的幼崽羽翼未丰之前到来。因此，每天当她飞去寻找食物时，她都会要求他们记下她不在时听到的情况，并在她回家时告诉她。

paragraphs.text: One day when she was gone, they heard the owner of the field say to his son that the grain seemed ripe enough to be cut, and tell him to go early the next day and ask their friends and neighbors to come and help reap it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，她走后，他们听到田主对儿子说，谷子似乎成熟了，可以收割了，让他第二天早点去，叫朋友邻居来帮忙收割。

paragraphs.text: When the old Lark came home, the Little Ones quivered and chirped round her and told her what had happened, begging her to take them away as fast as she could. The mother bade them be easy; "for," said she, "if he depends on his friends and his neighbors, I am sure the grain will not be reaped tomorrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当老百灵鸟回到家时，小家伙们在她身边颤抖着叽叽喳喳地告诉她发生了什么事，恳求她尽快把它们带走。母亲嘱咐他们要轻松； “因为，”她说，“如果他依赖他的朋友和邻居，我确信明天粮食就收不到了。”

paragraphs.text: Next day she went out again and left the same orders as before. The owner came, and waited. The sun grew hot, but nothing was done, for not a soul came. "You see," said the owner to his son, "these friends of ours are not to be depended upon; so run off at once to your uncles and cousins, and say I wish them to come early to-morrow morning and help us reap."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天，她又出去了，留下的订单和以前一样。主人来了，等待着。太阳变得很热，但什么也没做，因为没有一个人来。 “你看，”主人对他的儿子说，“我们的这些朋友是靠不住的；所以赶紧跑到你的叔叔和表兄弟那里去，告诉他们我希望他们明天一早来帮我们收割。”

paragraphs.text: This the Young Ones, in a great fright, told also to their mother. "Do not fear, children," said she. "Kindred and relations are not always very forward in helping one another; but keep your ears open and let me know what you hear to-morrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 年轻人惊恐万分，也把这件事告诉了他们的母亲。 “不要害怕，孩子们，”她说。 “亲属和亲戚并不总是很主动地互相帮助；但是请竖起耳朵，让我知道你明天听到的消息。”

paragraphs.text: The owner came the next day, and, finding his relations as backward as his neighbors, said to his son, "Now listen to me. Get two good sickles ready for to-morrow morning, for it seems we must reap the grain by ourselves."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天，主人来了，发现他的亲戚和邻居一样落后，就对儿子说：“听我说，准备两把好镰刀，明天早上用，看来我们必须自己收割粮食了。”

paragraphs.text: "Then, my dears," said she, "it is time for us to go; for when a man undertakes to do his work himself, it is not so likely that he will be disappointed." She took away her Young Ones at once, and the grain was reaped the next day by the old man and his son.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “那么，亲爱的，”她说，“我们该走了；因为当一个人开始自己做自己的工作时，他就不太可能会失望。”她立刻带走了自己的孩子，第二天，老人和儿子就收割了谷物。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Stories to Tell to Children: THE LAMBIKIN

paragraphs.text: Once upon a time there was a wee, wee Lambikin, who frolicked about on his little tottery legs, and enjoyed himself amazingly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，有一个小兰比金，他用他那条摇摇晃晃的小腿到处嬉戏，玩得很开心。

paragraphs.text: Now one day he set off to visit his Granny, and was jumping with joy to think of all the good things he should get from her, when whom should he meet but a Jackal, who looked at the tender young morsel and said, "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在有一天，他出发去拜访他的奶奶，一想到他应该从她那里得到所有好东西，他就高兴得跳起来，除了一只豺狼之外，他还能遇到谁，豺狼看着这嫩嫩的小块，说：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: By and by he met a Vulture, and the Vulture, looking hungrily at the tender morsel before him, said, "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久，他遇到了一只秃鹫，秃鹫饥饿地看着面前的嫩肉，说道：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: And by and by he met a Tiger, and then a Wolf and a Dog and an Eagle, and all these, when they saw the tender little morsel, said, "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 渐渐地，他遇到了一只老虎，然后是一只狼、一只狗和一只鹰，所有这些人，当他们看到这嫩肉时，都说：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: At last he reached his Granny's house, and said, all in a great hurry, "Granny, dear, I've promised to get very fat; so, as people ought to keep their promises, please put me into the corn-bin AT ONCE."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后他到了奶奶家，急忙说道：“奶奶，亲爱的，我已经答应要变得很胖了；所以，作为人应该遵守诺言，请立即把我放进玉米仓里。”

paragraphs.text: So his Granny said he was a good boy, and put him into the corn-bin, and there the greedy little Lambikin stayed for seven days, and ate, and ate, and ate, until he could scarcely waddle, and his Granny said he was fat enough for anything, and must go home. But cunning little Lambikin said that would never do, for some animal would be sure to eat him on the way back, he was so plump and tender.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他的奶奶说他是个好孩子，把他放进了玉米仓里，贪婪的小兰比金在那里呆了七天，吃啊吃啊吃，直到他几乎无法摇摇晃晃，他的奶奶说他已经胖得什么都做不了，必须回家了。但狡猾的小兰比金说那是不行的，因为他回来的路上一定会被某种动物吃掉，他又肥又嫩。

paragraphs.text: "I'll tell you what you must do," said Master Lambikin; "you must make a little drumikin out of the skin of my little brother who died, and then I can sit inside and trundle along nicely, for I'm as tight as a drum myself."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我会告诉你必须做什么，”兰比金大师说。 “你必须用我死去的弟弟的皮做一个小鼓，然后我就可以坐在里面很好地滚动，因为我自己像鼓一样紧。”

paragraphs.text: So his Granny made a nice little drumikin out of his brother's skin, with the wool inside, and Lambikin curled himself up snug and warm in the middle and trundled away gayly. Soon he met with the Eagle, who called out,--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是，他的奶奶用他哥哥的皮做了一个漂亮的小鼓，里面是羊毛，兰比金把自己温暖地蜷缩在中间，然后快乐地拖着脚步走开。不久他遇到了老鹰，老鹰大声喊道：

paragraphs.text: At last the Jackal came limping along, for all his sorry looks as sharp as a needle, and he, too, called out,--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，豺狼一瘸一拐地走了过来，尽管他可怜的样子像针一样锋利，他也喊道：

paragraphs.text: But he never got any further, for the Jackal recognized his voice at once, and cried, "Hullo! you've turned yourself inside out, have you? Just you come out of that!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但他再也没有继续下去，因为豺狼立刻认出了他的声音，并喊道：“哈喽！你把自己翻了个底朝天，是吗？你刚刚从那儿出来了！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Aesop for Children: THE MILLER, HIS SON, AND THE ASS

paragraphs.text: One day, a long time ago, an old Miller and his Son were on their way to market with an Ass which they hoped to sell. They drove him very slowly, for they thought they would have a better chance to sell him if they kept him in good condition. As they walked along the highway some travelers laughed loudly at them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 很久以前的一天，一位老磨坊主和他的儿子带着一头驴前往市场，他们希望能卖掉这头驴。他们把他开得很慢，因为他们认为如果他们保持良好的状态，他们就有更好的机会卖掉他。当他们沿着高速公路行走时，一些旅客大声嘲笑他们。

paragraphs.text: "What foolishness," cried one, "to walk when they might as well ride. The most stupid of the three is not the one you would expect it to be."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “真是愚蠢啊，”其中一个喊道，“他们还不如骑车，却选择步行。这三个人中最愚蠢的一个，可不是你想象的那样。”

paragraphs.text: The Miller did not like to be laughed at, so he told his son to climb up and ride.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 磨坊主不喜欢被人嘲笑，就叫儿子爬上去骑。

paragraphs.text: "Oho, what have we here?" they cried. "Respect old age, young man! Get down, and let the old man ride."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦哦，我们这里有什么？”他们哭了。 “年轻人，敬老！快下来，让老人骑。”

paragraphs.text: Though the Miller was not tired, he made the boy get down and climbed up himself to ride, just to please the Merchants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 磨坊主虽然不累，但还是让男孩下来，自己爬上去骑，只是为了讨好商人。

paragraphs.text: At the next turnstile they overtook some women carrying market baskets loaded with vegetables and other things to sell.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在下一个十字转门处，他们追上了一些妇女，她们提着装满蔬菜和其他待售物品的购物篮。

paragraphs.text: "Look at the old fool," exclaimed one of them. "Perched on the Ass, while that poor boy has to walk."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看看这个老傻瓜，”其中一个人喊道。 “坐在屁股上，而那个可怜的男孩却必须走路。”

paragraphs.text: The Miller felt a bit vexed, but to be agreeable he told the Boy to climb up behind him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 磨坊主感到有点烦恼，但为了表示同意，他让男孩爬到他身后。

paragraphs.text: They had no sooner started out again than a loud shout went up from another company of people on the road.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们刚重新出发，路上另一队人就大声喊叫起来。

paragraphs.text: "What a crime," cried one, "to load up a poor dumb beast like that! They look more able to carry the poor creature, than he to carry them."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “真是罪孽啊，”一个人喊道，“把这样一只可怜的、愚蠢的野兽驮在身上！他们看起来比他更能承载这个可怜的动物，而不是他承载它们。”

paragraphs.text: The Miller and his Son quickly scrambled down, and a short time later, the market place was thrown into an uproar as the two came along carrying the Donkey slung from a pole. A great crowd of people ran out to get a closer look at the strange sight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 磨坊主和他的儿子很快就爬了下来，不一会儿，市场上一片哗然，两人抬着一根杆子上的驴子过来了。一大群人跑了出来，想要近距离观察这奇异的景象。

paragraphs.text: The Ass did not dislike being carried, but so many people came up to point at him and laugh and shout, that he began to kick and bray, and then, just as they were crossing a bridge, the ropes that held him gave way, and down he tumbled into the river.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 驴子并不讨厌被人抬着，但这么多人过来指着他又笑又叫，他开始又踢又叫，然后，正当他们过桥时，绑住他的绳子松开了，他跌进了河里。

paragraphs.text: The poor Miller now set out sadly for home. By trying to please everybody, he had pleased nobody, and lost his Ass besides.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可怜的米勒现在悲伤地踏上回家之路。他试图取悦所有人，却没有取悦任何人，而且还失去了他的屁股。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 6. THE INDIANS' GUNPOWDER HARVEST

paragraphs.text: At first the Indians were very kind to the white men; but after the white men began to be cruel and hard to them, they too grew hard and cruel, and nothing was too terrible for the Indians to do in revenge. They had very strange ways of carrying on their battles. They never came out and met their enemy face to face, but would skulk around behind trees in swamps or in the high grass. When the white men used guns and gunpowder, the Indians were terribly frightened, but it was not long before they themselves learned to use them. One day an old Indian chief begged some gunpowder from a white man, and ran away to his wigwam with it. The white man watched to see what he would do with it. When he reached his wigwam he called some of his friends about him, and, after a long council together, they began to plant the powder. They thought it would grow like corn and beans. Later a French trader persuaded some Indians living near the Missouri River to give him skins and furs in exchange for gunpowder, telling them it was a seed, which would grow if sown in the ground. The innocent Indians sowed all they bought, and placed a guard to protect the fields from wild beasts, going out to the field from time to time to see if the powder was growing. When they found out the trick that had been played on them they waited until the trader's partner came to exchange more goods. Then the Indians who had been tricked into sowing gunpowder gathered, went into his tent, and each helped himself to what goods he wanted. Soon the whole stock disappeared. The Frenchman, in anger, went to the chief, who said, "Yes, you shall have justice as soon as the gunpowder harvest is gathered." The Frenchman said, "Gunpowder grows in France, but your Missouri land is not good to produce it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 起初，印第安人对白人非常友善。但当白人开始对他们残忍、严厉之后，他们也变得冷酷、残忍，对于印第安人来说，没有什么可怕的事情是不能报复的。他们以非常奇怪的方式进行战斗。他们从不出来与敌人面对面交锋，而是躲在沼泽地的树后或高高的草丛中。当白人使用枪支和火药时，印第安人被吓坏了，但没过多久，他们自己也学会了使用它们。有一天，一位年长的印第安酋长向一位白人索要一些火药，然后带着火药跑到了他的棚屋。白人看着他会用它做什么。当他到达自己的棚屋时，他叫来了一些朋友，经过长时间的商议后，他们开始种植粉末。他们认为它会像玉米和豆子一样生长。后来，一位法国商人说服了一些居住在密苏里河附近的印第安人，用皮毛换取火药，并告诉他们这是一颗种子，种在地里就会生长。无辜的印第安人把买来的都播种了，还派了守卫保护田地免遭野兽的侵害，时不时地到田里看看粉末是否长起来。当他们发现有人对他们玩的把戏时，他们等到商人的伙伴来交换更多的货物。然后那些被骗去撒火药的印第安人就聚集起来，走进他的帐篷，各取所需的物品。很快，所有库存都消失了。法国人愤怒地去找酋长，酋长说：“是的，火药收成后，你就会得到正义。”法国人说：“法国盛产火药，但你们密苏里州的土地不适合生产火药。”

paragraphs.text: All his arguments were in vain. The Indians said, "When the gunpowder harvest is reaped then the Frenchman shall have back his goods."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他所有的争论都是徒劳的。印第安人说：“当火药收成时，法国人就会拿回他的货物。”

paragraphs.text: So the French trader returned with less goods and less money than he went, finding out, when too late, that Indians, like some other men, can be deceived but once.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 因此，这位法国商人带着比去时更少的货物和更少的钱回来了，但为时已晚，他发现印度人和其他一些人一样，只能被欺骗一次。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fourth Reader: The Alexandra Readers: THE BATTLE OF BLENHEIM

paragraphs.text: It was a summer evening, Old Kaspar's work was done, And he before his cottage door Was sitting in the sun, And by him sported on the green His little grandchild Wilhelmine.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那是一个夏日的傍晚，老卡斯帕的工作完成了，他坐在小屋门前晒太阳，旁边的草地上有他的小孙子威廉敏娜。

paragraphs.text: She saw her brother Peterkin Roll something large and round, Which he beside the rivulet In playing there had found; He came to ask what he had found, That was so large, and smooth, and round.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她看到她哥哥彼得金滚了一个又大又圆的东西，那是他在小溪边玩耍时发现的；他过来问他发现了什么，那东西又大又光滑又圆。

paragraphs.text: Old Kaspar took it from the boy, Who stood expectant by; And then the old man shook his head, And with a natural sigh: "'Tis some poor fellow's skull," said he, "Who fell in the great victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老卡斯帕从男孩手中接过它，男孩站在一旁期待着；然后老人摇了摇头，自然地叹了口气：“这是某个可怜人的头骨，”他说，“他在伟大的胜利中倒下了。

paragraphs.text: "I find them in the garden, For there's many here about; And often when I go to plough, The ploughshare turns them out! For many thousand men," said he, "Were slain in that great victory."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我在花园里找到了他们，因为这里有很多人；常常当我去犁地时，犁铧把他们犁出来了！有成千上万的人，”他说，“在那场伟大的胜利中被杀了。”

paragraphs.text: "Now tell us what 'twas all about," Young Peterkin, he cries; And little Wilhelmine looks up, With wonder-waiting eyes; "Now tell us all about the war, And what they fought each other for."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在告诉我们这到底是怎么回事，”年轻的彼得金喊道。小威廉敏娜抬起头来，用惊奇等待的眼睛； “现在告诉我们有关战争的一切，以及他们为何而战。”

paragraphs.text: "It was the English," Kaspar cried, "Who put the French to rout; But what they fought each other for, I could not well make out; But everybody said," quoth he, "That 'twas a famous victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是英国人，”卡斯帕喊道，“他们击溃了法国人；但是他们互相争斗的目的，我不太清楚；但每个人都说，”他说，“那是一场著名的胜利。

paragraphs.text: "My father lived at Blenheim then, Yon little stream hard by; They burnt his dwelling to the ground, And he was forced to fly; So with his wife and child he fled, Nor had he where to rest his head.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当时我父亲住在布伦海姆，就在小溪边；他们把他的住所烧毁了，他被迫逃亡；于是他带着妻子和孩子逃走了，也没有地方休息。

paragraphs.text: "With fire and sword the country round Was wasted far and wide, And many a nursing mother then And new-born baby died; But things like that, you know, must be At every famous victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “在火与剑的影响下，整个国家被蹂躏得很远，许多哺乳期的母亲和新生的婴儿都死了；但是，你知道，这样的事情必然发生在每一次著名的胜利中。

paragraphs.text: "They say it was a shocking sight After the field was won; For many thousand bodies here Lay rotting in the sun; But things like that, you know, must be After a famous victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “他们说，在赢得这场比赛之后，这是令人震惊的景象；这里有成千上万的尸体在阳光下腐烂；但像这样的事情，你知道，一定是在一场著名的胜利之后。

paragraphs.text: "Great praise the Duke of Marlbro' won, And our good Prince Eugene." "Why, 'twas a very wicked thing!" Said little Wilhelmine. "Nay--nay--my little girl," quoth he, "It was a famous victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “马尔布罗公爵和我们善良的尤金王子赢得了极大的赞誉。” “为什么，这是一件非常邪恶的事情！”小威廉敏娜说道。 “不——不——我的小女儿，”他说道，“这是一次著名的胜利。

paragraphs.text: "And everybody praised the Duke, Who this great fight did win." "But what good came of it at last?" Quoth little Peterkin. "Why, that I cannot tell," said he, "But 'twas a famous victory." --ROBERT SOUTHEY.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “每个人都称赞公爵，他赢得了这场伟大的战斗。” “但是最后有什么好处呢？”小彼得金说。 “为什么，我不能说，”他说，“但这是一场著名的胜利。” ——罗伯特·索西。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Yellow Fairy Book: By Flora Annie Steel

paragraphs.text: Once upon a time there was a wee wee Lambikin, who frolicked about on his little tottery legs, and enjoyed himself amazingly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，有一个小兰比金，他用他那条摇摇晃晃的小腿到处嬉戏，玩得很开心。

paragraphs.text: Now one day he set off to visit his Granny, and was jumping with joy to think of all the good things he should get from her, when who should he meet but a Jackal, who looked at the tender young morsel and said: "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在有一天，他出发去拜访他的奶奶，一想到他应该从她那里得到所有好东西，他就高兴得跳了起来，当他遇到的除了一只豺狼之外，还有谁，豺狼看着这嫩嫩的小块，说：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: By and by he met a Vulture, and the Vulture, looking hungrily at the tender morsel before him, said: "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久，他遇到了一只秃鹫，秃鹰饥饿地看着面前的嫩肉，说道：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: And by and by he met a Tiger, and then a Wolf, and a Dog, arid an Eagle, and all these, when they saw the tender little morsel, said: "Lambikin! Lambikin! I'll EAT YOU!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 渐渐地，他遇到了一只老虎，然后是一只狼，一只狗，还有一只鹰，所有这些人，当他们看到这嫩嫩的小东西时，都说：“兰比金！兰比金！我要吃掉你！”

paragraphs.text: At last he reached his Granny's house, and said, all in a great hurry, "Granny, dear, I've promised to get very fat; so, as people ought to keep their promises, please put me into the corn bin at once."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后他到了奶奶家，急急忙忙地说：“奶奶，亲爱的，我已经发誓要变得很胖了，所以，作为人应该遵守诺言，请立即把我放进玉米仓里。”

paragraphs.text: So his Granny said he was a good boy, and put him into the corn bin, and there the greedy little Lambikin stayed for seven days, and ate, and ate, and ate, until he could scarcely waddle, and his Granny said he was fat enough for anything, and must go home. But cunning little Lambikin said that would never do, for some animal would be sure to eat him on the way back, he was so plump and tender.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他的奶奶说他是个好孩子，把他放进了玉米仓里，贪婪的小兰比金在那里呆了七天，吃啊吃啊吃，直到他几乎无法摇摇晃晃，他的奶奶说他胖得什么都做不了，必须回家。但狡猾的小兰比金说那是不行的，因为他回来的路上一定会被某种动物吃掉，他又肥又嫩。

paragraphs.text: "I'll tell you what you must do," said Master Lambikin, "you must make a little drumikin out of the skin of my little brother who died, and then I can sit inside and trundle along nicely, for I'm as tight as a drum myself."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我会告诉你你必须做什么，”兰比金大师说，“你必须用我死去的弟弟的皮制作一个小鼓，然后我就可以坐在里面很好地滚动，因为我自己像鼓一样紧。”

paragraphs.text: So his Granny made a nice little drumikin out of his brother's skin, with the wool inside, and Lambikin curled himself up snug and warm in the middle, and trundled away gayly. Soon lie met with the Eagle, who called out:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是，他的奶奶用他哥哥的皮做了一个漂亮的小鼓，里面是羊毛，兰比金把自己温暖地蜷缩在中间，然后快乐地拖着脚步走开。不久，他遇到了老鹰，老鹰喊道：

paragraphs.text: At last the Jackal came limping along, for all his sorry looks as sharp as a needle, and he too called out—
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，豺狼一瘸一拐地走了过来，尽管他那可怜兮兮的表情像针一样锋利，他也喊道——

paragraphs.text: But he never got any further, for the Jackal recognized his voice at once, arid cried: "Hullo! you've turned yourself inside out, have you? Just you come out of that!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但他再也没有继续下去，因为豺狼立刻认出了他的声音，大声喊道：“哈啰！你把自己翻了个底朝天，是吗？你刚刚从那儿出来了！”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 10. WHY BOYS TAKE OFF THEIR HATS IN CHURCH

paragraphs.text: When the boy Moses was old enough to leave his mother he went to live with his new mother in the king's palace.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当男孩摩西长大到可以离开他的母亲时，他和他的新母亲一起住在国王的宫殿里。

paragraphs.text: Moses was a good boy. He studied his lessons so well in school that he grew up to be one of the wisest and best young men in all the land. But Moses never forgot his own Hebrew people. He was not careless of the cruel way they were treated as slaves by the king's officers. He tried to improve their sad condition in his own hasty way, but he soon saw that neither his own people nor their masters wanted a princess's son to interfere. They were both ready to kill him for trying to help. So Moses had to flee for his life into the mountains where he became a shepherd. One day as he was leading his sheep up the mountainside, he saw a thorn-bush all aflame; and it kept on burning, but was not burned up. Moses wondered to see so strange a sight. Leaving his sheep he went near. Suddenly a Voice called out of the midst of the fire-bush, "Moses! Moses!" Moses answered, "Here am I." The Voice said, "Take off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground," It was God, in the form of an angel, speaking to him. Moses at once took off his shoes and bowed reverently in the presence of God. Then God told him a better way by which he could help his downtrodden people and set them free from their cruel masters who were beating them and making their life so hard. He told Moses he wanted him to lead his people out of their bondage. At first Moses was afraid he was not able to do what God wanted him to do, but God said, "Certainly, Moses, I will be with thee." Moses obeyed the Voice that spoke that day to him out of the fire-bush, and he became one of the greatest of leaders and lawgivers that this world ever saw. Men and boys take off their hats in church to-day for the same reason that Moses removed his shoes before the fire-bush--to show reverence in the presence of God and respect for his wonderful way of speaking to men.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 摩西是个好孩子。他在学校学得很好，以至于长大后成为全国最聪明、最优秀的年轻人之一。但摩西从未忘记他自己的希伯来人。他对国王官员残酷地对待他们如奴隶的方式并没有漠视。他试图以自己仓促的方式改善他们的悲惨处境，但他很快就发现，无论是他自己的人民还是他们的主人都不希望公主的儿子干涉。他们都准备杀了他，因为他试图提供帮助。因此，摩西不得不逃到山里，在那里当了牧羊人。有一天，他牵着羊群上山时，看见一丛荆棘着火了。它继续燃烧，但没有被烧毁。摩西很惊讶看到如此奇怪的景象。他离开他的羊走近了。突然，火丛中传来一个声音：“摩西！摩西！”摩西回答说：“我在这里。”声音说：“把你脚上的鞋子脱下来，因为你所站的地方是圣地。”这是上帝以天使的形式对他说话。摩西立刻脱掉鞋子，在上帝面前恭敬地下拜。然后上帝告诉他一个更好的方法，他可以帮助他的受压迫的人民，并把他们从残酷的主人手中解放出来，这些主人正在殴打他们，使他们的生活变得如此艰难。他告诉摩西他希望他带领他的人民摆脱束缚。起初摩西担心自己不能做神要他做的事，但神说：“当然，摩西，我会与你同在。”摩西听从了那天从火丛中对他说话的声音，他成为了这个世界上最伟大的领袖和立法者之一。今天，男人和男孩在教堂里脱下帽子，其原因与摩西在火丛前脱鞋的原因相同，即在上帝面前表示敬畏，并尊重他与人类交谈的奇妙方式。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's First Eclectic Reader: Chip'py food stone be fore' chick'ens kept

paragraphs.text: There was once a big, white hen that had twelve little chickens. They were very small, and the old hen took good care of them. She found food for them in the daytime, and at night kept them under her wings.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一只又大又白的母鸡，它养了十二只小鸡。它们很小，老母鸡把它们照顾得很好。她白天为它们寻找食物，晚上则将它们保护在自己的羽翼之下。

paragraphs.text: One day, this old hen took her chickens down to a small brook. She thought the air from the water would do them good.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，这只老母鸡带着她的小鸡来到一条小溪边。她认为水中的空气对他们有好处。

paragraphs.text: When they got to the brook, they walked on the bank a little while. It was very pretty on the other side of the brook, and the old hen thought she would take her children over there.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 到了小溪边，他们在岸边走了一会儿。小溪的另一边非常漂亮，老母鸡想带她的孩子们去那里。

paragraphs.text: There was a large stone in the brook: she thought it would be easy for them to jump to that stone, and from it to the other side.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小溪里有一块大石头：她认为他们很容易跳到那块石头上，然后从石头跳到对岸。

paragraphs.text: So she jumped to the stone, and told the children to come after her. For the first time, she found that they would not obey her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她跳到石头上，让孩子们跟上她。她第一次发现他们不听她的话。

paragraphs.text: She flapped her wings, and cried, "Come here, all of you! Jump upon this stone, as I did. We can then jump to the other side. Come now!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她拍打着翅膀，喊道：“你们都过来！像我一样，跳到这块石头上。然后我们就可以跳到另一边了。快来吧！”

paragraphs.text: "Yes you can, if you try," said the old hen. "Just flap your wings, as I did, and you can jump over."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是的，如果你尝试的话，你可以的。”老母鸡说。 “只要像我一样拍动翅膀，就可以跳过去。”

paragraphs.text: "I am flapping my wings," said Chippy, who stood by himself; "but I can't jump any better than I could before."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我正在拍打翅膀，”奇皮独自站在一旁说道。 “但我不能比以前跳得更好了。”

paragraphs.text: "Well," said the old hen, "I must give it up." So she jumped back to the bank, and walked slowly home with her brood.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好吧，”老母鸡说，“我必须放弃了。”于是她跳回岸边，带着孩子慢慢走回家。

paragraphs.text: When they got home, the old hen began to look about for something to eat. She soon found, near the back door, a piece of bread.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他们回到家时，老母鸡开始寻找吃的东西。她很快在后门附近发现了一块面包。

paragraphs.text: So she called the chickens, and they all ran up to her, each one trying to get a bite at the piece of bread.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她叫来了鸡，它们都跑到她身边，每只都想咬那块面包。

paragraphs.text: "No, no!" said the old hen. "This bread is for Chippy. He is the only one of my children that really tried to jump to the stone."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不，不！”老母鸡说。 “这个面包是给奇皮的。他是我的孩子中唯一一个真正尝试过跳到石头上的人。”

paragraphs.text: Your parents are very kind to send you to school. If you are good, and if you try to learn, your teacher will love you, and you will please your parents.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你的父母非常友善地送你去学校。如果你表现好，如果你努力学习，你的老师就会爱你，你也会让你的父母高兴。

paragraphs.text: Be kind to all, and do not waste your time in school. When you go home, you may ask your parents to get you a Second Reader.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 善待所有人，不要在学校浪费时间。当你回家时，你可以要求你的父母给你找一本第二读者。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Beacon Second Reader: THE THREE BILLY-GOATS GRUFF

paragraphs.text: Once on a time there were three Billy-goats who were to go up to the hillside to make themselves fat, and the name of all the three was "Gruff."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，有三只比利山羊，它们要去山坡上养肥，三只山羊的名字都叫“粗鲁”。

paragraphs.text: On the way up was a bridge over a burn they had to cross; and under the bridge lived a great ugly Troll, with eyes as big as saucers and a nose as long as a poker.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 上去的路上有一座桥，架在烧伤处，他们必须穿过。桥下住着一个又大又丑的巨魔，眼睛像碟子那么大，鼻子像拨火棍那么长。

paragraphs.text: "Oh! it is only I, the tiniest billy-goat Gruff; and I'm going up to the hill-side to make myself fat," said the billy-goat, with such a small voice.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦！只有我，最小的公山羊格拉夫；我要去山坡上把自己变胖。”公山羊用很小的声音说道。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, no! pray don't take me. I'm too little, that I am," said the billy-goat. "Wait a bit till the second billy-goat Gruff comes; he's much bigger."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦，不！请不要带走我。我太小了，我就是这样，”公山羊说。 “等一下，第二只公山羊格鲁夫来了；他要大得多。”

paragraphs.text: "Oh! it's the second billy-goat Gruff, and I'm going up to the hill-side to make myself fat," said the billy-goat, who hadn't such a small voice.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦！这是第二只公山羊，嘎嘎，我要去山坡上把自己胖起来。”公山羊声音不小，说道。

paragraphs.text: "TRIP, TRAP! TRIP, TRAP! TRIP, TRAP!" went the bridge, for the billy-goat was so heavy that the bridge creaked and groaned under him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “绊倒，陷阱！绊倒，陷阱！绊倒，陷阱！”走上桥，因为公山羊太重了，桥在他身下吱吱作响，呻吟着。

paragraphs.text: "It's I! THE BIG BILLY-GOAT GRUFF," said the billy-goat, who had an ugly hoarse voice of his own.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是我！粗暴的大公山羊。”公山羊说，他的声音也丑陋而沙哑。

paragraphs.text: "Well, come along! I've got two spears, And I'll poke your eyeballs out at your ears; I've got besides two curling-stones, And I'll crush you to bits, body and bones."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好吧，来吧！我有两根长矛，我会把你的眼珠子从你的耳朵里戳出来；我还有两块冰壶，我会把你压得粉身碎骨。”

paragraphs.text: That was what the big billy-goat said; and so he flew at the Troll and poked his eyes out with his horns, and crushed him to bits, body and bones, and tossed him out into the burn, and after that he went up to the hill-side. There the billy-goats got so fat they were scarce able to walk home again; and if the fat hasn't fallen off them, why they're still fat; and so,--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那是大公山羊说的；于是他飞向巨魔，用角戳出他的眼睛，将他碾成碎片，连尸带骨，扔进火里，然后他就上了山坡。在那里，公山羊变得太胖了，几乎无法再走回家了。如果脂肪没有从他们身上掉下来，为什么他们还是胖？所以， -

paragraphs.text: The following droll seems to indicate that the folk had a strain of satirical humor which they could use with fine effect. The translation is that of Dasent's _Popular Tales from the Norse_. (An old English verse form of the same story will be found in No. 146.) The old proverb about the shoemaker sticking to his last is sure to come to mind as one reads, but it seems to lose force when we notice that the "goody" has no trouble with the mowing, while the good "man" has much with the housework!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 下面的滑稽动作似乎表明民间有一种讽刺幽默的风格，他们可以利用这种幽默来达到很好的效果。译本是达森特的《挪威流行故事》的译本。 （同一个故事的一首古老的英语诗歌形式可以在第 146 篇中找到。） 阅读时，人们肯定会想到关于鞋匠坚持楦楦的古老谚语，但当我们注意到“好人”在割草上没有麻烦，而好“人”则有很多家务时，它似乎就失去了力量！

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 17. THE LITTLE BOY KING

paragraphs.text: There were troublous times in a king's palace when a little prince was born. He was only two months old when his father, the king, was killed in battle and this little baby boy had to be hidden away by his aunt in a storeroom in the sacred temple to save his life. For seven years he was hidden there and very few knew that the little boy, who should be the king, was alive. His grandmother, a very wicked and cruel woman, Athaliah, became queen. She first ordered all the royal children she could find to be put to death and then she did many such cruel and evil things so that her people became worse and worse. After seven long years, one day a good man in the temple told five brave captains his secret, and showed them the young king and asked their help to crown him king in the place of the wicked, cruel grandmother. They promised. Soon many other soldiers came to know the secret, and on a day they decided upon, these men armed themselves with swords and spears and shields, and gathered in the temple to crown the little boy king. His granduncle, the high priest, brought him out from his hiding-place, set him upon a high platform, put a little crown of gold upon his head, while all the men clapped their hands and cried, "Long live the king!" When the queen-grandmother heard the shouts she came to the temple and looked in, and behold, there was the little boy king standing on the platform with the crown upon his head and all the captains and guards and trumpeters and people rejoicing and blowing trumpets. The queen tore her clothes in anger and cried out, "Treason! Treason!" But all her soldiers and people were sick and tired of her cruel reign. So the captains seized her, led her out of the temple, and slew her near the horse-gate of her palace. So this little boy only seven years old was crowned king because he was of the family of King David and because God took care of him in his hiding-place during all those seven long years. He reigned as King of Judah many, many years, doing great good for God and for his people. His name was Joash.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当一位小王子出生时，王宫里正经历着一段动荡的时期。当他的父亲国王在战斗中阵亡时，他才两个月大，这个小男孩不得不被他的姨妈藏在神圣寺庙的储藏室里以挽救他的生命。他被藏在那里七年了，很少有人知道这个本应成为国王的小男孩还活着。他的祖母亚他利雅是一个非常邪恶和残忍的女人，她成为了王后。她先是下令将所有能找到的王室子弟都处死，然后又做了许多这样残忍邪恶的事情，让她的子民变得越来越坏。漫长的七年后，有一天，寺庙里的一位好心人告诉了五位勇敢的队长他的秘密，并向他们展示了年轻的国王，并请求他们帮助他加冕为王，代替邪恶、残忍的祖母。他们答应了。很快，许多其他士兵也知道了这个秘密，在他们决定的某一天，这些人拿起剑、矛和盾牌，聚集在寺庙里，为小男孩加冕。他的叔叔，大祭司，把他从藏身之处带了出来，把他放在一个高台上，把一顶小金冠戴在他的头上，所有的人都拍手喊道：“国王万岁！”太后听到呼喊声，来到庙里一看，只见小国王头戴王冠，站在平台上，众将领、侍卫、吹号手和百姓都欢呼雀跃，吹着号角。皇后愤怒地撕破衣服，大喊：“叛国！叛国！”但她所有的士兵和人民都厌倦了她的残酷统治。于是，众将领抓住了她，将她带出了圣殿，并在她宫殿的马门附近将她杀了。所以这个年仅七岁的小男孩就被加冕为王，因为他是大卫王的家族，也因为在这漫长的七年里，上帝在他的藏身之处照顾了他。他作为犹大王统治了很多很多年，为上帝和他的子民做了很多好事。他的名字叫乔阿施。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 3. THE FIRST TWO BROTHERS

paragraphs.text: The first two brothers in the world were Cain and Abel. They were born after their parents were driven out of their beautiful garden-home in Eden. When these boys grew up, Cain, the elder, became a farmer, and Abel became a shepherd. Their parents brought them up always to ask God to forgive them when they did wrong, and to bring offerings to him of what they had. One day when they came with their gifts, Abel, with a loving heart, carried a lamb, the best of his flock, but Cain brought some fruit in a careless way. God was well pleased with Abel's gift, because of the love that came with it; but not with Cain's, because Cain kept hatred to his brother in his heart. Cain was angry and his face became dark and scowling. God said: "Why are you angry and scowling? If you do well, will you not be happy? If you do not well, hatred in your heart will crouch, like a lion, ready to spring at you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 世界上最早的两兄弟是该隐和亚伯。他们是在父母被赶出伊甸园美丽的花园住宅后出生的。当这些男孩长大后，大该隐成为了农民，亚伯成为了牧羊人。他们的父母抚养他们长大，当他们做错事时，总是祈求上帝宽恕他们，并用他们所有的东西向上帝献祭。有一天，当他们带着礼物来的时候，亚伯怀着一颗爱心，带着一只羊羔，那是他羊群中最好的，而该隐却漫不经心地带来了一些水果。上帝对亚伯的礼物非常满意，因为它伴随着爱。但该隐的却不然，因为该隐心里对他的兄弟怀有仇恨。该隐很生气，他的脸色变得阴沉且皱起眉头。上帝说：“你为什么生气、皱眉？如果你做得好，你会不高兴吗？如果你做得不好，你心中的仇恨就会蹲下，像一头狮子，准备向你扑来。”

paragraphs.text: But Cain paid no attention to God's loving word. One day he said to Abel, "Come into the field with me." When they were there alone, the crouching lion of hatred in Cain's heart sprang up, and Cain lifted up his hand and slew his brother. Then Cain heard God's voice saying, "Where is thy brother?" He answered untruthfully, "I know not; am I my brother's keeper?" Then as Cain had done this wicked deed, God sent him from his home and parents to become a wanderer on the earth, working even harder than his father and his mother did. Cain's suffering was just what he had brought upon himself, yet he said, "My punishment is greater than I can bear." He was afraid wherever he went men would seek to kill him, for he knew he deserved to be killed. But God gave him a mark by which he could know that God was still watching over him and would not let any one kill him. So Cain went away and built a city and lived unhappily the rest of his life, away from his father and mother, because he had allowed hatred instead of love to live in his heart, and because he had not tried to please his loving Father in heaven.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但该隐没有留意神慈爱的话语。有一天，他对亚伯说：“跟我一起到田里去吧。”当他们单独在那里时，该隐心中仇恨的蹲狮猛然窜起，该隐举起手来，杀了他的兄弟。然后该隐听到上帝的声音说：“你的兄弟在哪里？”他谎称：“我不知道，我是看守我兄弟的吗？”当该隐做了这件恶事时，上帝就派他离开他的家和父母，到地上漂泊，比他的父母更辛苦地工作。该隐的痛苦正是他自己造成的，但他却说：“我的刑罚太重了，过于我所能承受的。”他担心无论他走到哪里，人们都会想杀他，因为他知道他活该被杀。但神给了他一个记号，让他知道神还在看顾着他，不会让任何人杀他。于是该隐离开，建造了一座城市，离开了父母，过着不幸的余生，因为他心中充满了仇恨而不是爱，也因为他没有试图取悦他慈爱的天父。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: THE BIRDS AND I

paragraphs.text: The springtime belongs to the birds and me. We own it. We know when the mayflowers and the buttercups bloom. We know when the first frogs peep. We watch the awakening of the woods. We are wet by the warm April showers. We go where we will, and we are companions. Every tree and brook and blade of grass is ours; and our hearts are full of song.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 春天属于鸟儿和我。我们拥有它。我们知道五月花和毛茛花何时开花。我们知道第一只青蛙何时窥视。我们看着树林的苏醒。我们被四月温暖的阵雨弄湿了。我们想去哪儿就去哪儿，我们是同伴。每一棵树、每一条小溪、每一片草叶都是我们的；我们的心中充满了歌声。

paragraphs.text: There are boys who kill the birds, and girls who want to catch them and put them into cages, and there are others who steal their eggs. The birds are not partners with them; they are only servants. Birds, like people, sing for their friends, not for their masters. I am sure that one cannot think much of the springtime and the flowers if his heart is always set upon killing or catching something. We are happy when we are free, and so are the birds.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有些男孩杀鸟，有些女孩想抓住它们并把它们关进笼子里，还有一些人偷鸟蛋。鸟儿不是它们的伙伴；它们是它们的伙伴。他们只是仆人。鸟儿和人一样，为朋友歌唱，而不是为主人歌唱。我相信，如果一个人总是一心想着杀戮或捕捉什么东西，他就不会重视春天和花朵。当我们自由的时候我们很快乐，鸟儿也是如此。

paragraphs.text: The birds and I get acquainted all over again every spring. They have seen strange lands in the winter, and all the brooks and woods have been covered with snow. So we run and romp together, and find all the nooks and crannies which we had half-forgotten since October. The birds remember the old places. The wrens pull the sticks from the old hollow rail and seem to be wild with joy to see the place again. They must be the same wrens that were here last year, for strangers could not make so much fuss over an old rail. The bluebirds and wrens look into every crack and corner for a place in which to build, and the robins and chirping-sparrows explore every tree in the old orchard.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每年春天，我和鸟儿都会重新熟悉起来。他们在冬天看到了陌生的土地，所有的小溪和树林都被雪覆盖了。于是我们一起跑来跑去，找到了自十月以来我们几乎忘记的所有角落和缝隙。鸟儿记得故地。鹪鹩们从旧的空心铁轨上拔下木棍，似乎因再次见到这个地方而欣喜若狂。它们一定是去年这里的鹪鹩，因为陌生人不可能对一根旧铁轨如此大惊小怪。蓝知更鸟和鹪鹩在每一个裂缝和角落寻找可以建造的地方，知更鸟和鸣雀探索老果园中的每一棵树。

paragraphs.text: If the birds want to live with us, we should encourage them. The first thing to do is to leave them alone. Let them be as free from danger and fear as you and I. Take the hammer off the old gun, give pussy so much to eat that she will not care to hunt for birds, and keep away the boys who steal eggs and who carry sling-shots and throw stones. Plant trees and bushes about the borders of the place, and let some of them, at least, grow into tangles; then, even in the back yard, the wary catbird may make its home.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果鸟儿想和我们一起生活，我们应该鼓励它们。首先要做的就是让他们独自一人。让他们像你我一样远离危险和恐惧。把旧枪上的锤子拿下来，给小猫吃很多东西，让她不再关心捕鸟，并远离那些偷鸡蛋、拿着弹弓和扔石头的男孩。在该地区的边界周围种植树木和灌木丛，至少让其中一些生长成缠结；然后，即使在后院，警惕的猫鸟也可能安家。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Fourth Eclectic Reader: XIII. PICCOLA. (48)

paragraphs.text: By Celia Laighton Thaxter, who was born at Portsmouth, N. H., June 29, 1836. Much of her childhood was passed at White Island, one of the Isles of Shoals, off the coast of New Hampshire. "Among the Isles of Shoals," is her most noted work in prose. She published a volume of poems, many of which are favorites with children. She died in 1894.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 作者：西莉亚·莱顿·撒克斯特 (Celia Laighton Thaxter)，1836 年 6 月 29 日出生于新罕布什尔州朴茨茅斯。她的童年大部分时间是在新罕布什尔州海岸外的浅滩群岛之一怀特岛度过的。 《浅滩群岛》是她最著名的散文作品。她出版了一本诗集，其中许多诗深受孩子们的喜爱。她于 1894 年去世。

paragraphs.text: 1. Poor, sweet Piccola! Did you hear What happened to Piccola, children dear? 'T is seldom Fortune such favor grants As fell to this little maid of France.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1.可怜的、可爱的短笛！亲爱的孩子们，你们听说皮科拉出了什么事吗？命运很少如此眷顾这位法国小姑娘。

paragraphs.text: 2. 'T was Christmas time, and her parents poor Could hardly drive the wolf from the door, Striving with poverty's patient pain Only to live till summer again.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 2. 圣诞节到了，她的父母穷困潦倒，几乎无法将狼赶出家门，在贫困的痛苦中挣扎，只为再次活到夏天。

paragraphs.text: 3. No gift for Piccola! sad were they When dawned the morning of Christmas day! Their little darling no joy might stir; St. Nicholas nothing would bring to her!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 3. Piccola没有礼物！当圣诞节早晨黎明时，他们感到悲伤！他们的小宝贝不会有任何欢乐；圣尼古拉斯什么也不能带给她！

paragraphs.text: 4. But Piccola never doubted at all That something beautiful must befall Every child upon Christmas day, And so she slept till the dawn was gray.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 4. 但皮科拉从不怀疑，圣诞节那天，美丽的事物一定会降临到每个孩子身上，所以她一直睡到天色灰蒙蒙的。

paragraphs.text: 5. And full of faith, when at last she woke, She stole to her shoe as the morning broke; Such sounds of gladness filled all the air, 'T was plain St. Nicholas had been there.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 5. 当她终于醒来时，她满怀信心，黎明破晓时，她偷偷地走到鞋子旁；空气中充满了欢乐的声音，显然圣尼古拉斯也在那里。

paragraphs.text: 6. In rushed Piccola, sweet, half wild-- Never was seen such a joyful child-- "See what the good saint brought!" she cried, And mother and father must peep inside.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 6. 在匆忙的短笛中，甜美，半狂野——从未见过如此快乐的孩子——“看看善良的圣人带来了什么！”她喊道，爸爸妈妈必须往里面看。

paragraphs.text: 7. Now such a story I never heard! There was a little shivering bird! A sparrow, that in at the window flew, Had crept into Piccola's tiny shoe!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 7.现在这样的故事我从来没有听说过！有一只小鸟在瑟瑟发抖！窗外飞来一只麻雀， 爬进了皮科拉的小鞋子里！

paragraphs.text: 8. "How good poor Piccola must have been!" She cried, as happy as any queen, While the starving sparrow she fed and warmed, And danced with rapture, she was so charmed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 8.“可怜的皮科拉一定是多么优秀啊！”她像任何女王一样高兴地哭泣，当她喂养和温暖饥饿的麻雀时，她欣喜若狂地跳舞，她是如此着迷。

paragraphs.text: 9. Children, this story I tell to you Of Piccola sweet and her bird, is true. In the far-off land of France, they say, Still do they live to this very day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 9. 孩子们，我给你们讲的这个关于皮科拉·斯威特和她的小鸟的故事是真实的。他们说，在遥远的法国土地上，他们仍然活到今天。

paragraphs.text: DEFINITIONS.--3. Dawned, began to grow light. Stir, excite. 4. Be-fall, happen. 7. Shiv'er-ing, trembling from cold. Ti'ny, very small. 8. Rapture, great joy. Charmed, greatly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 定义--3。天亮了，天开始亮了。搅拌、激发。 4. 发生，发生。 7. 冷得瑟瑟发抖。很小，很小。 8. 狂喜，极大的喜乐。被迷住了，非常。

paragraphs.text: EXERCISES.--What is meant by "driving the wolf from the door"? In the third stanza, what does "St." before Nicholas mean? Who is St. Nicholas? What did Piccola find in her shoe on Christmas morning?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 练习——“把狼赶出家门”是什么意思？在第三节中，“St.”是什么意思？尼古拉斯之前的意思是？圣尼古拉斯是谁？圣诞节早上，皮科拉在她的鞋子里发现了什么？

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: Fifty Famous People: THE WHISTLE

paragraphs.text: His mother shook her head and said: "No, Benjamin. I cannot give you any more. So you must be careful not to spend these foolishly."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的母亲摇摇头说：“不，本杰明。我不能再给你了。所以你一定要小心，不要愚蠢地花掉这些。”

paragraphs.text: The little fellow ran into the street. He heard the pennies jingle in his pocket. How rich he was!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小家伙跑到街上。他听到口袋里的硬币叮当作响。他多么富有啊！

paragraphs.text: Boston is now a great city, but at that time it was only a little town. There were not many stores.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 波士顿现在是一座伟大的城市，但当时它只是一个小镇。商店不多。

paragraphs.text: As Benjamin ran down the street, he wondered what he should buy. Should he buy candy? He hardly knew how it tasted. Should he buy a pretty toy? If he had been the only child in the family, things might have been different. But there were fourteen boys and girls older than he, and two little sisters who were younger.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当本杰明沿着街道奔跑时，他想知道他应该买什么。他应该买糖果吗？他几乎不知道那味道如何。他应该买一个漂亮的玩具吗？如果他是家里的独生子，情况可能会有所不同。但比他大的男孩女孩有十四个，还有两个比他小的妹妹。

paragraphs.text: What a big family it was! And the father was a poor man. No wonder the lad had never owned a toy.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那是一个多么大的家庭呀！而父亲是个穷人。难怪这个小伙子从来没有拥有过玩具。

paragraphs.text: "I have some pennies," said Benjamin. He held them in his hand, and showed them to the boy. "You may have them, if you will give me the whistle." "All of them?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我有一些硬币，”本杰明说。他把它们拿在手里，拿给男孩看。 “如果你愿意给我哨子，你就可以拥有它们。” “他们全部？”

paragraphs.text: "Well, it's a bargain," said the boy; and he gave the whistle to Benjamin, and took the pennies.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “嗯，这很划算，”男孩说。他把哨子交给本杰明，然后拿走了硬币。

paragraphs.text: Little Benjamin Franklin was very happy; for he was only seven years old. He ran home as fast as he could, blowing the whistle as he ran.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小本杰明·富兰克林非常高兴。因为他只有七岁。他以最快的速度跑回家，边跑边吹口哨。

paragraphs.text: "Well, well!" he said. "You've paid a dear price for this thing. It's only a penny whistle, and a poor one at that."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好啦好啦！”他说。 “你已经为这东西付出了高昂的代价。这只是一个便士哨子，而且是一个很差劲的哨子。”

paragraphs.text: "You might have bought half a dozen such whistles with the money I gave you," said his mother.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “用我给你的钱，你可能可以买六打这样的哨子。”他的母亲说。

paragraphs.text: The little boy saw what a mistake he had made. The whistle did not please him any more. He threw it upon the floor and began to cry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小男孩看到了自己犯了多么大的错误。口哨声不再让他高兴了。他把它扔到地板上，开始哭泣。

paragraphs.text: "Never mind, my child," said his mother, very kindly. "You are only a very little boy, and you will learn a great deal as you grow bigger. The lesson you have learned to-day is never to pay too dear for a whistle." Benjamin Franklin lived to be a very old man, but he never forgot that lesson.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “没关系，我的孩子，”他的母亲非常和蔼地说。 “你只是一个很小的男孩，当你长大后，你会学到很多东西。你今天学到的教训是永远不要为哨子付出太高的代价。”本杰明·富兰克林虽然活得很老，但他从未忘记这一教训。

paragraphs.text: Every boy and girl should remember the name of Benjamin Franklin. He was a great thinker and a great doer, and with Washington he helped to make our country free. His life was such that no man could ever say, "Ben Franklin has wronged me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个男孩和女孩都应该记住本杰明·富兰克林的名字。他是一位伟大的思想家和伟大的实干家，他与华盛顿一起帮助我们的国家实现自由。他的生活就是这样，没有人可以说：“本·富兰克林冤枉了我。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: McGuffey's Sixth Eclectic Reader: LXXIX. THE SONG OF THE POTTER. (290)

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! Turn round and round, Without a pause, without a sound: So spins the flying world away! This clay, well mixed with marl and sand, Follows the motion of my hand; For some must follow, and some command, Though all are made of clay!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！转啊转啊，没有停顿，没有声音，就这样旋转飞天世界吧！这种粘土，与泥灰和沙子充分混合，跟随我手的运动；因为有些人必须遵循，有些人必须命令，尽管一切都是粘土做的！

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! All things must change To something new, to something strange; Nothing that is can pause or stay; The moon will wax, the moon will wane, The mist and cloud will turn to rain, The rain to mist and cloud again, To-morrow be to-day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！一切都必须改变，变得新的、陌生的；没有什么可以暂停或停留；月有盈，月有缺，雾云化雨，雨复雾云，明日即今日。

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! All life is brief; What now is bud will soon be leaf, What now is leaf will soon decay; The wind blows east, the wind blows west; The blue eggs in the robin's nest Will soon have wings and beak and breast, And flutter and fly away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！所有的生命都是短暂的；现在的花蕾很快就会变成叶子，现在的叶子很快就会枯萎；风向东吹，风向西吹；知更鸟巢中的蓝色蛋 很快就会长出翅膀、喙和胸脯， 扑腾着飞走。

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! This earthen jar A touch can make, a touch can mar; And shall it to the Potter say, What makest thou? Thou hast no hand? As men who think to understand A world by their Creator planned, Who wiser is than they.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！这个瓦罐 一触即成，一触即毁；难道它要对陶匠说：你做什么？你没有手吗？正如那些想了解造物主所计划的世界的人一样，谁比他们更聪明。

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! 'Tis nature's plan The child should grow into the man, The man grow wrinkled, old, and gray; In youth the heart exults and sings, The pulses leap, the feet have wings; In age the cricket chirps, and brings The harvest home of day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！这是大自然的计划，孩子应该成长为男人，男人会变得皱纹、衰老、灰白；青春时，心欢喜歌唱，脉搏跳跃，脚有翅膀；到了老年，蟋蟀鸣叫，把白天的收获带回家。

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! The human race, Of every tongue, of every place, Caucasian, Coptic, or Malay, All that inhabit this great earth, Whatever be their rank or worth, Are kindred and allied by birth, And made of the same clay.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！人类，各种语言，各个地方，高加索人，科普特人，或马来人，所有居住在这个伟大地球上的人，无论他们的等级或价值如何，都是血缘相亲和结盟的，都是由同一个粘土组成的。

paragraphs.text: Turn, turn, my wheel! What is begun At daybreak must at dark be done, To-morrow will be another day; To-morrow the hot furnace flame Will search the heart and try the frame, And stamp with honor or with shame These vessels made of clay.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 转动，转动，我的轮子！黎明时开始的事，必须在天黑时完成，明天又是新的一天；明天，炽热的炉火将探寻心灵，考验框架，并以荣誉或耻辱的方式印记这些用粘土制成的器皿。

paragraphs.text: Stop, stop, my wheel! Too soon, too soon The noon will be the afternoon, Too soon to-day be yesterday; Behind us in our path we cast The broken potsherds of the past, And all are ground to dust at last, And trodden into clay. --Longfellow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 停下来，停下来，我的轮子！太快了，太快了，中午就是下午，太快了，今天就是昨天；在我们身后的道路上，我们抛下了过去破碎的陶片，最后一切都被磨成尘土，被踩成泥土。 ——朗费罗。

paragraphs.text: NOTE.--Coptic was formerly the language of Egypt. and is preserved in the inscriptions of the ancient monuments found there; it has now given place entirely to Arabic.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 注：科普特语以前是埃及的语言。并保存在那里发现的古代纪念碑的铭文中；现在它已完全让位于阿拉伯语。

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: For the Children's Hour: 15. NATHAN HALE

paragraphs.text: Nathan Hale, a young man of twenty, had just graduated from Yale College, and was a school-teacher in Connecticut when the war of Independence began. At once he enlisted in the American army, saying, "Let us not lay down our arms till we have gained independence." When General Washington moved his army from Boston to Brooklyn, Nathan Hale, early one morning, when it was darkest, rowed out with a few friends to an English supply ship and sailed it away from the man-of-war that was guarding it and brought it in safety to the American camp. For this brave deed he was promoted to become "Captain" Hale. Soon after this a call was made for a volunteer for a most dangerous service. The British held possession of the lower part of New York City, and were planning a further advance, and Washington greatly needed to know their plans. It was agreed to send a spy to get these. But who would volunteer? "I will undertake it," said Captain Hale. He walked fifty miles up Long Island Sound, along the Connecticut shore and then rowed over to Long Island. Disguised as a traveling school-teacher, he visited all the English camps, making drawings and notes which he hid in his shoes. On his way back he was betrayed by a man who knew him, a traitor to the American cause. He was taken on board the English ship, carried back to New York and led before General Howe. The English general said: "You shall be pardoned and receive money and a fine position in the English army if you will give up the American cause." He said, "I cannot turn against my country!" "Then you can die for her," said the general, and sentenced him to die at daybreak. He listened to his sentence without a word, erect and fearless. At break of day the young spy, brave as a lion, faced his death without a tremor.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 内森·黑尔（Nathan Hale）是一位二十岁的年轻人，刚刚从耶鲁大学毕业，独立战争开始时是康涅狄格州的一名学校教师。他立即加入了美国军队，并说道：“在我们获得独立之前，我们不要放下武器。”当华盛顿将军将他的军队从波士顿转移到布鲁克林时，内森·黑尔在一天清晨，天最黑的时候，与几个朋友划船来到一艘英国补给船上，将其驶离守卫它的战舰，并将其安全地带到美国营地。由于这一勇敢的行为，他被晋升为黑尔“船长”。不久之后，有人呼吁一名志愿者参与最危险的服务。英国人占领了纽约市的下半部分，并计划进一步推进，华盛顿非常需要知道他们的计划。双方同意派一名间谍去获取这些。但谁会自愿呢？ “我会承担这件事，”黑尔船长说。他沿着康涅狄格州海岸，沿着长岛海峡步行五十英里，然后划船到达长岛。他伪装成一名旅行学校老师，走访了所有的英国营地，画了画，做了笔记，藏在鞋子里。在回来的路上，他被一个认识他的人背叛了，他是美国事业的叛徒。他被带上英国船，带回纽约并被带到豪将军面前。这位英国将军说：“如果你放弃美国的事业，你将被赦免，并获得金钱和在英国军队中的良好职位。”他说：“我不能背叛我的国家！”将军说道：“那么你就可以为她去死了。”天一亮就判了他死刑。他听着他的一句话，一言不发，挺身而出，无所畏惧。天一亮，这位年轻的间谍勇敢如狮子，面对死亡，毫无颤抖。

paragraphs.text: In City Hall Park, New York City, not far from the jail in which he was kept that last night, and as near as possible to the spot where he died, is the statue of this noble, young patriot-martyr, who in such heroic unselfishness laid down all he possessed for his country. Upon the statue you will read his last words: "I regret that I have but one life to give for my country."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在纽约市市政厅公园，距离他昨晚被关押的监狱不远，距离他去世的地点尽可能近，矗立着这位高贵、年轻的爱国烈士的雕像，他以如此英勇无私的方式为国家奉献了自己的一切。在雕像上你会读到他的遗言：“我很遗憾我只能为我的国家献出一条生命。”

entries.title: Project Gutenberg: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: HENRY WADSWORTH LONGFELLOW

paragraphs.text: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow (1807-1882) was a native of Maine and a graduate of Bowdoin College, in the same class with Nathaniel Hawthorne. Longfellow came of early New England ancestry, his mother being a daughter of General Wadsworth of the Revolutionary War.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亨利·沃兹沃斯·朗费罗（Henry Wadsworth Longfellow，1807-1882 年）是缅因州人，毕业于鲍登学院，与纳撒尼尔·霍桑同班。朗费罗有早期新英格兰血统，他的母亲是独立战争时期沃兹沃思将军的女儿。

paragraphs.text: After his graduation from college he spent several years abroad and upon his return to America held professorships first in Bowdoin and later in Harvard College. When he moved to Cambridge and began his active work at Harvard, he took up his residence in the historic Craigie House, overlooking the Charles River-a house in which Washington had been quartered for some months when in 1775 he took command of the Continental army.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 大学毕业后，他在国外呆了几年，回到美国后，他首先在鲍登学院担任教授，后来又在哈佛大学担任教授。当他搬到剑桥并开始在哈佛积极工作时，他在历史悠久的克雷吉别墅 (Craigie House) 定居，俯瞰查尔斯河，华盛顿在 1775 年指挥大陆军时曾在这座房子里驻扎了几个月。

paragraphs.text: Longfellow is the poet who has spoken most sincerely and sympathetically to the hearts of the common people and to children. His style is notable for its simplicity and grace. His Hiawatha is a national poem that records the picturesque traditions of the American Indian. Its charm and melody are the delight of all children, and in years to come, when the race which it describes has utterly disappeared, we shall value at even higher worth these stories of the romantic past of America and of the brave people who inhabited these mountains and plains before the white man came.
